Shows [Dashboard] page shortcuts for the control of areas, doors and outputs with the click and tap control. Once a door, area or output is added, they will appear on the [Dashboard] page as they do in the state/control page. The user will be able to directly control the item by simply clicking the item.

Note: The control of these items are based on the user’s permissions. For a user to control an output, it must be configured as a user controllable output.
Select entities to display on the dashboard. Users who are assigned this Web Page Profile will be able to view the state of the selected entities and control them (if applicable).

If enabled, this option will require users to enrol for Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) on their first login before they can access the Inception web interface (if they are not already enrolled). This option only applies if 2FA is enabled on the [Configuration > General > System] page.

Determines a user’s ability to interact with the [Mobile Access Connection Settings] page.

  • None - The [Mobile Access Connection Settings] page will not be accessible.
  • View - The [Mobile Access Connection Settings] page can be viewed, allowing view of the Mobile Access connection settings and status.
  • Modify - The [Mobile Access Connection Settings] page can be viewed, the Mobile Access settings can be changed, and the controller may be linked or unlinked to a Mobile Access customer system.

Determines a user’s ability to interact with the [Mobile Credentials] page.

  • None - The [Mobile Credentials] page will not be accessible, and Mobile Credentials cannot be allocated managed from the User Page.
  • View - The [Mobile Credentials] page can be viewed, allowing view of all mobile credentials on the system.
  • Modify - The [Mobile Credentials] page can be viewed, and the mobile credentials may be allocated, revoked, or deleted via the [Manage Users] page or the [Mobile Credentials] page.

Determines a user’s ability to interact with the [Integrated Connections] page.

  • None - The [Integrated Connections] page will not be accessible, and IR Video Gateway Connections cannot be created or managed.
  • View - The [IR Video Gateway Connections] can be viewed, allowing view of all IR Video Gateway Connections on the system. However, new IR Video Gateway Connections cannot be created, nor existing ones modified or deleted.
  • Modify - The [IR Video Gateway Connections] on Hardware page can be viewed, and IR Video Gateway Connections can be added, modified and deleted.

Determines a user’s ability to interact with the [IR Video Gateway Cameras].

  • None - The [IR Video Gateway Cameras] will not be accessible, and IR Video Gateway Cameras cannot be modified or saved to the Inception system via an IR Video Gateway Connection's Refresh Devices command.
  • View - The [IR Video Gateway Cameras] can be viewed, allowing view of all IR Video Gateway Cameras on the system.
  • Modify - The [IR Video Gateway Cameras] can be viewed, and theIR Video Gateway Cameras may be modified or deleted. Additionally, this allows new IR Video Gateway Cameras to be saved via an IR Video Gateway Connection's Refresh Devices command.

Sets the maximum number of illegal PIN attempts before LCD Terminal will lock for the Lockout Time.

The system will communicate the Max PIN Attempts event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured.
Determines the duration of time that the keypad will be unresponsive if a user exceeds the Max Login Attempts.
If enabled, the keypad beeper will sound an audible tone with each key press.

If enabled, ensures that the LCD Terminal backlight is always enabled, even when logged off. This may be useful if the terminal is mounted in a dark area to have attention drawn to it or to be able to see the logged off display text.

By default, the backlight on the terminal will be off when logged off, but will turn on when interacted with or displaying a message about the associated area or door state. The backlight can also be turned on briefly by pressing a button that does not have a function when logged off, such as the ON, OFF, MENU or an Arrow key.

Determines what is displayed on the LCD Terminal when logged off.

  • Basic Display - Displays the message System Ready. Often used so that no system information is visible from the logged off terminal
  • Date and Time - The logged off screen will display the current system date and time.
  • Custom Text - The logged off screen will display the messages entered in the Custom Text Line 1 and Custom Text Line 2 fields below. Each line can display a maximum of 16 characters.
  • Associated Area State - Displays the state (Armed or Disarmed) of the Associated Area to the LCD Terminal. (configured on the next step)

The text that will be displayed on the first line of the LCD Terminal if the Logged Off Display option above is set to Custom Text.

Each line can display a maximum of 16 characters.

The text that will display on the second line of the LCD Terminal if the Logged Off Display option above is set to Custom Text.

Each line can display a maximum of 16 characters.

Determines whether or not area alarm messages are displayed on the LCD Terminal when logged off. Will display the last alarm message that occurred in an area, including the input that triggered the alarm. Once someone has seen the message the user can press the [END] key or log on to the terminal to clear the message. This will also clear the message from all other terminals.

  • None - Area alarm messages are not displayed on the Logged Off screen, this is useful in situations where alarms must be discreetly generated or would rather be ignored in certain locations.
  • All Areas - Alarm messages from all areas are displayed on this LCD Terminal's logged off screen.

Note, if an area is associated to the terminal via the Associated Area option, the Area Alarm Active feedback of the terminal will override the alarm messages from that area

If enabled, outputs can be controlled by a user from the logged off screen. Outputs must be configured as User Controllable and have the Logged Off Control option enabled, see the [Configuration > Outputs > Set As User Controllable] page. Press the [^] button (up arrow) on the LCD Terminal to access output control.

If enabled, a duress event will be sent to the monitoring station when a user presses the HELP button on the LCD Terminal three times in a row.

Note, careful consideration should be given when enabling this option if this terminal is located in a public place, as a silent alarm could be easily activated by someone playing with the terminal who is unfamiliar with the system

This number is the unique module address and is used to distinguish between modules when more than one type of module is connected to Inception.

When adding a new module to inception the module is addressed based on the provided instruction manual. If a module is connected to the Inception Controller LAN it will be auto discovered and will indicate the module number, the module number field will not be editable.

If a module is added to the Inception Controller by clicking the Add New button on the hardware configuration page you are able to edit and select a module number, ensure that the physical module is addressed the same module number when connecting it later.

The maximum duration of time that the module can remain out of communication with the controller. If the module does not communicate within the poll time, it is considered to be offline and will create a hardware event.

The system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured.

If enabled, this Integrated Connection will fail to validate the remote certificate of the integration if is not trusted, such as in scenarios with a self-signed certificate or improper certificate installation.

If enabled, this Integrated Connection will fail to validate the remote certificate of the integration if the certificate is invalid in any way, such as when the certificate is misconfigured, the domain is mismatched, or there is a break in the certificate's chain of trust.

This value is the currently trusted certificate fingerprint for this Integrated Connection. If this value matches the one found while attempting to validate the certificate of the Integrated Connection, it will be successfully validated given that there are no other errors and it has not been blocked due to the Integrated Connection's configuration.

The first successful connection to the integration will automatically trust the certificate fingerprint of that connection. After that, this value must be changed manually or matched to the last attempted connection's fingerprint using the link icon to the left.

If enabled, the RF inputs configured for this module will be monitored to ensure they regularly poll in. If an input fails to poll in within the specified time (configured with the RF Poll Supervision Time option below), an RF Poll event will be sent to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled. Notifications to users can also be sent in these cases.

Note that inputs that fail to poll in will not causes area alarms or sirens to occur, however they will cause area arm attempts to fail if an input seal check is performed.

If RF poll supervision is enabled, this option determines the amount of time that is allowed to pass before an input is considered to have failed to poll in.

The amount of time to select should depend on the poll frequency of your devices. It is recommended that the selected time is at least two or three times longer than the longest device poll time. As polls can occasionally be missed, this should help to prevent unnecessary poll fail messages from being reported due to a single missed poll.

Enable this to require the PIN number to be entered immediately on waking the terminal.

Otherwise all users are required to enter a PIN at least once per active Touchscreen session on interacting with Areas, Input, Outputs or Doors. If disarming an area in Exit delay the user will be required to enter the PIN.

This option can be used to prevent unauthorized users interacting with the touchscreen. This feature is generally enabled where the touchscreen is accessible to the public and ensures any touchscreen operation and system status/s remain secure. For private dwellings such as residential homes, this feature is generally not enabled.

PINs can be created and updated on the Manage Users on the Inception webpage and Users will require related Permissions and LCD Terminal Profile to properly interact with the Touchscreen device.

Enable this option to require that a PIN number must be provided at some point to access the Emergency request options.

Note that this option is only relevant if the above PIN Login Required option is disabled, to support a scenario where a User can interact with the Touchscreen without providing a PIN to wake it up, but access to the emergency shortcuts still requires a PIN code. This ability may be desired in home environments where interacting with the touchscreen without a PIN lets the user see the system status easily, but they still want to prevent someone from accidentally requesting emergency services.

Sets the maximum number of illegal PIN attempts before the touchscreen will lock for the Lockout Time.

The system will communicate the Max PIN Attempts event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured.

If enabled, the touchscreen will monitor the 12V input for Low Voltage and will be included in the overall system health monitoring.

Note: Only enable this option if this module is connected to a 12V Power Supply and not a dedicated PoE connection.

The Inception Controller will report this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured, should any alarm conditions occur with this power supply.
Enables the periodic sync of the system time using NTP (network time protocol)

Enter the NTP server(s) to synchronise the system time with. If using more than one NTP server, use spaces to separate multiple servers.

If an internet connection is not available for the Inception controller, or the specified NTP servers can not be reached, specify a local NTP server. This ensures the Inception system time is accurate, events have the correct time stamp, and any scheduled tasks or permissions occur at the correct time.

Sets the system's timezone that the controller is located in. The time zone is used as the offset for GMT that is kept in sync by the NTP server.

In addition, Daylight Savings adjustments are automatically handled by the timezone configuration.

Note: changes to the time zone may take up to a minute to take effect.
Whether or not the connection to Mobile Access system is enabled. When disabled, warnings related to mobile access will not be generated, and new mobile credentials are unable to be allocated.

The URL to the Mobile Access server. This shouldn't ever need to change, and is only exposed on Beta variants.

The default URL for the Mobile Access server is: https://mobileaccess.innerrange.cloud/

Informational Only: The site code that all mobile credentials generated by this system will use. A corresponding card template is created using this site code, and used by the mobile credentials.

This is set during initial configuration of the Mobile Access connection, and cannot be changed afterwards without unlinking and relinking the Mobile Access connection.
Informational Only: The name of the Mobile Access system that has been linked to the Inception system. This can only be changed via the Mobile Access Portal.

This disables Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) which is a server protocol that automatically provides the controller with its IP address. This option should be selected if there is no DHCP server on the connected network or the Inception controller requires a specific IP address.

Selecting the use static IP option will make the IP address, subnet mask, default gateway and DNS server options below editable.

An IP address is a unique string of numbers separated by full stops that identifies each device using the internet protocol to communicate over a network.

With Use Static IP disabled: an informational only field that displays the automatically assigned IP address.

With Use Static IP enabled: this field is used to manually enter the static IP address.

The subnet mask is a binary pattern that is stored in the controller. It is matched with the IP address of a packet to determine which network segment the packet is destined for.

With Use Static IP disabled: an informational only field that displays the subnet mask.

With Use Static IP enabled: this field is used to manually enter the subnet mask.

The default gateway is the router used to forward all traffic that is not addressed to a device within the local network or local subnet.

With Use Static IP disabled: an informational only field that displays the default gateway.

With Use Static IP enabled: this field is used to manually enter the default gateway.

DNS server is short for domain name system, this is an internet service that translates domain names into IP addresses.

With Use Static IP disabled: an informational only field that displays the DNS server.

With Use Static IP enabled: this field is used to manually enter the DNS server.
Informational only: the physical address of the controllers network interface. Commonly required by on site it to allocate an IP to the device.

Enables the use of Wi-Fi as a method to connect to the Inception web interface via the controller. This option will only work with the Inception Wireless Adapter.

If you aren't connecting Inception to a local WiFi network, it is recommended to still leave this option enabled and set to Wireless Hotspot. Then, during maintenance, a wireless adapter can be connected temporarily to allow an to easy connection to the controller without touching the on-site network.
Displays the IP address assigned to the controller when connected to a Wi-Fi network. Wi-Fi allows DHCP only, so to fix the IP address of the controller the MAC address shown below should be used.
Displays the subnet mask assigned to the controller when connected to a Wi-Fi network.
Displays the default gateway assigned to the controller when connected to a Wi-Fi network.
DNS server is short for domain name system, this is an internet service that translates domain names into IP addresses.
Informational only: the physical address of the WiFi Adapter. Commonly required by the on site IT department to allocate an IP to the device.

Enables remote access to the web interface over the internet via Skytunnel. Skytunnel is a hassle free connection method that allows any device with internet access connect to an Inception Controller.

To connect to your Inception Controller, open a web browser and navigate to [https://skytunnel.com.au/Inception/SERIALNUMBER/] , where SERIALNUMBER is your controller’s serial number.

Note: Since the web interface will be accessible over the internet it is recommended that you use secure usernames and passwords.
Sets the Domain Name or IP Address of the SkyTunnel server to connect to.
Sets the Port used to communicate with the SkyTunnel server.
Sets the Domain Name or IP Address of the KeyPoint server to connect to.

By default, Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is disabled on Inception devices. If the Inception REST API is accessed via cross-origin requests, the origin address to allow through can be provided here.
When enabled, Inception will redirect all standard HTTP requests to HTTPS for increased security of web traffic.

This selects the mode of operation for the Inception Wi-Fi Adapter.

  • Wireless Hotspot - Allows a pc or mobile device to connect via Wi-Fi directly to the Inception controller without connecting to the on-site network. Note: This is intended for easy connection during installation and maintenance of the system and is not recommended as a permanent configuration option.
  • Connect to WPA2 Personal Network - Allows the Inception controller to connect to a wireless network using WPA-personal authentication. Once connected, a pc or mobile device on the same network can be used to configure the Inception controller.
  • Connect to WPA2 Enterprise Network - Allows the Inception controller to connect to a wireless network using WPA-enterprise authentication. Once connected, a pc or mobile device on the same network can be used to configure the Inception controller.

The SSID (service set identifier) is the name assigned to a Wi-Fi network. This is used to configure the Inception controller's wireless hotspot name, this name will be broadcast to all wireless devices.
This defines the password that is used when a device attempts to connect to the wireless hotspot. This password must be atleast 8 characters long and can be a combination of letters and numbers.

The name of the wireless network the Inception controller should attempt to connect to. The name can be manually entered, or optionally the Detect SSID button can be used to detect nearby Wi-Fi networks and select one from a list.

Note: the Detect SSID feature only works when the Enable Wi-Fi option is enabled and the connection mode is not Hotspot. Ensure these settings are correct and saved to the system before attempting to detect networks.
The shared password used to authenticate the connection to the wireless network.
The username to use when connecting to the wireless network.
The user-based password to authenticate the connection to the wireless network.

Allows the name of the system to be configured. This name will be shown on the login page and in the footer of every page. This can be helpful in identifying the system you are working with if interacting with multiple Inception interfaces at once.
Sets the region of the Inception system, enabling region specific functionality (such as Europe specific GSM devices and reporting settings).

Sets the end of line resistance scheme that is used by the system to determine the state of detector inputs. The default setting is 2k2 + 2k2 or 2k2 + 6k8 which supports the resistors supplied with the Inception controller and the Inner Range expansion modules. If you are using a different End of Line resistor configuration, select the one that you are using for the site.

The End of Line configuration such as 2k2 + 6k8 determines the resistance values for the seal and alarm states. The first number 2k2 is the resistance that should represent a sealed input. The sum of both numbers 2k2 + 6k8 = 9k is the resistance for an input in alarm.

If the resistance scheme required is not available, Inner Range may be able to supply an upgrade file for a fee to allow your Inception system to support the custom scheme. Pricing and availability is subject to Inner Range discretion

Determines the duration of time to delay reporting of module AC Fail events to monitoring stations, or to users via notifications. If the AC supply is restored before this delay expires then no message will be sent.

Backup batteries should be fitted to the system to ensure the system can run without an AC supply for an extended period of time. AC supply to the system could be removed for a short period of time, for example, due to a brief power outage or brownout to the site or building maintenance that requires power to be removed for a short period of time. In these cases, power is restored quickly with little drain on the batteries, so the delay prevents the unnecessary notification to the monitoring station of the event. This in turn removes the need for the monitoring station to action the event and follow their response plan of informing the client for the short outage.

If set to None, no delay will be used and an AC Fail event will be sent as soon as one is detected.

If enabled, modules and alarm reporting paths will also be tested for any issues when an area arm attempt is made. If a user requested the arm, the attempt would fail if any issues are present and the user would be shown the reason for the failure and given the option to temporarily isolate them and try again. Alternatively, if the system requested the arm, the issues will be temporarily isolated (in the same way as inputs are temporarily isolated) and the arm will succeed. Any health issues isolated by the user or the system in this way will be automatically deisolated when the area disarms.

This ensures that users are made aware of health issues that could compromise the security of the system when securing the premises, allowing them to either fix the issue or make the decision to temporarily override the issue.

The system only monitors module health items that have been configured via the [Configuration > Hardware] page. If a health issue will persist for several days, the module can be permanently isolated via the [State / Control > View Hardware State] page. Similar to permanent isolation of inputs, this isolation will persist until a user manually removes it. Alternatively, if the issue will persist for longer or the system should not be monitoring the module for that issue, the module should be edited via the [Configuration > Hardware] page to remove system monitoring.

If a module has its health issues isolated, the system will ignore health issues raised by that module until it is deisolated. Any isolations of health issues are also reported to the monitoring station, ensuring that any potential compromise to the security of the system is known.

If testing of system health is desired on some areas but not others, each area can be configured to ignore health issues via the Ignore Module Health Issues option on the [Configuration > Areas > Edit Areas] page.

If enabled, area arm requests from users located off-site will include an Arm Warning phase prior to arming. This allows some time for someone to leave or cancel the arm if they are still on site. With this option configured, a remote Arm with Exit Delay request will ignore the Exit Delay part and only enter the Arm Warning phase, arming the area when the phase completes.

A user arm request is considered remote or off-site if it comes from the web interface (i.e the [State / Control > Control Areas] page), the IR Connect App, or any REST API integrations that indicate the request is remote.

Arm requests from an LCD terminal, an RF fob, a Reader action, or API integration with a system like a home automation system are all considered on-site.

Determines the duration of time after a module goes offline before the Inception controller updates the state of the modules inputs and peripheral devices.

Rarely, a LAN module may drop offline and re-register itself, coming back online often within one second. This could be caused by LAN signal interference or other factors. In the event that this occurs, this module fail time allows a buffer where the module can restore itself. If the time expires without the module coming back online, the module will be treated as failed and will have the state of its inputs updated and any peripheral devices attached to it set to offline.

It is recommended that you choose a value equal to or greater than your longest module poll time (1 minute by default), as this minimises false detections where the module thinks it is still online.

Input states are updated to either an Unknown or Tamper state based on the Set Inputs to Tamper on Module Fail option below.

If enabled, the detector inputs for the module will be set to the Tamper state if the module is offline longer than the Module Failed Time option above. Otherwise, the detector inputs will be set to an Unknown state.

The above failure time is designed to filter out brief LAN comms issues, but if the module remains offline for longer than the configured time then this option determines how the system responds. In this case the detector inputs on the module can not be known to the controller. This could be due to power loss or a lan cabling issue, or it could be due to an intruder intentionally interfering with the LAN.

The Tamper state of the inputs may cause area alarms depending on the input processing for those inputs, which will sound sirens and generate an alarm event that will be sent to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is configured.

Alternatively, the Unknown state is a secure state that won't generate an alarm or prevent area arming. A module offline event will still be sent to the monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled, as well as messages indicating the presence of an offline module will be displayed in the Inception web interface and on LCD terminals.

Note: Enabling this option creates a more secure system, where inputs on modules that have gone offline are treated as if they have been tampered with and are compromised. This could however cause undesirable behaviour (siren responses and alarms being sent to the monitoring station) if sections of the LAN are unreliable

If enabled, an input or module will only be able to trigger the sirens once for an area per arm cycle. Only the retriggering of sirens will be prevented, subsequent activations will still cause alarm events to be reported to a monitoring station if configured.

This option is used for a number of scenarios. If an input is faulty and repeatedly activates, or if it is externally accessible and an intruder can easily activate it, then repeated siren activations could annoy neighbours, cause people to eventually ignore the siren or potentially use up the siren activation limit of the area. With this option enabled however, subsequent activations of an input or module health issue would still be reported to the monitoring station, but the sirens won’t activate. Then, if a second input activates, the sirens will be able to sound and attract attention to the potential break in.

This functionality is also a requirement of AS2201.1 and should be enabled if meeting this standard is desired.

Allows the internal system functionality to be configured with a certain compliance standard in mind. Depending on the option chosen, internal processing logic may change or additional features may become available which are required by the selected standard.

By hiding away functionality that is specific to the compliance modes, it allows the system to remain simpler and easier to use when those standards are not required.

  • None - No additional specific compliance features will be enabled. This is the default option and should be selected for almost all installations, unless compliance with one of the listed standards is required at the site.
  • EN50131 - Adds additional processing logic to support the EN50131 standard used in many locations throughout Europe. This adds many things to the system, most of which are internal to the system, while some additional options and features are shown. For example, changes to area alarm processing logic are included, additional Review Events and rules to how they are saved, rules applying to Level 2 (standard users) and Level 3 (installer technicians) are applied and many others. These features, along with additional options and validation, modify the Inception from the original system used in countries around the world to allow compliance with standards required by a number of European countries.
  • EN50131 and BS8243 - BS8243 is in general an extension of the EN50131 standard. With this option, in addition to the features and processing logic added to support the EN50131 standard, the extra features and logic required by the BS8243 standard are enabled. On top of the existing EN50131 enhancements, the BS8243 standard primarily adds Confirmation logic to area alarm processing to differentiate between unconfirmed and confirmed intruder and hold up alarm events. Anti-code functionality is also included, allowing the ARC / monitoring station / alarm company to provide a level 2 user with a code to temporarily let them perform installer level actions.

Determines the amount of time that can pass after an unconfirmed intruder alarm occurs before the system is reinstated. To be compliant with the BS8243 standard, a time between 30 minutes and 1 hour must be selected. However, shorter times are available to aid with testing.

Determines the amount of time that can pass after an unconfirmed hold up/duress alarm occurs before the system is reinstated. To be compliant with the BS8243 standard, a time between 8 hours and 20 hours must be selected. However, shorter times are available to aid with testing.

Allows areas to have their confirmation logic grouped together, letting unconfirmed alarms from one area confirm alarms in other areas of the group. This affects both Intruder confirmation and Hold Up confirmation logic.

Inception allows multiple security areas to be defined, letting individual areas be armed and disarmed to provide more flexibility on site design and use. Certain sections of the site can be secured, such as server rooms and other critical areas, while others are freely accessible.

When the BS8243 standard is selected, each area will automatically enable alarm confirmation logic, allowing alarms and other triggers to generate unconfirmed alarms and confirmed alarms in those areas. However, each area is isolated, with alarms in one area not affecting the other areas which may be desirable in some circumstances and not desirable in others when it comes to confirmation logic.

By grouping areas together, it allows an unconfirmed alarm in one area to trigger, then if another unconfirmed alarm occurs in another area within the same group, both areas will confirm their alarms. This allows for a multi-floor building to be able to have individual areas for each floor that can be armed separately, but if an intruder triggers one detector on the first floor and another on the second floor, confirmed alarm events would be activated, instead of multiple unconfirmed alarms. The alternative would be to have one large area covering the whole building, with the only option to have the building either armed or not.

Disarming an area to reinstate it (which restores any unconfirmed or confirmed alarms and stops any confirmation timers), will remove those alarms from the group. For areas that confirmed their unconfirmed alarm based on this other area's alarms, if there are no other alarms in the group, then those areas may restore their confirmed alarm, reverting back to just an unconfirmed alarm and waiting for a second activation.

Multiple groups can be created by selecting which areas should be grouped with the Add Items button, then selecting which confirm group they should belong to.
Sets the minimum number of digits that can be used for user PIN numbers. This number is also used when PIN numbers are automatically generated in the [Configuration Users > Manage Users] page.

Sets the maximum number of digits that can be used for user PIN numbers. This number is also used when PIN numbers are automatically generated in the [Configuration > Users > Manage Users] page.

Note, if increasing PIN length limits, LCD Terminals can accept at most 8 digit PINs and the Auto-Generate PIN is limited to at most 10 digits.
Sets the minimum number of characters required when creating or changing user web profile passwords.

Specifies the minimum requirements for a user's created or changed password. By default, there are no minimum requirements. Selecting numeric characters as a requirement means users will need to put a number in their password. Alphabetic characters as a requirement means any letter. Symbols means users require a non-number non-alphabetical character in their password.

Sets the number of attempts any particular user may make before being locked out of login for a duration specified by the Lockout Duration setting, default 6 attempts. If the user is unknown or erroneous (their username is not found) and does not enter their username correctly after 3 attempts, instead a default lockout will be used for a minute and a half independent of settings.

Setting this value to 0 will disable lockouts, meaning that any individual user may attempt as many times to login, but the default lockout will still be present for any unknown or erroneous usernames.

Sets the duration of a lockout when a user exceeds the number of attempts specified by the Login Attempts Before Account Lock setting. A lockout time of None will set all lockouts to last indefinitely until intervention by another user with the ability to control users - to disable lockout, the Login Attempts Before Account Lock setting must be set to zero.

Entering an unrecognised username in 3 different attempts is distinct and will use the default lockout time.

Specifies the amount of time between mandatory password changes. When a user has not changed their password in the specified timespan, they will not be able to access other pages until they change their password. By default, the user is never required to change their password unless it does not meet the specifications in the Minimum Password Requirements setting.

This option determines whether the system's local IP address is shown on the login page.

While it is useful for installers and system administrators to easily access this IP address from the login page, potential malicious third parties could use this knowledge to target other internal systems on the network.
Disallows users from using a PIN to login, removing the 'Switch to PIN Entry' option on the login page and preventing PINs from being used to authenticate with API requests.

If enabled, allows configuration of Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) for users, providing an added level of security when they log into the Inception web interface by requiring them to enter an external code generated by an app running on a smartphone or similar device. The Two-Factor Authentication Mode option below determines which 2FA system will be used for authenticating user login. Users can be enrolled for 2FA on the [Manage Users] page.

The Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) system that the Inception web interface will utilise for user login. Users can be enrolled for 2FA on a per-user basis from the Manage Users page. For added security, 2FA can also be made compulsory for specific Web Page Profiles, requiring new logged-in users to enrol for 2FA before they are allowed to access the rest of the web interface.

  • Google Authenticator/TOTP - Configures the controller to request 6-digit Time-based One-time Password (TOTP) codes from users in addition to their username and password. These login codes can be generated by the Google Authenticator mobile app or another TOTP-compatible 2FA app. TOTP codes allow users to log in using 2FA even while the controller or user device cannot connect to the internet.

NOTE: Changing the 2FA method will delete all existing 2FA enrolment data for users and require them to be re-enrolled.

If enabled, the system will have the duress user functionality. This enables a user that is under duress to type in the duress PIN number instead of their own. If the duress user has permission (configured below) they will be granted access as usual, however the system will generate a silent duress alarm event that will be reported to the monitoring station. This event will not generate area sirens or LCD messages.

Sets the PIN number that is used to trigger a duress event from an LCD terminal or keypad reader. This PIN number uses the minimum and maximum PIN lengths values specified on this page in the Access Configuration section and must be unique compared to all other users in the system.

Sets the permissions provided to a user when triggering a duress event from an LCD Terminal or keypad reader. It is recommended that some permissions are assigned to make it appear that an actual user has logged in, however, it is not recommended that high risk doors and areas are assigned as it would be a PIN number known to many users.

Sets which terminal profile will be applied if the duress user PIN is used to log in to an LCD Terminal. It is recommended that a profile is selected that allows the user access to menus that they would need when under duress and to appear that an actual user logged in. However, the profile shouldn't let them access items like system information, network settings or installer menus as it would be a PIN number known to many users.

Determines how often the Battery Test will run. The frequency is based on a weekly scale range, you can select which day of the week and the what time of the day below.

It is recommended to run the Battery Test regularly to ensure that the in the event of AC failure, each module can be powered from its attached battery.

Once the battery testing is triggered, all modules with a power supply connected, battery connected and battery testing enabled will be added to a queue for testing. Modules are tested one at a time so that if there is an AC fail mid test, only part of the system is vulnerable, not all of it.

For a module to be included in the battery test it will need to have a power supply connected, battery connected and battery testing enabled on the [Configuration > Hardware] page. The duration of the battery test is set on a per module basis, on the [Configuration > Hardware] page. The length of the battery test will depend on the average current consumption of the module and the capacity of the connected battery.

If a battery issue is detected during a battery test, the monitoring station will be notified about it and a warning will appear on the web interface. A module with a failed battery test state must pass a battery test to clear it. This can happen with the next scheduled test, or a test can be manually run on the [State/Control > Hardware Test] page.

Battery tests can abort for a number of reasons, a user can cancel the test, AC was lost on the power supply, a wire was shorted, comms to the module was lost or the module needed to sound sirens. An aborted test is neither a pass or fail and nothing is reported to the monitoring station.
Determines what day of the week the Battery Test will run.
Determines what hour and minute of the day the Battery Test will run.

By default, SIFER readers will be able to read SIFER credentials and ECOLITE credentials. The Multi-Format version of the SIFER reader will also be capable of reading the CSN data of various third party 13.56Mhz credentials.

With this option enabled, the readers will be limited to reading SIFER credentials only. There may be a slight performance improvement with this option as the reader only needs to read one format instead of testing multiple formats. It also offers slightly higher reader security as only the high security SIFER credentials are accepted.

Inception requires a LAN Encryption Key in order to communicate with Ethernet LAN modules on the network. You can either use the default encryption key, or specify a custom passphrase or key to use. All Ethernet LAN modules in your system must be configured with the same encryption key as the controller in order for them to be able to communicate.

If the LAN Encryption Key is changed while Ethernet LAN modules are connected to Inception, Inception will attempt to migrate modules from the old key to the new one. If some modules are failing to connect after changing the LAN encryption key, use the LAN Encryption Key Changeover toolbox button to manually migrate modules from their old key to the new one.

  • Default - The default LAN encryption key will be used.
  • Passphrase - Allows a passphrase to specified for the LAN encryption key.
  • Key - Allows a 32 character hexadecimal key (0-9, A-Z) to be specified for the LAN encryption key.
The passphrase that will be used to derive the LAN Encryption Key for communicating with Ethernet LAN modules.

The hexadecimal key that will be used as the LAN Encryption Key for communicating with Ethernet LAN modules. The key must be exactly 32 characters long and can only contain the characters 0-9, A-Z and a-z.

By default, any Intelligent LAN Access Module that is connected will have all entities that can be stored for offline functionality synchronised upon initial connection and any relevant entity changes.

Not all details can be synchronised with full compatibility to the ILAM offline database (such as non-Time Period permissions), so a warning is generated whenever something synchronised does not fully match the Inception system.

With this option enabled, all warnings generated related to ILAM offline database incompatibilities with anything in the Inception system will be suppressed, and will no longer appear.

Inception requires an email server to be configured in order to send email notifications. Currently, an SMTP server is the only option for sending emails, however in future there will be other server options.

Once a server is configured, test emails can be sent using the Send Test Email option in the toolbox at the top of this page.

  • None - No email server is configured for the system. Inception will not be able to send email messages.
  • SMTP Server - Allows an SMTP server to be configured for the system to use. This will require settings like the host address and port number to be configured, along with a username and password if required.

A custom logo image can be uploaded to the Inception system for branding purposes, for example: if an installer wishes to display a company logo in place of the Inception logo on the login screen and on the navigation bar. The custom logo also replaces the Inception logo in PDF reports generated from the [System > Reports] page.

An image can be uploaded by dragging and dropping the image file directly onto the image upload area (if using a PC), or by clicking the upload area and selecting the image that you want to upload.

If the image field is left blank, the default Inception logo will appear on all web pages as normal.

The image's dimensions and aspect ratio should be equivalent to the default Inception logo image (500 x 100px, or 5:1) for optimal quality.

This field sets a custom text message that will be shown to users on the login screen when they log into Inception via the web interface. An installer can use this field to display information such as important phone numbers, email addresses, Installed by Company X, etc.

Enables the Dynamic User Import Module (DUIM) service for this Inception controller.

The DUIM service periodically scans a network share folder and imports CSV files containing user data in the same format as the one specified by the Import CSV dialog on the [Configuration > Users > Manage Users] page.

Successfully imported files are deleted from the network share folder after the user data is imported. Files that fail to import are moved into a user-specified subfolder, or deleted if no subfolder is given. If the DUIM service fails to import a file, a system message will be created with the details of the error to help identify why the import failed, so the file can be amended and reimported if required.

The full network share folder path that the DUIM service will monitor for new user data CSV files, e.g. \\\\ExampleServer\\InceptionDuim. NOTE: The network share location must already exist and allow full read/write access, otherwise the DUIM service may not function correctly.
Specifies the username to use for network/domain authentication. If on a domain network, the domain may also need to be specified before a / character, e.g SampleDomain/user1.
Specifies the password to use for network/domain authentication.

Optionally specifies the subfolder where files that fail to import should be moved to. If no subfolder is specified, the files will be deleted instead. The folder must exist and be writable in order for the DUIM service to function correctly.

NOTE: This field specifies a subfolder of the Network Share Location, not the absolute path. For example, if the Network Share Location is \\\\ExampleServer\\InceptionDuim and the subfolder is failures, failed imports will be moved to \\\\ExampleServer\\InceptionDuim\\failures.
Determines how often the DUIM service should check for new files to import from the network share path.

Optionally determines which files the DUIM service should process or ignore based on whether they match the mask.

The wildcard character (*) can be used to match patterns in filenames, e.g. example_*.csv will only match .csv files starting with example_.

If no filename mask is specified, the default mask *.csv will be used.
The Credential Template that will be used when importing any users from CSV files. A template must be selected if you are importing users with Credential data.
The Fob Template that will be used when importing any users from CSV files. A template must be selected if you are importing users with Fob credential data.

Whenever using DUIM or the Import CSV feature on the [Configuration > Users > Manage Users] page, columns that do not correspond to a user property and are not mapped in the Custom Column Mapping in the DUIM settings will normally prevent the import of that .csv file.

Enabling this option will allow for .csv files to be imported regardless of whether unmapped columns are found or not, and no error or system message will occur.

Specifies the user properties that will have a mapped alternative column header name for imported user data CSV files, whether imported automatically through DUIM or manually through user configuration at [Configuration > Users > Import CSV].

When a user property is selected, a custom column name can be entered which will be treated as the original user property when imported. For example, the 'Name' user property could be mapped to the alternate column name 'Full Name' so that any data in the CSV file under 'Full Name' will be imported into the user's 'Name' property.
Select the user properties that will have an alternate column name under CSV imports.

When a user property is selected, a custom column name can be entered which will be treated as the original user property. During the import process, user property data under that alternate column name will be imported as if it was for the original user property.

To prevent too many emails from being sent and potentially filling an inbox or having the configured SMTP server marked as a spam server, Inception will attempt to group messages together if they occur close together. Messages will be grouped only if they are being sent to the same recipient address and have the same subject. Any messages that are grouped will include the date and time they occurred within the message.

When an email is to be sent, Inception will wait 15 seconds in case any other events occur for the same recipient address and subject and will group them into a single email message. After sending, if any new messages occur for that same recipient address and with the same subject, Inception will wait for the configured Rate Limit Time before sending those messages.

As messaging is grouped on both recipient address and subject, a longer rate limit time can be selected to allow common messages to be sent as only a few emails, while higher priority messages can be sent to a different address or with a different subject.

Rate limiting can be disabled by setting the value to None, however if too many emails are sent too quickly then external systems may treat the configured SMTP server as suspicious and mark future emails as spam.

The email address that is used when sending email notifications from this system. However, depending on the SMTP server used, this address may be overridden by the server account.

The address must be in a valid format such as jsmith@example.com
The name that will appear as who the email came from when this system sends an email message.

The address of the mail server you wish to use for sending emails. This would be the outgoing address of the mail server, such as smtp.gmail.com if using Gmail.

You may need to contact your service provider to learn the server address.

The port used to connect to the mail server. This is often port 465 or 587 if SSL/TLS is required, or port 25 otherwise.

You may need to contact your service provider to learn the server port.
If enabled, Inception will connect to the mail server with an encrypted connection. This should be enabled if the mail server you are connecting to requires or supports a secure connection
If enabled, Inception will use the username and password specified in the Username and Password fields below to authenticate with the mail server.
The username that Inception will use to authenticate with the mail server if authentication is required. This may be in the form of an email address such as jsmith@example.com
The password that Inception will use to authenticate with the mail server if authentication is required.
Please refer to your Azure App Registration
The Email user name of the service account. This can be different from your From address

Modules with failed battery tests

This could indicate that the batteries backuping up these modules are no longer capable of powering them when the main power supply is lost, either because the capacity has fallen over time or because the amount of power needed has increased. It could also be because the main supply was lost for a time prior to the test, causing the batteries to be tested when not fully charged.

Check the batteries to verify their capacity is still suitable for the modules they must support and replace if necessary. To clear the failed test state the test must pass. Battery tests can be run manually from the [Hardware Test] page.

Inputs in permanent isolation

Permanently isolated inputs will not report alarms or tampers and can only be de-isolated by a user. Often used for inputs that are faulty or having maintenance done to them, these inputs should be repaired and de-isolated when possible.
Modules in permanent isolation

Permanently isolated modules will have any health issues ignored by the system and can only be de-isolated by a user. Used primarily when issues have been identified with the module that will take some time to fix. If an issue is expected to last for an extended period of time, it may be better to edit the module on the [Configuration > Hardware] page and stop the system from monitoring that item. The issues with the module should be fixed and the module de-isolated when possible.

Inputs being Soak Tested
Inputs that failed Soak Test
Inputs in Soak Test are isolated from the system and will not report any alarms.

To pass a Soak Test, an input must not generate a reportable alarm for the duration of the test. If no alarm is generated, the input will automatically return to normal operation at the end of the test. Otherwise, if the test fails, the input will remain in the failed soak test state until someone de-isolates the input.
To stop a Soak Test early, or to remove the failed soak test state from an input, the input must be de-isolated via the [Control Inputs] page.
Control Inputs

This page is used to view live and historical events that are created by the Inception Controller. These events can be displayed, filtered and exported.

Review Events are a live and historical log of every event that has occurred on the Inception System. Review events are created every time a scheduled event occurs, a user interacts with a system or even when a programming change is made.

When this page is opened the last 24 hours of events are shown (up to a maximum of 500), new events are dynamically added to the top of the list. If you want to load more events clicking the Load More button will load an additional 500 events. Clicking the Load All button will load every event stored on the Inception controller. This is not recommended as displaying this number of events may have an impact on the pages performance.

The displayed events can be filtered to make it easier to find particular events. The To and From fields are used to filter events to certain dates and times, which is useful if you know what time the event you are looking for occurred. The Search field will filter events based on the text you enter. The Message Category filter is used to show or hide events that belong to a particular category, for example, if you were looking for door access events it would be recommended to only show the Access category.

The displayed events can be exported as a PDF or CSV file to be printed or used in a different application.

If an incident occurred 1 week ago and you wanted to review the events, click the Load More button until the events from a week ago are displayed. If there are too many events displayed to find the incident, reduce the number by using one or more filters.
This page is used to configure the users that interact with the Inception Controller and the Inception web interface. This includes their credentials and user permissions.

A user is any person that interacts with the Inception System by either badging a credential, entering a pin number or logging into the web interface. Every user will have one or more credentials and some permissions that determine how and what they can interact with in the system. This page is used to create or manage users, assign credentials and RF Fobs and assign permissions for the Inception Controller and web interface.

Create or select an existing user and assign the credentials required for the user to interact with the system. Credentials are what identify a user and must be unique between all users. Credentials include the PIN number used to request at access keypad readers or log in to the LCD terminal, cards or biometric data for access control and RF remotes for remote control. Also included are the credentials required to log on to the web interface, both via the local network and over the internet via SkyTunnel (if enabled). Web access requires a username, password and a profile assigned, which determines which pages the user can view and how they can interact with those pages.

Permissions determine what areas, doors and outputs a user can access or control on the system and when they are allowed to control them. Permissions are assigned in one of two ways:

  • Individual items are assigned to each user, this offers a lot of flexibility, however, does not scale very well.
  • Permission Groups containing many permissions can be created to suit particular roles in a business. Many users who share the same role, for example, all the warehouse staff, could share the same permission group. This option scales well for larger sites.

Individual permissions can also be marked as Deny, allowing exceptions to the rule. Even if a permission group gives them permission to something, the denied permission would override that.

If there are many users that need to be created, you can import multiple users from a CSV file by clicking the Import CSV button. The CSV import can create users from data supplied in the CSV file. CSV import can map user data from columns representing any of the data fields visible on the Manage Users page, for example, a CSV file could contain a list of column names like Name, Notes, Security PIN, Credentials, Email Address, User Expiry Time. See the Import CSV dialog for more information.

Users can also be exported to CSV which includes some basic information about them. You can select the columns you wish to be included by clicking the Export CSV button to bring up the export dialog. As user updates can also be performed with the Import option, this CSV uses the same column names as the Import CSV format. This allows the users to be exported, some fields changed and then the file re-imported to perform updates to many users at once. Note that the Password and PIN columns will never be populated in the export and are only used to match with import format. Additionally, if the Credentials, Mobile Credentials or Remote Fobs columns are exported, only the first credential or fob number assigned to a user will be exported due to CSV format limitations. Similarly, if the Permissions column is exported, only the user's first assigned Permission Group will be exported.

Depending on your web page profile you may only be able to view users and not modify them.

It is important to identify how a user should be allowed to interact with the system and only give them permission to do the things they need to do, but nothing more.
This page is used to configure Permission Groups which are a collection of permissions that can be assigned to one or more users that have similar roles.

Permissions can be assigned to users in one of two ways, the first method is to add individual items such as a doors, areas or outputs directly to a user. This method does offer a lot of flexibility however does not scale very well once the site has more than a few users. The preferred method for assigning user’s permission is a permission group. A permission group is a collection of permission rules that can be assigned to multiple users.

Alternatively, you can use a combination of these two methods by assigning the user one or more permission groups based on their role and then allowing or denying additional items on a per user basis. The benefit of this is that it reduces the overall number of permission groups required, with the core permissions that a number of users share forming a permission group and any exceptions to that rule being applied to the individual users. Permission groups can also contain additional permission groups, which may be used when a role in the company requires the same permissions as another, as well as additional permissions. For example, warehouse managers could have a permission group created including the warehouse staff permission group, as well as the other permissions required as a manager. Multiple permission groups could also be combined together in this way.

Permission groups are generally created to suit particular roles on a site such as warehouse staff. The Warehouse Staff permission group would contain all the permissions required for a warehouse worker to access the doors and control the areas and outputs for them to do their job. If a new person was hired in the warehouse, you would simply assign them the Warehouse Staff permission group. The other benefit of using a permission group such as Warehouse Staff, is that if a new door was installed in the warehouse, this door would only need to be added to the permission group once rather than adding it individually to ten warehouse staff members.

Depending on your web page profile you may only be able to view permission groups and not modify them.

When creating permission groups it is recommended to first identify what is common between a number of users, this will generally be role based such as IT Staff, Admin Staff etc. However on some sites it may simply be different level of access such as Basic Access or Management Access. The Basic Access permission group would contain all the doors, areas and outputs needed for every user, you would then provide additional permissions or permission groups to users that have other access requirements.

It is recommended that you clearly name the permission groups based on the role to allow them to be assign to users without needing to look at their contents.
This page is used to create and edit credential templates.

Credential templates are used when assigning credentials (e.g. cards, biometric data) to users. The credential template contains information such as the credential type, the site code and the card format of the cards, if applicable.

The Type of Credential field is used by the Inception system to distinguish between different categories of credential data when it is received from a card reader, a fingerprint/iris scanner, or another source. The type of the credential template is taken into account when users attempt to access a door with Dual or Triple Credential Authentication enabled, for example, Dual Credential Authentication requires that at least 2 unique types of credentials are presented at a door reader (e.g. Card type and Fingerprint type) before access is granted.

The site code is a unique facility code that is contained within the data on card type credentials, the site code acts as a second authentication for the credential number. Cards for a site are generally purchased with a single site code, however, multiple purchases of cards can lead to one site using multiple site codes. The card format determines how the data from the card that is badged at a reader is interpreted. This will determine what part of the card data is the credential number and what part of the data is the site code.

When assigning a credential to a user, the first step is to select the credential template. This instructs the system how to interpret that data and what site code to expect. Once a credential template has been added to the user, the credential number is typed in.

If the cards on site use more than one card format or more than one site code a credential template will need to be created for each. Alternatively if the site is only using the raw data from the cards the card format would be set to raw (direct entry).

Without a correctly programmed credential template, no user will be able to access a door.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to create and edit remote fob templates.

The Inception Controller supports the use of RF remotes with either the Concept Paradox RF Expander or the Inovonics RF Expander. This will provide users the ability to remotely control a door, area or output from the press of a button.

Remote fob templates are used when assigning remote fobs to users. The remote fob template contains the type of remote and describes what each button on the remote will do. When assigning a remote fob to a user, the remote fob template is selected and the serial number of the remote fob is typed in. This allows multiple users to be given remotes that have the same button behaviour and if any behaviour changes are required it can be edited in one place. Multiple remote fob templates can also be created if different users require different button behaviours.

The Inception System supports the following remotes:

  • The REM101 is a Paradox single button remote that can be used as a duress pendant or to perform automation.
  • The REM1, REM2 and REM15 are Paradox four button remotes that can be configured with the main control function using the open and closed padlock buttons. These buttons are used to change the state of a door or area. The unlock button could be used to disarm an area and the lock button to arm. The 2 remaining buttons can be configured to perform one of 3 different actions.
  • The Inovonics remotes allow custom actions to be configured for each of the buttons on the remote.

Without a correctly programmed remote fob template, the buttons on the remote fob will not do anything.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to create, edit and control areas.

An area is a collection of inputs that are grouped by their location or purpose. Inputs are assigned to areas and linked with an input type (process group) that determines how the system treats and reacts to the input state changes. Depending on the input type, especially Intruder related ones, certain input state changes may only be processed when the area is armed. The process group determines if sirens or strobes are activated and which alarm reporting message to send, if any.

A user will often arm and disarm an area from an LCD Terminal within that area. This means that the user needs an entry/exit path between the main point of entrance and the LCD Terminal. This is where the detectors along that path won't report alarms or sound sirens during a short entry or exit delay time.

The area can be configured to activate sirens and strobes. The 8 Input Expander can be configured as a Siren Device to drive an internal and external 8-ohm sirens. The Siren Device generates different siren tones based on the process group assigned to the input. In addition to alarm tones, the sirens can also be used to indicate when the area successfully arms, fails to arm, or is about to automatically arm. You can also configure standard outputs as Siren Outputs to activate piezo screamers.

You can configure an area to automatically arm based on an Auto-Arm Schedule or Auto-Arm Inactivity Time. This ensures that rarely used areas are armed more often without needing user interaction, or that areas aren't accidentally left disarmed overnight.

When this page is first opened the list of areas will display the state of each area. As the area changes state the area list dynamically updates. The area state can easily be identified by the text description and colour. The areas can be controlled by clicking the Control button in the toolbar, this will open a window that contains control options and a progress display.

Your ability to view and edit areas from this page is based on your Web Page Profile. Your ability to control areas is based on your User Permissions.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception System. Most users will control areas from the [State/Control > Control Areas] page.
This page is used to easily create multiple areas.

This page allows you to quickly create every area needed for your site. The area creation is done separately from the area configuration to aid the Commissioning Checklist process, allowing the areas to be created early. This allows the areas to be used on the [Configuration > Hardware] page to assign areas to doors and LCD Terminals.
This page is used to easily map inputs to areas.

This page simplifies the process of adding inputs to areas. This is done by adding the inputs to one of four categories. Three of these categories are related to Intruder Detection and will automatically assign the correct Process Group. This ensures that the inputs are processed correctly and the area functions as expected.

You can select the area you wish to map inputs to on the left-hand side, once selected you can define inputs in one of two categories, the Entry/Exit Path inputs and the Intruder and Other inputs.

Entry/exit path inputs are specialised inputs that are located on the primary entry/exit path into the building. These create safe paths from the primary point of entry/exit to an LCD Terminal inside the area that allows area control. This allows for a brief period of time where the area can be entered while armed with no sirens sounding to give a chance to disarm, or armed without sirens sounding to give a chance to exit. Identify any inputs that are on the entry/exit path. For the input(s) that will be triggered first when entering the area, add them to the Primary (Entry Delay Start) section. All other inputs that may trigger after the first inputs have triggered along the entry/exit path, assign them to the Handover (Entry/Exit Path) section. The system will automatically assign in the appropriate process group.

All other intrusion related inputs that are monitored by this area are to be added to the Intruder Inputs section.

Finally, if this area monitors any inputs not related to intrusion such as smoke detectors or duress buttons, these inputs are to be added to the Other Inputs section where you can specify a process group your choice.

Once all the inputs that are monitored by this area have been mapped click the Save button.

Your ability to view and edit input mapping for an area from this page is based on your webpage profile.

This page is commonly used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception System.
This page is used to test area strobes and sirens.

This page allows quick and easy testing of the strobe and sirens linked to each area to verify they are connected and configured correctly. The manual control options can aid in diagnosing complicated issues as it can confirm whether the siren and strobe configuration within each area is correct.

Select the area you wish to test from the area list and click the Turn On or Turn Off button based on the item to test. It is important to remember that there are local regulations that determine the duration of time that a siren can be turned on for, this control will not automatically turn the siren off after a certain period of time.

If the strobe or siren is not functioning correctly navigate to the [Configuration > Areas > Edit Areas] page to check the programming.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to control when doors should automatically lock and unlock, when areas should automatically arm and when lift floors should be set to free access.

The Scheduler is a simple interface for updating door free access times, auto-arming of areas based on a time period and lift floor free access times.

To configure door free access times, select a time period from the Free Access Time Period drop-down selection, when the time period is valid the door will unlock and when invalid the door will lock. Alternatively, you can select an area from the Area State to Follow drop-down selection, when the area is armed the door will lock. This feature can be used together with the Area State to Follow option. In this case, the door will unlock when the time period is valid and the followed area is disarmed. Otherwise, if the followed area is armed or the time period is invalid, the door will remain locked.

This can be used, for example, to allow free access through a door during working hours, but only once an employee has entered accessed the door first and hence the followed area on the other side of the door has been disarmed.

To configure area auto-arm select a time period from the Auto-Arm Time Period drop-down selection. The area will automatically arm when the selected time period becomes active. This can be used, for example, to automatically arm the area late at night. This ensures that the area is armed overnight in case a user forgets to arm it. By default, the area will not automatically disarm when the time period goes invalid, however this can be changed with the Auto-Disarm when Schedule Inactive option on the [Configuration > Areas > Edit Area] page.

Permissions for this page can be provided to a user without them requiring the ability to edit areas or doors. This allows a user to be able to update these properties for doors and areas as required while minimising the chance of important door or area configuration settings being changed accidentally.

To configure lift floor free access times, select a time from the Free Access Time Period drop-down selection, when the time period is valid the floor will be set to free access and when invalid the floor will secure. You can also set the Follow Area State option for the floor. If set, the floor will be set to free access when the time period is valid and the Associated Area is disarmed. Otherwise, if the followed area is armed or the time period is invalid, the floor will remain secured.

This can be used, for example, to allow free access to a floor during working hours, but only once an employee has accessed the floor first and hence the area on that floor has been disarmed.
This page is used to easily set outputs as user controllable outputs so that they can be controlled directly by users, either via the Inception web interface or LCD Terminals.

For a user to be able to control an output, the output must be flagged as user controllable. This option is located on the [Configuration > Outputs > Hardware Outputs] and [Configuration > Outputs > Custom Outputs] pages and can be enabled a single output at a time. This page provides a single location where multiple outputs can be set as user controllable outputs at once. In addition to configuring output as user controllable this page can also configure the output as logged off controllable, allowing the output to be controlled from LCD Terminals without logging in. The logged off control menu is accessed by pressing the [UP] arrow key on the LCD Terminal.

Clicking the Add New button will launch a window that displays all of the currently configured outputs, from this window you can select one or many outputs. Once configured as a user controllable output the output will be displayed when assigning permissions to the user. Provided the user is assigned the output in their permissions they will be able to control the output via the LCD terminal or the web interface.

Your ability to view and edit outputs from this page is based on your webpage profile.

This page is used by the Installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to configure web page profiles. Web page profiles determine the level of functionality a user has on the Inception web interface.

The web page profile determines which pages a user is able to view on the web interface and what options are available on those pages. Their control of items will be based on the permissions assigned to them.

This page is used to create and edit web page profiles which are then assigned to the user on the [Configuration > User Management] page. Once the user is provided with a username, password and web page profile they will be able to login to the web interface.

The Item Editing Permissions can be configured on a per item type basis to allow the user to view the page and related pages and edit the items on it, view the page but not modify items, or not see the page at all. All edit-type pages in the Inception system will appear in this section.

The Item Monitoring Permissions can be configured on a per item type basis to allow the user to view the state of and in some cases control items. Every monitor-type page in the Inception system will appear in this section.

The Task Execution Permissions are configured on a per item basis to allow the user to perform administration related tasks such as backing up the database, restore the database, updating firmware and enabling service mode. These tasks are not dependent on the user permissions and only require the execute option to be selected to enable this functionality.

The Dashboard Content will determine what is displayed for the user on the homepage of the Inception web interface. The dashboard content provides options to show shortcuts to common pages grouped by the type of action. For a shortcut to appear on the dashboard, the web profile also needs to include the correct permission to the page the shortcut relates to.

The web profile can also include a list of areas, doors and outputs that will be shown in the favourite items section of the users dashboard, located on the top of the page. The favourites section allows the user to view the state of and control any items added to without having to navigate to the state/control page.

It is recommended that when creating or modifying profiles, the profile should only be able to view and interact with the pages that are actually required, with the rest being hidden away. This results in an uncluttered and easy to use interface with unimportant items for that user hidden away.
This page is used to configure terminal profiles. Terminal profiles determine the level of functionality a user has on the LCD Terminal.

The terminal profiles determine which menus a user can view on the an LCD Terminal. The user can only control the areas, outputs and doors that their permissions allow them to, even if the profile lets them see the control menus.

This page is used to create and edit terminal profiles which are then assigned to the user on the [Configuration > User Managment] page. Once the user is provided a Security PIN and a terminal profile they will be able to login to an LCD Terminal.

The Terminal Permission section can be configured to allow or deny the user access to commonly used menus on the LCD Terminal.

The Installer Menus section can be configured to allow or deny the user access to menus that are usually restricted to the Installer.

Note: The options found in the Installer menu can compromise the sites security.

It is recommended that when creating or modifying terminal profiles, the profile should only provide the user with menus that are required, with the rest being hidden.
This page is used to control, edit and view the state of inputs. Inputs are created on the [Configuration > Hardware] page

This page is not used for the creation of inputs, inputs are created and named when a hardware module is configured on the [Configuration > Hardware] page. This page is used for the renaming and configuration of the input and also provides you state and control of the inputs.

When naming an input, it is important to use a common naming convention across all inputs to ensure the easy identification of the input purpose and location. Inputs can be configured with the Swap Active and Secure States option if the input is a normally open detection device such as a smoke detector, as opposed to normally closed detection device such as a PIR.

To edit an input click the input from the list and make the necessary changes and click the save button. The input list will also provide you the state of the input this is indicated by a text field that appears in a particular colour.
