Once the input is selected and can be controlled by clicking the control button this allows you to isolate or de-isolate the input. If an input is isolated, the area that monitors the input will not process the inputs state changes until the input is de-isolated.

Your ability to view and edit inputs from this page is based on your webpage profile.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.

This page is used to edit calculated inputs. Calculated inputs are virtual/logical inputs that can be used as real hardware inputs throughout the system, but their active/inactive state is controlled by the system itself.

A calculated input is an input that has no physical location or mechanism and exists only within the Inception Controller's logic. The calculated input and the state that it represents can be used in the system as a normal input, being added to areas and assigned an input type, triggering reportable alarm events and activating area sirens. With no physical hardware, the state of the input is instead controlled by the system.

Calculated inputs are created automatically under certain circumstances. For example, when a door is created in the system and is assigned a reed or tongue sense input, the Inception controller knows whether that door is open or closed. Two calculated inputs are automatically generated, one that represents when that door is forced (the door opened while locked) and one that represents when that door is open too long (held open past the held time limit and held warning time). These inputs can then be added to areas for monitoring and reporting those events.

The Inception system controls the state of calculated inputs automatically, based on the state of other things in the system. This allows scenarios that would normally require one or more automated actions or complex workarounds to be easily monitored and reported on. For example, the door forced calculated input will only activate if a door opens while in the locked state. This replaces techniques in some other systems where they monitor the reed or tongue input directly and regularly isolate and de-isolate the input on every door unlock.

This page is used to configure calculated inputs such as changing their name and also provides you with the state of the input and the ability to isolate it.

Your ability to view and edit inputs from this page is based on your web page profile.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception System.

This page is used to control and edit custom inputs. Custom inputs are virtual/logical inputs that can be used as real hardware inputs throughout the system, but their active/inactive state is instead controlled by automated actions.

A custom input is an input that has no physical location or mechanism and exists only within the Inception Controller's logic. The custom input and the state that it represents can be used in the system as a normal input, being added to areas and assigned an input type, triggering reportable alarm events and activating area sirens. With no physical hardware, the state of the input is instead controlled by automated actions. These actions could be triggered by any state changes within the system, or by a 3rd party system via the 3rd Party Automation Interface, allowing reportable and actionable alarm events to be generated from other systems.

While this feature allows 3rd party system to create reportable alarm events, it should not be used as the method for reporting critical alarms such as fire alarms. It is recommended that a low-level connection is used between the systems involving physical inputs, outputs and end of line resistors for reporting any critical alarms.

This page is used to create and configure custom inputs and also provides you with the state of the input and the ability to isolate it.

To add a new custom input click the Add button. These inputs can be assigned a name, but beyond that are just simple inputs that can be either active or inactive.

Your ability to view and edit outputs from this page is based on your web page profile.

Your ability to control inputs from this page is based on your user permissions.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception System.
This page is used to control, edit and view the state of outputs. Outputs are created on the [Configuration > Hardware] page.

This page is not used for the creation of outputs, outputs are created and named when a hardware module is configured on the [Configuration > Hardware] page. This page is used for the renaming and configuration of the outputs and also provides you state and control of the outputs.

When naming an output, it is important to use a common naming convention across all outputs to ensure the easy identification of the output purpose and location. The output can be configured as User Controllable, this will allow a user to control the output via the web interface or by logging into the LCD Terminals provided the output is assigned to their user permissions. An output can also be configured as Logged off Controllable to allow the output to be controlled from LCD Terminals without logging in. The logged off control menu is accessed by pressing the [UP] arrow key on the LCD Terminal.

To edit an output, click the output from the list and make the necessary changes and click the save button. The output list will also provide you the state of the output, this is indicated by a text field that appears in a particular colour. Once the output is selected it can be controlled by clicking the control button this allows you to turn on or turn off the output.

Your ability to view and edit outputs from this page is based on your webpage profile.

Your ability to control outputs from this page is based on your user permissions.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.

This page is used to control and edit custom outputs. Custom outputs are virtual/logical outputs that are primarily used in automation as a state holder that is controlled without a physical output activating.

A custom output is an output that has no physical location or mechanism and exists only within the Inception Controller's logic. The custom output and the state that it represents can then be used to trigger Automated Actions. When programming automation, you may need to use a custom item to trigger some automation or to hold the state of some automation. This could be an output turning on when an input goes into alarm or needing something that is user controllable. It is not recommended to use a physical output as something could eventually be connected to the output or the relay may activate thousands of times a day.

This page is used to create and configure custom outputs and also provides you with the state of the output and the ability to control it.

To add a new custom output click the Add button. Much like a normal output, a custom output can have a name and be configured as User Controllable and Logged Off Controllable. A user controlled custom output could be used to manually control a number of automation responses, or reset the current state of the automation.

Your ability to view and edit outputs from this page is based on your web page profile.

Your ability to control outputs from this page is based on your user permissions.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception System.
This page is used to create and edit automated actions to allow additional automation functionality on the Inception system.

The Inception system contains options for many of the commonly used automatic responses of intruder and access control systems. For any custom functionality outside of the core features, automated actions can be used. These would most often be simple automation cases such as turning on the lights when the area is disarmed, but have the flexibility to handle very complicated scenarios.

The automated action is configured in two parts; the trigger condition and the action responses. The trigger condition determines when this automated action is true or false. When the trigger condition changes from false to true, a set of actions can be automatically executed. Similarly, when the trigger condition changes from true to false, a different set of actions can be automatically executed.

The trigger condition can use the state of any area, door, input, output or time period and will create a trigger statement that is either true or false. For example, a trigger statement could be that the Area Building Area is Disarmed, or that the Door Front Door is not Locked. Groups can also be created, linking multiple statements or even other groups together. All of these statements and groups will combine together and the trigger condition will still be either true or false.

You may want the Automated Actions to do the opposite of each other, for example, when the trigger condition is true an output turns on and when false the output turns off. Alternatively, you may only want to setup a single action.

There are no default automated actions, you can add an additional automated action by clicking Add button in the toolbar.

This page should only be used by the installer during the setup or ongoing maintenance of the system. Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit the automated actions.
This page is used to control and edit doors. Use the Hardware page to add new Doors to your system

An access control door is any electronically controlled obstruction, such as a hinged door, auto door, roller door or boom gate, that restricts a user accessing an area. The door and door related hardware are connected to the Inception Controller or Standard LAN Access Modules (SLAM).

The door is made up of a number of components, including:

  • Lock Output - This is the output that controls the electronic lock, when a door is unlocked the output will turn on. The door can be unlocked and locked by a user requesting access with a card or PIN, the web interface, or by pressing a REX (Request to Exit) button. The door can also be configured to automatically unlock and lock based on schedules or the state of the area on one side of the door.
  • Readers - SIFER/OSDP or Wiegand readers are installed on one side or both sides of a door. The readers provide the controller with card or PIN information to identify a user who is requesting access. If they have permission to access the door, the door can unlock.
  • REX/REN inputs - The Request to Exit and Request to Enter inputs are usually wired to buttons or detectors that, if pressed, will cause the door to briefly unlock. Often placed on the side of the door where access control is not restricted, allowing the door to unlock to exit the door without presenting a credential or creating a door forced alarm.
  • Reed/tongue inputs - These inputs allow the system to know if the door is open/unsecured or closed securely. This can be used with the door's lock state to identify DOTL (Door Open Too Long) or Door Forced states.
  • DOTL - DOTL (Door Open Too Long) is a logical state for a door which occurs when the door has been unlocked and opened and the system has tried to lock the door, however the door has not been closed within the Held (DOTL) Time configured on this page. The DOTL input goes into the alarm state once the configured Held (DOTL) Time and Held (DOTL) Warning Time expire without the door closing securely. Once triggered, the DOTL input seals when the door is closed.
  • Door Forced - Door Forced is a logical state for a door which occurs when a door is opened without the door being unlocked first. When this state occurs the door forced input will go into alarm. Once triggered, the Door Forced input will seal when the door closes or is unlocked.

If doors are configured with inside and outside areas, numerous features can be used. User locations can update with access requests, the door can follow the area state to prevent unlocking when the area is armed, readers linked to the door can provide audible and visual feedback for area state changes and the readers can also be used to arm the area.

The door is created and configured in the [Configuration > Hardware] page. This page provides the same options as the [Configuration > Hardware] page, as well as some additional advanced options to configure the operation of the door. In addition to naming and configuring the doors, this page provides the state and control of doors, allowing you to see live state changes to any configuration changes you make. If your web page profile and user permissions allow, you can click on the control button to unlock, lock, override and grant access to the selected door.

This page is used by the Installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to edit readers. Readers must be configured from the [Configuration > Hardware] page in order for them to appear on this page.

The reader is the access point that a user interacts with to unlock a door. Depending on the door there may be a reader on one side or on both sides of the door. The reader is usually configured and associated with a door in the configuration hardware menu. This page provides additional reader specific options to configure the operation of the reader.

A large number of options on this page are not editable as they are determined by the configuration hardware menu. The options displayed on this page will vary based on the type of reader connected, the common option across all readers is a three badge action. The three badge action is the action that will occur when a user presents a card three times in quick succession to the reader. This action can be set to arm the area on the other side of the door to where this reader is located, or arm all of the areas in the system. Please note that if the Arm the Area on the Other Side of the Door option is selected the door programming requires an area to be specified.

When using SIFER or OSDP readers, they can be configured to provide door or area feedback, including showing state information via the status LED. For SIFER readers this is the small LED, for OSDP readers it is the main LED. SIFER readers also include options to change the volume of the beeps produced by the SIFER reader and the brightness of the LEDs or the colour to display to customize the appearance if not showing state feedback.

This page is used by the Installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to enrol and configure hardware modules.

The hardware page displays all the unconfigured and configured modules on Inception System. A hardware module is any expansion module that connects to the Inception controller by the RS-485 LAN to provide additional inputs, outputs, doors or readers. For the devices to work correctly they must first be configured.

When configuring a module, you will be stepped through multiple sections of programming. These steps will depend on the type of module that is being configured, for example, a reader module will have reader and door programming options as well as inputs and outputs, however, an input expander will only have options for inputs and outputs.

A module can be configured by selecting it and clicking the Edit/Configure button or by double-clicking the module. A module is considered to be configured once all of the steps are completed and the Finish button has been clicked.

In addition to configuring modules that are currently attached to the Inception Controller, this page can be used to create and configure new hardware that will be attached in the future. Clicking the Create New button will open a window where you are able to select the module type you wish to add.

This page does not dynamically update as modules are attached. If you have added new hardware to the Inception Controller while the page is open click the Refresh button to update the page.

What you able to do on this page will depend on your web page profile, in some cases, you may only be able to view the hardware modules and not edit them.
This page is used to create and edit lift floors.

A lift floor is a destination that a button in a lift car could take a user to. A lift floor can either span multiple lift cars or be limited to a single lift car. A floor that spans multiple lift cars usually contains an area or facilities that are required by many users such as a basement carpark. When a lift floor is only accessed by a single lift car it is usally beacause the lift car serves a particular purpose, such as a service lift or one that provides access to restricted levels.

Users are granted permission to access lift floors, so that when they present a card or PIN at a reader within a lift car, the buttons that would take them to the floors they can access would be enabled by Inception. Lift floors can also be in one of three states, Free Access, Secured or Locked Out. A floor in free access will cause all of the lift cars that can access that floor to enable the button for that floor, allowing any user to select that floor without needing to present a card. A secured floor requires a card to be presented in a lift car before a button linked to the floor would be enabled. A locked out floor will prevent a button linked to the floor from enabling, even if a user with permission to the floor presents their card.

Lift floors are secured by default, however a time period and an area can be configured to schedule when free access to a floor is allowed. When the time period assigned to the Free Access Time is valid, the floor will be in free access and if invalid, the floor will be secure. This can be used with the Follow Area State option, causing the floor to be in free access only if the time period is valid and the Associated Area is disarmed, otherwise the floor is secure. This can allow, for example, free access to a floor during working hours but only once an employee has entered the area and disarmed the area.

There doesn’t need to be one lift floor created per building floor though, in some cases there may be multiple lift floors created for the same physical floor in a building, for example if the lift car has two doors and there are different buttons for 7A and 7B. Two lift floors can then be created with different areas and permissions assigned to each side of the floor. Alternatively, if a particular lift car needs its access to be restricted to only certain users, for example a service elevator, there could be normal floors created for the other cars, along with another set of service floors for the service lift car, with only maintenance workers having permission to those floors.

You need only create lift floors for the levels in a building where you wish to restrict users access. If there are lift car buttons and floors that Inception does not need to control access to, then they do not require a lift floor.

If using the lift integration functionality for a reason other than lift car control, such as lockers, garages etc, then lift floors could be created to represent the inside of the locker or parking spot, or whatever destination that is being protected by an output. Users could then be given permission to access that lift floor.
This page is used to create and edit lift cars.

A lift car represents a physical lift car or button panel that the user interacts with. Lift cars require hardware outputs in the Inception system to be wired to the lift button panel so they can enable and disable whether the lift button works. Inputs can also be wired into the button to indicate when the button has been pressed. Using this information and ability in addition to a credential reader, allows Inception to control which floor buttons a user is allowed to press when they present their card, biometric, or PIN credential.

Lift cars are made up of a number of components, including:

  • Readers - Multiple readers can be configured for a single lift car, along with the number of credential types that are required for a user to be identified (Single, Dual or Triple Credential Authentication). This allows, for example, readers to be mounted at different heights for accessibility, multiple readers to be used for different credential types or a separate reader and keypad for card and PIN entry.
  • Buttons - Represents each physical lift button that Inception is able to control. Requires both a Lift Floor for the button's destination and the output that has been wired into the lift button. Optionally, an input can be specified that would indicate when the button has been pressed.
  • Feedback Outputs - These are the outputs that can be used to give visual or audible feedback to the user in the lift. A number of outputs can be specified along with how they should behave.
  • Button Times - Configures how long to enable the buttons for once a user presents their credential. Includes an optional extended enable time if the user presenting their credential has the Use Extended Unlock Times option enabled.

The inputs, outputs and readers required by the lift car must be created separately using the [Configuration > Hardware] page.

A lift car should be created for the physical button panel that it controls. This could mean, for example, that a destination panel in the lobby would also be treated as a lift car. Also, if there are two readers and two button panels within the same lift car, then two separate lift car items should be created so the button panels can be controlled individually.

If the lift integration functionality is being used for a reason other than lift car control, such as lockers, garages etc, then lift cars would link the reader of the system to the outputs that are being controlled. The readers would still be where the user presents their credential, however a button would instead represent a locker door or a garage roller door that can be controlled by an output. The lift floor would be the inside of the locker or the parking spot and the users could be given permission to access that lift floor.
This page is used to create and edit storage units.

To protect the storage units, they can contain motion or door position detectors to monitor unauthorized access to the space. If unauthorized access is detected, the unit enters an alarm state which can sound sirens and be reported to a monitoring station.

To monitor a storage unit, when a unit is created, a Calculated Input is also created to represent if that unit is in alarm. Instead of monitoring the detector inputs directly by the Area, this calculated input can be monitored instead. When assigned to an area, it is recommended that this special input is assigned the Storage Unit input type, which will cause local sirens if that area is disarmed and sirens and a reported alarm event if that area is armed. This allows staff to control whether alarm events should be reported for the unit alarms, for example, leaving the area disarmed while staff are on site during the day, and arming at night for after-hours customer access.

Units in a Secure state will monitor its inputs and enter an alarm state when they activate. If Unlocked, then the unit can be accessed and the alarm state will not occur. Any outputs selected in the Unit Outputs option will also turn on when the unit unlocks, which could be used to control unit lighting, a door lock, etc. If a unit is not being rented, it can be marked as Vacant allowing free access to the unit.

The unit will enter a Tamper state if any of the inputs within the unit are tampered with.

Storage Blocks (configured on the [Configuration > Access Control > Storage Unit > Storage Blocks] page) can be used to group units together and provide additional functionality for them. Typically this represents a set of units, or the entire facility. When a User presents a credential at the Entry Reader for a block, their unit can be automatically unlocked. Similary, when they leave the block via an Exit Reader, their unit is automatically secured. Blocks provide a common programming location for their units, allowing an Unlocked Too Long time to be configured which alerts when a unit has been unlocked for too long, and an Auto-Resecure time that lets a unit re-secure automatically after its door has been closed.

Users are granted permission to control storage units, letting them unlock and secure the unit and control the vacancy state. In addition, if a user has Unlock on Block Access permission to a unit, it will automatically unlock as they enter the Block and automatically secure when they leave. The granular permissions allow customers to be able to conveniently access the unit or units they are renting, while allowing staff users full control over all the units.

User permissions to units and storage unit state can both be managed via the REST API, allowing integrations to automatically create users and give them permission to units, as well as controlling those units and their vacancy.
This page is used to create and edit storage blocks.

A storage block represents a group of storage units. This could represent an entire site with 100s of units, a single building of units in a multi-building site, a single section of a building, and so on. There can be as many blocks as required in a system.

Storage blocks are made up of a number of components, including:

  • Entry Readers - Multiple readers can be configured for a block's entry. These could be dedicated readers or the readers that control existing access control points like an entry gate, the entry door of a building, or so on. A user presenting a credential at one of these readers will cause any Units that they have Control on Block Access permission to control to unlock, if that unit is in this block.
  • Exit Readers - Multiple readers can be configured for a block's exit. Similar to Entry Readers, these could be dedicated readers or the readers that control existing access control points like an exit gates or building exit door. A user presenting a credential at one of these readers will cause any Units that they have Unlock on Block Access permission to control to be secured, if that unit is in this block.
  • Block Outputs - Any outputs selected will turn on if any unit in the block is unlocked. This can allow building or section lighting to turn on only if any units in that building/section are unlocked.
  • Storage Units - These are the units that belong in this block. If required, units can belong to more than one block.
  • Unit Auto-Resecure Time - Configures how long to units in this block will wait before automatically re-securing after their door was opened and then closed.
  • Unit Unlocked Too Long Time - Configures how long to units in this block will wait before entering the Unlocked Too Long state.

The outputs and readers required by the storage block must be created separately using the [Configuration > Hardware] page.

A storage block should represent a logical set of storage units with clear entry and exit points controlled with access control. For simple sites, there may be one block for the entire site with entry and exit gate readers used for automatic unit control in the block. Blocks could also represent a building full of units, and the entry and exit readers for that building would be used by the block.

Blocks serve a secondary feature though, providing summary states of their units for use with automation. Many smaller blocks could be created that all use the same entry and exit readers, but only control a smaller set of blocks. This would allow more granular section lighting or other automation to be easily managed.

Alternatively, to better manage common settings, a whole site block could exist with entry and exit readers to control all of the units, set common resecure and unlocked too long times, and so on. Additional blocks can then be created just for local building automation. Those additional blocks wouldn't have readers configured, and would just contain the units in a section of the building and the outputs to control lighting in that section. If using this approach, be sure to set the Unit Unlocked Too Long and Unit Auto-Resecure times to None to not override the values in the main Block.

This page is used to create and edit connections that allow the Inception system to handle text-based 2-way communications with other systems. These connections can be used to trigger certain actions when specific messages are received from the other system, or send messages to the other system when events occur in the Inception system.

Inception's Connections are intended to act as low-level, generic text based communication handlers which enable integration with other systems through sending and receiving short text messages over a network, serial or HTTP connection.

Serial Connections can be configured to connect to any serial device that has been previously connected to the Inception system. You can choose to either connect to the first plugged in and available serial device, or pick one of the devices from a list. The baud rate, parity bit, number of data bits and number of stop bits are all configurable for serial connections.

TCP Connections can be configured to act as a server and listen for incoming connections on a specified port on the Inception device, or to act as a client and establish a connection to a specified server given an IP address and port number.

TCP (Client) Connections work by opening a connection to an IP address/port number, and holding that connection open indefinitely while sending text messages through to the other system. They are also capable of bidirectional communication through receiving text messages back from the other system's server, if the server supports that functionality. If the TCP connection fails due to network problems or configuration issues, the TCP Client connection will continually attempt to reconnect to the server until it is successful.

TCP (Server) Connections work by continuously listening for incoming connections on a specific port and accepting clients, and accepting all incoming text messages from any of those connected clients. Only one TCP Server connection can be listening on a single port at a given time; attempting to have more than one connection listening on the same port will result in the first connection successfully listening on that port and the others failing to initialise. The TCP Server connection is also capable of sending text messages to the connected clients if an action uses the server connection as a target. Sending a text message through the TCP Server connection will forward that message to all TCP clients currently connected to the server (if no clients are connected, no message will be sent), which allows for bidirectional communication with clients if they support this functionality.

If bi-directional communication over TCP is desired, both the TCP Client and TCP Server connection types are capable of both sending and receiving messages over the one connection; however, it is generally more consistent and reliable to use a dedicated connection for each responsibility, i.e. using a dedicated client connection solely for sending messages and a dedicated server connection solely for receiving messages, if the other system supports this configuration.

HTTP (Send) Connections work by sending a HTTP GET request to a URL on a web server, with optional authentication. This type of connection is best suited for communicating with systems that expose a HTTP API, for example, a camera that can be made to perform pan & zoom actions when it receives requests for certain URLs. When the HTTP connection sends a message, it works by appending the message text to the URL Path specified in the connection details, and performing a GET request aimed at that URL. For example, if the URL Path is http://www.example.com/ and the message being sent is zoom.cgi?dir=in&amount=100, the connection would perform a GET request on the URL http://www.example.com/zoom.cgi?dir=in&amount=100. HTTP connections are only capable of sending messages, they do not support receiving messages. If a connection sends a message and receives a Forbidden response from the server, or no response at all, the connection status will reflect this by changing to Connection Failed. The data framing prefix/suffix fields are ignored by HTTP connections.

HTTP (Send) Connections also support Basic and Digest authentication for sending requests to systems that require user authorisation. Basic authentication is the simplest and most common type, and it only requires a username & password to be set. Digest authentication is less commonly used but can be more secure, as it only sends hashed information. Digest authentication requires that a username, password, and domain is specified. Check the requirements of the target system's HTTP API to find out what type of authentication should be used for the Connection, if any.

HTTP (Receive) Connections work by listening for incoming HTTP requests on the /receive_http_connection endpoint on the Inception web server, with a key URL parameter to identify the intended receiving Connection in the system by its Key String field, and the message text supplied in either another URL parameter (for GET requests) or in the request body (for POST requests). To send a request to a Connection with example as its Key String, the request would have to contain /receive_http_connection?key=example. The Key String field must be unique amongst all HTTP Receive Connections. HTTP Basic authentication can be enabled for added security with a required username and password.

HTTP (Receive) Connections support receiving messages via both GET and POST requests. When sending messages to HTTP Receive Connections via GET requests, clients should supply the message text in the message URL parameter, for example: to send a message of test to a Connection with the Key String example, a client would make a request like GET in123456.local/receive_http_connection?key=example&message=test. When sending messages via POST requests, the message text should be placed in the request body instead, e.g. POST in123456.local/receive_http_connection?key=example with a body containing test.

If a Connection is repeatedly failing or messages are not being sent/received properly, enabling Detailed Events from the [System > Service] page can help to diagnose faults by showing reasons for failure and individual message information in the [State / Control > Review Events] page. For example, if the review messages show that only partial messages are being received, there may be a problem with the Connection's framing strings.

Connections can be optionally configured to have framing characters to denote the start or end of a message being sent or received. These framing characters also support escape sequences to aid in specifying special characters like newline or null characters. For example, setting the suffix for received messages to '\\r\\n' would require the other system to end all messages with a carriage return + line feed character sequence to signify that the message ends before those characters.

Incoming messages received through Connections can be used to trigger actions in the Inception system by configuring an Automated Action ([Configuration > Automated Actions]) and using the Connection item as part of a trigger condition. They can also be used to send messages when an Automated Action is triggered, by adding a Send Custom Text response to the Automated Action. Text messages in trigger conditions and action responses can also contain any of the escape sequences that the framing prefixes/suffixes support.

Your web page profile will determine if you can create or modify the items on this page.
This page is used to create and edit counters to allow advanced automation events when used in conjunction with automated actions.

Counters are items that contain an integer number value for their internal state, which can be modified manually or by events in the system. They have no additional properties besides an optional minimum and maximum limit value; the counter's functionality consists entirely of its state value, which can be viewed and controlled on the [State / Control > Control Counters] page.

Counters are primarily intended for usage in combination with automated actions, to allow advanced automation functionality to be configured, such as triggering an action once a certain number of events have occurred, e.g. an input activation counter or reader badge counter. Counter state values are persisted across system power cycles.

The counter's value can be restricted to a certain range by using the Limit Min/Max Values, Minimum Value and Maximum Value fields. Attempting to set a counter's value outside this range will cause it to be clamped to the nearest min or max limit value. When setting the minimum and maximum allowed values for a counter, the minimum value cannot be larger than the maximum value, and vice versa.

You can control counters on this page by clicking the Control button in the toolbox in the top-right corner of the page. The control dialog allows you to increment, decrement and manually set values for counters.

Counters can be used in conjunction with automated actions in 2 ways: as a trigger condition, and as an action response. As a trigger condition, counters can be used to trigger actions when their value becomes less than, greater than, or equal to a certain value. As an action response, the automated action can increment or decrement the counter's value by a specific number, or set the counter's value to a specific number.

This page should only be used by the installer during the setup or ongoing maintenance of the system. Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit the counters.
This page is used to view and control the state of areas. To control an area you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page will dynamically display the state of areas on the Inception Controller. This page also allows you to arm and disarm areas remotely via web interface.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the areas and the page will dynamically update as the areas change state. The area state can easily be identified by the colour of the area and the text description in the Status column.

The areas can be controlled in three different ways:

  • Clicking the Quick Control button for each area will arm or disarm the area based on its current state.
  • The Advanced Control button will open a window that contains control options and a progress display.
  • The Control All button in the toolbar will also open a window that contains control options and a progress display, however, these controls will affect every area that you have permission to control.

The [Control Areas] page also displays the Next Scheduled Arm. This is the time that, without any user interaction, this area is scheduled to automatically arm.

The areas that you are able to control will be based on your user permissions which applies any time or control restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the areas.

The [Control Areas] page would commonly be used if a user had left the site and had forgotten to arm an area or were unsure if the area arm was successful. In most cases this could be avoided by using an Auto-Arm Schedule for the area. However, you would be able to remotely arm the area ensuring the site is secure.

This page is used to view and control the state of doors. You can lock, unlock, lockout or grant access to doors from this page. To control a door, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page will dynamically display the state of doors. The doors state is determined by combining the state of the door lock, door reed and tongue sense. The door reed and tongue sense determine if the door is closed securely or open/unsecured. This page lets you control a single door or all doors based on your user permissions.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the doors, the page will dynamically update as the doors change state. The door state can easily be identified by the colour of the door and the text description in the Status column.

The doors can be controlled in 3 different ways:

  • Clicking the Quick Control button for a locked door will unlock the door for its Default Unlock Time, allowing a user to access the door. If the door is unlocked the Quick Control button will lock the door.
  • The Advanced Control button will open a window that contains control options to override the doors state and a progress display.
  • The Control All button in the toolbar will also open a window that contains control options and a progress display, however, these controls will affect every door that you have permission to control.

The override options are used to temporarily change the normal behaviour of a door, locking a door that should be unlocked or unlocking a door that is normally locked. A door can also be placed in the Lockout state in an emergency, preventing any users from being granted access to the door until the lockout is cleared. Once an override is cleared, the door will revert back to its normal behaviour.

The doors that you are able to control will be based on your user permissions including any time or access restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the doors.

In most cases, a door is controlled by a user presenting a card at a reader, schedules or automation. You would use the [Control Doors] page to temporarily override the default behaviour of a door if the user has forgotten their card and is unable to unlock a door and enter the building.
This page is used to control and view the state of inputs. You can isolate and de-isolate inputs from this page.

This page is to identify any abnormal status for inputs and, if found, isolate the input to avoid unnecessary alarms being generated or remote arming of areas failing.

When this page is first opened all the current states for the inputs are displayed and the page will dynamically update as inputs change state. The input state can easily be identified by the colour of the input and the text description in the Status column.

The inputs can be controlled by clicking the Advanced Control button, this will open a window that contains control options and a progress display. If an input is isolated from this page, the system will ignore all of the inputs state changes until it is de-isolated. An input that is isolated on this page can only be de-isolate from this page. This allows faulty inputs or those undergoing maintenance to remain isolated until they are fixed.

Since inputs can not be assigned to user permissions the control of inputs is based only on your web page profile. Your web page profile will determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control inputs.

This page is used to view and control the state of outputs. In order for you to control an output, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web profile. The output itself requires the User Controllable option set in the [Configuration > Outputs > Set as User Controllable] page.

This page will dynamically display the state of all the configured outputs on the Inception Controller. This page also allows you to turn outputs on or off remotely via web interface.

When this page is first opened, all the current states for the outputs are displayed and the page will dynamically update as the outputs change state. The state of the output can easily be identified by the colour of the output and the text description in the Status column. The outputs can be controlled in two different ways:

  • Clicking the Quick Control button for each output will turn the output on or off based on its current state.
  • The Advanced Control button will open a window that contains control options and a progress display. When an output is controlled from this window the control is timed using the Control for Time option, this allows you to turn an output on or off for a period of time.

Only outputs that are configured as User Controllable in the [Configuration > Outputs > Hardware Outputs] or [Configuration > Outputs > Custom Outputs] pages will appear on this page.

The Outputs that can be controlled are based on your user permissions, which applies any time or control restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the outputs.

Since outputs can be used for many non-security functions such as controlling lighting or air-conditioning this page is commonly used to interact with building automation. You could easily turn lighting on or off using a smart phone, this could be used to ensure that all lighting is turned off at the end of the day.

Please note: It is not recommended to provide a user control of an output associated to the security system such as a piezo screamer. They may accidentally turn on an extremely loud screamer remotely that nobody onsite can turn off.
This page is used to view the state of hardware modules. This is used to identify modules that are online, offline or have an abnormal status.

This page will dynamically display the state of the Inception Controller and all Inception hardware modules. Inception hardware modules are devices that are added to the Inception Controller to provide additional inputs, outputs, doors and readers. These hardware modules require an RS-485 LAN connection to the controller to function. If this connection is severed, the module does not function and is considered to be offline and all detector inputs on that module are considered tampered with.

When this page is first opened, all the current states of the hardware modules are displayed and as the modules states change the page will update. The module state can easily be identified by the colour of the module and the text description in the Status column. This page is informational only and does not provide any control options for the user.

This page will only display the modules that have been configured in the [Configuration > Hardware] page.

Depending on their configuration, the system will monitor the modules for a number of health issues such as tampering or power issues. If these issues will persist for a period of time, the module can be Isolated, causing the system to ignore health issues from that module. This prevents health issues from that module from being reported to monitoring stations and also prevents users from being prompted about the health issues from that module when they attempt to arm areas. Once the health issue is fixed, the module can be de-isolated to return it to normal behaviour.

The [View Hardware] page is commonly used by the installer during the initial installation of the Inception System during maintenance. This page allows the installer to identify any issues with the modules that may impact site security. It also allows modules to be isolated if issues are present that may take some time to repair.

This page is used to view the state of peripheral devices. Peripherals are the advanced devices that can be connected to hardware modules, like Unibus expanders on 8 Input Expanders, or SIFER or OSDP readers on SLAMs or the Controller.

This page will dynamically display the state of all the attached peripheral devices. Inception peripherals are devices that users interact with such as SIFER Readers that attach to hardware modules. These peripherals are monitored, for example, SIFER or OSDP readers are monitored to determine if they are still connected, unlike a Wiegand reader. This page allows an installer to identify and troubleshoot issues with peripheral devices.

When this page is first opened all the current states for the Peripherals are displayed, as the peripherals change state the page will update. The peripherals state can easily be identified by the colour of the peripheral and the text description in the Status column. This page is informational only and does not provide any control options for the user.

This page will only display the peripherals that have been connected to the Inception system.

The [View Peripherals] page would commonly be used by the installer during the initial installation of the Inception System or during maintenance. This page allows the installer to identify any issues with the peripherals that may impact site security.
This page is used to view the state of time periods. Time periods are specific days/times that can be used with permissions or automated actions.

This page will dynamically display the state of all the time periods. Time periods are generally used for scheduling system responses or user permissions. This page displays the time period state and shows when the next time period change will occur, allowing you to identify why a door is not yet unlocked or why a user does not have access to a particular item.

When this page is first opened, all the current states for the Time Periods are displayed and as the Time Periods change state the page will update. The time period state can easily be identified by the colour of the time period and the text description in the Status column. This page is informational only and does not provide any control options for the user.

The [View Time Periods] page would commonly be used to identify whether a time period is active or inactive and when the next time period change will occur without needing to open time period programming. If time periods states appear to be incorrect, ensure the current date and time of the controller is correct (seen at the bottom of the web page or on the [Configuration > General > Date and Time] page)
This page is used to view the location of users. Each time a user enters a door that is configured with areas on the other side, the user's location will update.

This page is used to identify where a user is located, this may be required in an emergency situation or simply when you want to easily find a person. User location can also be used to identify who is on site or off site if users need to badge in when they arrive and badge out to leave.

This location is updated based on the door access requests. If access is granted, the user will have their location updated to the area on the other side of the door. Note, if the door has a reed or tongue configured, the user's location will update when the door opens. This of course cannot account for doors in free access or with REX buttons, or a user tailgating another user in or out of a door.

User location is more accurate when used on a site that has multiple areas, with all areas physically separated by Read In and Read Out doors.

When this page is first opened, all the current locations for the Users are displayed and as the user location changes the page will update the location column. This page is informational only and does not provide any control options for the user. If the user does not have a location they have not entered a door programmed with inside or outside areas.

If all your doors are configured with inside and outside areas this page can give you an accurate indication of their last known location. In the case of an emergency such as a fire alarm, this page can be used to provide emergency services the user's last known location to assist in finding the user.

If anti passback is enabled on any doors, anti passback violations or exemptions will also be shown on this page. Forgiveness for violations gives the user an anti passback exemption and can be given on a per-user basis, to all users with a violation or to every user. If a user has an exemption, their next access request that their permissions allow will be granted, even if it would have caused an anti passback violation. The purpose of an exemption is to help the user correct their location in the system, so a successful location change will automatically use up the exemption.

If there are a certain number of login attempts allowed for each user, they will be locked out after exceeding those login attempts and it will be displayed on this page. Quick control will allow you to end the lockout early for this user. This page can also forgive all users with a login lockout at the same time as long as your priority ranking is higher than each user being forgiven.

Your ability to grant anti passback forgiveness or lift login lockouts for users will depend on your web page profile and whether it lets you control users.

This page is used to view and control the state of lift floors. You can set to free access, secure or lockout floors from this page. To control a floor, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page will dynamically display the state of floors. Floors can be in one of three states, Free Access, Secured or Locked Out. A floor in free access will cause the lift cars that can access that floor to enable the button for that floor, allowing any user to select that floor without needing to present a card. A secured floor requires a card to be presented in a lift car before a button linked to the floor would be enabled. A locked out floor will prevent a button linked to the floor from enabling, even if a user with permission to the floor presents their card.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the floors, the page will dynamically update as the floors change state. The floor state can easily be identified by the colour of the floor and the text description in the Status column.

The floors can be controlled in 3 different ways:

  • Clicking the Quick Control button for a secured floor will override its state to free access or if the floor is in free access it will secure the floor.
  • The Advanced Control button will open a window that contains control options to override the floors state and a progress display.
  • The Control All button in the toolbar will also open a window that contains control options and a progress display, however, these controls will affect every floor that you have permission to control.

The override options are used to temporarily change the normal behaviour of a floor, securing a floor that should be in free access or freeing a floor that is normally secured. A floor can also be placed in the Lockout state in an emergency, preventing any users from being granted access to the floor until the lockout is cleared. Once an override is cleared, the floor will revert back to its normal behaviour.

The floors that you are able to control will be based on your user permissions, including any time or access restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the floors.

In most cases, a floor is controlled by schedules or automation. You would use the [Control Lift Floors] page to temporarily override the default behaviour of a floor for an emergency or a special scenario.

This page is used to view and control the state of storage units. You can unlock, secure, or control the vacancy of units from this page. To control a unit, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page will dynamically display the state of storage units. Unlocked or Vacant units can be opened and accessed without triggering alarm states. If a unit has a Door Position Sensor input specified, the unit will also show if its door is open or not.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the units, the page will dynamically update as the units change state. The unit state can easily be identified by the colour of the floor and the text description in the Status column. It will also include information on who last accessed or controlled the unit.

The units can be controlled in 3 different ways:

  • Clicking the Quick Control button for a unit will unlock or secure it based on its current state. If vacant, the quick control button will clear that vacancy.
  • The Advanced Control button will open a window that contains control options to override the unit state and a progress display.
  • The Control All button in the toolbar will also open a window that contains control options and a progress display, however, these controls will affect every unit that you have permission to control.

A secure unit will enter the Alarm state if any of its sensors activate. Unlocked units can be opened without risk of alarm, but may enter an Unlocked Too Long state based on configuration. For units that will remain empty, they can be marked as vacant, allowing free access and no automation responses.

Units that have their inputs tampered with will enter a Tamper state.

The units that you are able to control will be based on your user permissions, including any time or access restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the units.

In most cases, a unit is controlled by a users block access. You would use the [Control Storage Units] page to manually unlock a unit, or control that unit's vacancy.
This page is used to view and control scheduled tasks. To control a scheduled task you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page will dynamically display the state of scheduled tasks in the Inception system. This page displays the scheduled task's state, the last time the task was run (if known), and the next time a task is due to run.

The scheduled task can be controlled by clicking the Advanced Control button and then the Run Task Immediately button from the advanced control dialog. This will queue the task to run immediately, and will show the progress and any errors encountered if the run was unsuccessful.

The scheduled tasks that you are able to view and/or control will be based on your assigned web page profile.

In most cases, a scheduled task automatically executes itself in accordance with a pre-defined schedule, or when triggered by an automated action. You would use the [Control Scheduled Tasks] page either to view the current state, last run time, and next scheduled run time of scheduled tasks; or to manually execute the scheduled tasks if you wish to test that the task is properly configured and handles the sending of results, emails, etc. correctly.

This page is used to view and control the state of counters. You can increment, decrement and manually set counter values from this page. To control a counter, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page will dynamically display the state of counters. Counters' states are represented by a number value, which can be incremented, decremented or set to a specific value by events in the system.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the counters and the page will dynamically update as the counters change state.

Clicking the Advanced Control button will open a window that contains control options to change the counter's value and a progress display.

The Increment and Decrement buttons will add or subtract 1 from the counter's current value, restricted by the counter's configured minimum and maximum value if applicable. The Manually Set Value button will set the counter value to the specified number, restricted to the range of the configured minimum and maximum value if applicable.

In most cases, counters are controlled by Automated Action programming when events occur in the system, e.g. a card being presented when a person enters a room may increase a counter by 1, and a person leaving a room may decrease it by 1. You would use the [Control Counters] page to manually adjust or reset a counter value if the counter's value has been changed inadvertently or is causing automation to function incorrectly.

This page is used to allow testing of inputs within areas, verifying that they are operating correctly. When active, input alarms will not be processed, allowing lesser used inputs like user duress or emergency buttons to be tested without sirens and alarms being reported.

Area walk tests are intended for routine maintenance of the system, allowing for periodic testing of the detectors and buttons within an area to confirm they are functioning correctly. Most importantly, this includes important but infrequently used inputs like medical, smoke or emergency buttons, as well as duress buttons.

To test an input, it must move from an active state to a secure state. This typically involves pressing a button or activating a detector and then returning it to a normal state. This also ensures that user duress or emergency buttons which require resetting are returned to a normal position before the test is completed.

While the test is active, normal active states are ignored by the system, allowing buttons that would generate an immediate alarm to be tested without a siren and alarm response. Tamper detection and other fault states will still be processed however, preventing the system from being modified while the test is active. It is recommended that walk tests be performed as part of regular system maintenance and that tests do not run for too long, as some emergency response components may not be available during the test.

The current state of a test can be seen from multiple browsers with state dynamically updated. This allows tests to be run on multiple areas at once and allows progress be seen on portable devices like a smartphone. As each input is successfully tested, they will automatically move from the Inputs to Test list to the Passed list. The walk test completes successfully when all inputs have been tested and have moved to the Passed list.

Tests can only be started when the area is disarmed and there is no active alarms in that area. Once started, a test will run indefinitely until it either successfully completes or is cancelled. A test can be manually cancelled at any time to return input processing back to normal and will automatically cancel if the area is armed while a walk test is running.
This page is used to backup/restore the Inception controllers database and default the Inception controller to factory settings.

The Inception Controller gives you the flexibility to configure the entire system from any device with a web browser, however, all the programming is held on the panel and not on the device you connect with.

The backup process provides you with a way to extract all the programming contained within the Inception Controller. It is recommended to perform backups regularly to prevent programming loss in case of an issue during firmware update, future configuration mistakes or for the rare chance of hardware failure. This may happen due to environmental factors such as power surges.

The restore process provides a way to roll back to a previous version of programming to undo unwanted changes. It also allows the Inception Controller to be replaced with another controller while keeping all the previously programmed functionality.

You can also perform a factory reset from this page, this will delete all programming currently on the Inception Controller and return it to factory default. This may be used if you were repurposing an Inception Controller.

Your web page profile will determine if you can perform each of the tasks on this page.
This page is used to display system status and system warnings.

The system overview page shows summary information about the Inception System. Any issues that could keep the system from running optimally are also listed here and will cause a warning or error to display in the notification bar at the top of the page. Any warnings should be rectified as soon as possible. These could be indicating issues like modules or peripherals are offline or have health issues like power problems, or that an alarm reporting path is offline. Other warnings like inputs that are permanently isolated or doors which can not lock due to a broken emergency breakglass are also shown here.

The Enable Detailed Events option in the toolbar allows verbose review messages and system log messages to be created. These additional events can be used to aid in diagnosing issues and identify unusual behaviour. It is recommended that this option is only enabled while diagnosing issues and is otherwise disabled, as the verbose review events are unsuitable for day to day usage.

The Download Programming Report option allows a commissioning report to be downloaded, which includes details about all configured options in the system. This report is useful as it gives a human readable hardcopy of all programming in the system, allowing easy reference in future about the programming at the end of commissioning. Clicking Download Programming Report will open a window where you can select the items the report will contain. Clicking the Create Report button on this window will save a text file to your device.

If requested by Inner Range technical support you may need to use the Download System Log button to download the system log. The system log is a password protected event log that is designed to be read by Inner Range technical support staff, for use in advanced troubleshooting.
This page is used to view and upgrade the Inception controllers firmware.

As bug fixes and additional features are added to the Inception Controller new firmware versions will be released. These firmware files will be available on the Inner Range website (www.innerrange.com). The firmware file is then copied to the Inception Controller and once uploaded, the upgrade process can begin. This can take some time, do not power down the Inception Controller during this upgrade.

It is recommended that a database backup is taken before firmware updates are applied as the database is automatically updated during the upgrade and won't be compatible in case there is a need to go back.
This page is used to view and upgrade the Inception modules firmware.

As bug fixes and additional features are added to the modules connected to the Inception Controller new firmware versions will be released. These firmware files will be available on the Inner Range website. (www.innerrange.com). The module firmware file is then copied to the Inception Controller and once uploaded, the upgrade process can begin. One or more modules can be selected to be upgraded simultaneously, allowing the upgrade to be done in batches if on a live site. This can take some time, do not power down the Inception Controller or the module during this upgrade.

Peripherals such as Unibus modules and SIFER readers can also be updated in this way. When updating peripherals, the module or modules they are connected to is selected, which means peripheral updating can not be done individually and instead must be done on a per-module basis.

This page is used to enable, update or disable Service Mode. Service mode is used to disable area alarm processing, sirens, alarm reporting and door DOTL feedback during the initial setup and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.

Service Mode is enabled by default during the initial setup of an Inception Controller, the commissioning checklist provides a step to disable service mode.

When enabling service mode for maintenance of the Inception System, certain features like the processing of area alarms, ability to sound sirens or report alarms and door DOTL feedback can be optionally disabled. This allows the system to be safely interacted with, without sirens sounding and alarms being reported due to cabinet tampers and other states.

The disabled features can be optionally re-enabled too if they require testing. This could, for example, allow area alarm processing to be enabled without sirens or alarm reporting to perform dry-runs of the security area.

The service mode can only be enabled for a limited amount of time, ensuring that the system will revert back to its normal operation and can't be accidentally left in service mode. Disabling service mode can also be done with a delay allowing the completion of tasks such as closing cabinets after disconnecting the ethernet or wifi adapter without generating tamper alarms.

This page is used to view entities and their details that normally would be acquired through REST API requests. This page can be used as a quick reference to acquire details required to make other REST API requests without having to do other ones first to acquire IDs and other required details.
This page is used to configure the date and time settings of the Inception Controller

It is important that the Inception controller is set to the correct date and time to ensure that time periods and calendar events occur at the correct time. Since time periods can be used for user permissions and automation there could be problems if the controller time was out even by the slightest amount. You may have programmed a door to unlock at 8am every day, if the controller time is incorrect the door will not behave as expected. If the date is set incorrectly then time periods that should be active during the week may incorrectly become active on the weekends instead and unlock doors when no one is onsite.

The system can be configured to sync with an NTP server, either from the internet or one locally on site. Using this with the time zone configured ensures the date and time is kept correct. Daylight savings changes are automatically handled by the selected timezone.

If no NTP server exists, either due to no internet access or none existing on site, the date and time can be updated manually.

Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit Date and Time settings.
This page is used to configure the network settings of the Inception Controller

The only method for connection to the web interface is via a wired or wireless network. The default network settings are configured for wired Ethernet using DHCP, this means that if the Inception Controller is connected to an existing network it will automatically be assigned an IP address. This page allows you to configure the additional setting for the Ethernet connection and even enable Wi-Fi or allow access to the web interface via Skytunnel.

If the Inception Controller is connected to a network that does not support DHCP addressing, you may need to manually configure network settings such as the controller's IP address and the subnet mask.

You can also connect to the Inception Controller using a wireless network if you have purchased the Inception Wireless Adapter. You can configure the Wi-Fi connection in two different ways, wireless hotspot or wireless network. The wireless hotspot allows you to connect your laptop, tablet or phone directly to the Inception Controller on a private network using only the wireless adapter. This allows connection to the Inception web interface without connecting your device to the site's network.

The Inception Wireless Adapter can also be used to wirelessly connect the Inception Controller to the site's wireless network. This will allow a network connected laptop, tablet or phone to connect to the Inception Controller. The Wi-Fi connection supports WPA2 personal and enterprise authentication.

The Inception controller can also utilize the Inner Range SkyTunnel architecture. SkyTunnel is a hassle free connection method that requires an internet connection for the Inception Controller and the connecting device. SkyTunnel is commonly used when access to the Inception Controller is desired via the Internet, for example, a user accessing the Inception web interface from a mobile phone in a different location. Use of the SkyTunnel architecture for internet access requires a paid subscription or an active alarm monitoring account.

Your web page profile will determine if you are restricted to only view or view and edit network settings.

This page would commonly be used by an installer during the installation or ongoing maintenance of the Inception System. If incorrectly configured, you will not be able to connect to the web interface to change the programming. You will need correct the programming from an LCD Terminal via [MENU] [5] [2] or by factory defaulting the Inception Controller.
This page is used to create and edit notifiers that allow users to receive messages when various system events occur.

Notifiers are configured with a list of recipient users to send messages to, along with an optional schedule for when messages are allowed to be sent. Multiple notifiers can be created, each with different schedules and user recipients and different items to notify on. Notifiers can be configured, for example, to send notifications whenever an alarm occurs in a particular area or access to a particular door is granted or denied. Notifications can also be sent when repeated failed login attempts are made on the web interface or LCD Terminal, or if programming changes are made to particular entities. Maintenance events, such as power and battery issues of modules or RF devices can also be communicated.

Notifications can be sent to users via email with clear, easy to read language. To use the email notification path, an email server must be configured on the [Configuration > General > System] page. The users specified as recipients also require an email address assigned to them via the Email Address option on the [Configuration > Users > Manage Users] page. When multiple events need to be sent to the same user, they are consolidated into a single message to help prevent issues with the configured mail server or filling the user's mailbox. See the tooltip on the Rate Limit Time option on the [Configuration > General > System] page for more information.

Area notifications allow messages to be sent whenever arm or disarm attempts are made, or when alarm events occur. In addition, area arm and disarm validation can also be configured, with messages being sent if someone arms or disarms an area at the wrong time, or the area is not armed or disarmed when it should be. Specific input event types like alarm or duress events can also be chosen for monitoring, or events like low battery for RF devices can be sent to indicate potential maintenance is required. All areas in the system can be monitored, or specific areas can be manually chosen.

Door events allow monitoring of failed or successful user access attempts through doors. Messages can also be sent when doors are left open too long or if the door is overridden from it's default behaviour. Similar to areas, all doors in the system can be monitored or specific doors can be manually chosen.

Live state of the system can be monitored, with messages being sent if hardware modules fall offline, experience power issues or are tampered with. Messages can be sent if the system resets with the reason why, as users login to the web interface, make configuration changes within the system or many others.

Your web page profile will determine if you can create or modify the items on this page.
This page is used to configure various system settings such as the user PIN length, duress user, end of line config and battery testing

The user PIN length can be configured with a minimum and a maximum length, this length requirement is used when manually entering a PIN number for a user or when a pin number is automatically generated for a user. Both of the methods for assigning PINs to a user are done from the [Configuration > Manage User] page.

If users will be controlling areas or outputs from an LCD terminal, it is important to make sure the user PIN length is long enough that it is unlikely the user PIN's could be guessed. A longer PIN number combined with the Max Login Attempts setting for the LCD terminal will make it very unlikely that a person could guess a PIN number. The Max Login Attempts is configured in the [Configuration > Hardware] page for each of the LCD terminals.

The Duress User feature is used when a person is placed in a duress situation and is forced to login to an LCD terminal to control an area or output. If the Duress User has been enabled and a security PIN has been assigned, the user can login with this PIN instead of their own. Once logged in as the Duress User, the Duress input for the LCD Terminal will go into the alarm state, which will be reported to the monitoring station if alarm monitoring is configured without sounding any alarms. Alarm monitoring is configured from the [Configuration > General > Alarm Reporting] page.

Most security systems use End of Line resistors to determine if an input is in the alarm, tamper or sealed state. Inner Range products have historically used 2k2 and 6k8 or 2k2 and 2k2 ohm resistor pairs, however the Inception Controller and expansion modules can be configured to use other End of Line resistance values. This allows a security system from a different manufacturer to be easily replaced by an Inception Controller without changing the End of Line resistors found on every detection device.

Battery testing is required to ensure that the in the event of AC failure, each module can be powered from its attached battery. Testing should be done regularly to ensure that if a battery fails over time it is detected. For a module to be included in the battery test it will need to have a power supply connected, battery connected and battery testing enabled on the [Configuration > Hardware] page. The duration of the battery test is set on a per module basis, on the [Configuration > Hardware] page. The length of the battery test will depend on the average current consumption of the module and the capacity of the connected battery.

Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit System Settings.
This page is used to view the licensed feature summary and license keys for this Inception system.

This page can be used to apply license keys and fetch the latest license key information about the system. The latest licensing information is gathered from license.innerrange.com. The latest information is fetched daily, but can be done early by pressing the Update Licenses button in the toolbar. If there is a missing license due to a lack of a connection to Inner Range's licensing service, pressing the Apply Key button in the toolbar will allow you to apply a key manually.

The feature summary will display all possible licensed features and their current availability based on the associated license keys for the system.

The license keys section will show an independent breakdown of associated license keys and which feature the license key corresponds to. If the license has an expiration date, it will be displayed as well.
This page is used to configure alarm reporting.

The primary purpose of an intrusion detection system is to identify that an intruder has entered a building. The use of sirens, siren outputs and strobes are to ensure attention is drawn to the site and the intruder leaves the site quickly. In most cases, the intrusion detection is paired with alarm monitoring to report events to a monitoring station. The monitoring station will process the events according to their policies.

With analogue phone lines becoming obsolete the Inception controller provides 2 alternatives for alarm reporting.
