  • Skytunnel Reporting - This uses the Inception Controllers network connection with internet access to communicate with the SkyTunnel server. SkyTunnel is a cloud-based service that links two outbound connections, which means that you will not require port forwarding. Once the event is received by the SkyTunnel server it is delivered to a monitoring station. This works out of the box and requires no other specialized hardware.
  • T4000 Reporting - The T4000 is an Inner Range device that connects to the Inception Controller via USB. It provides multiple redundant reporting paths using multiple 3G networks in addition to ethernet internet reporting. With all paths polled frequently, this ensures reliable alarm monitoring.

Both reporting paths can be configured for Periodic Test Reports. These test reports ensure that communication to the monitoring station occurs successfully in the absence of alarm events.

The Inception Controller supports both of these reporting paths at the same time to provide redundancy. You will need to select a primary and backup path based on your preferred method of communication.

Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit settings on this page.
This page is used to create and edit time periods.

Time periods are items that change between active and inactive based on the current date and time. Time periods can be used to represent certain business rules and allow for things within the system to be scheduled, such as restricting when a user is allowed to do certain actions or automatically unlocking a door or arming an area.

Time periods are broken up into two sections. First are the weekly recurring times, where multiple time blocks across multiple days can be configured. These time blocks apply every single week to the time period, causing it to become active whenever the current day and time matches a time block. This could allow Monday to Friday, 9am to 5pm to be configured, and every week the time period would be active between those times. The second section contains the exceptions to the rule, which can override the weekly recurring times.

The first exception type is calendar exceptions. Calendars are focused on dates rather than days of the week and provide alternative start and end times to the regular recurring times of time periods for the dates defined within the calendar. Calendar exceptions are used when the same time change for a day should be repeated on multiple days throughout the year. The calendar can be updated, which could affect multiple time periods in a consistent way. A common use case example for calendar exceptions are extended trading hour days, where trading hours change consistently on many days throughout the year. The calendar in that case could apply to multiple time periods, like working hours, automatic door times and automatic arm times, each reacting to the same calendar. A user can then update the calendar throughout the year and the system reacts correctly. Another example is public holidays, a calendar can include holidays that that always fall on the same day each year, such as Christmas and New Year’s Eve and have them repeat, as well as being manually updated each year with holidays that change like Easter. The calendar exception could prevent the time period from being valid on a particular day and as such the normal operations would not apply for such things as user permissions or door free access times.

Time periods also offer another way to apply conditional rules, which is to add exceptions directly to the time period. These exceptions are similar to calendar exceptions, and ultimately achieve the same outcome, but are intended to be used in instances where a single exception may apply such as shutting for maintenance for a period of time or a brief period of extended trading hours.

In most circumstances exceptions and calendar exceptions will offer similar functionality, the major differences are that calendars can be programmed once and applied to many time periods, which saves programming time. Calendar exceptions also provided calculated dates, which offers much greater flexibility when trying to achieve otherwise difficult exception rules.

Note that you are not restricted in using one or the other of these exception settings, it's perfectly fine to have the time period use both exceptions and calendar exceptions. Overall, both exception types allow temporary modifications to be made to the time period, potentially well in advance, without needing to alter the standard weekly recurring times.

This page also provides a preview of the time period, this preview can be used to view any date in the future and will display how the time period would have state on that day, taking into account any calendar exceptions or date & time exceptions. This is useful to confirm whether exceptions planned for the future have been applied correctly.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system. Due to the fact that time periods are heavily used in user permissions and automation it is recommended that permissions for this page be limited to the installer or the system administrator.

Your ability to view or view and edit time periods from this page is based on your webpage profile.
This page is used to create and edit recurring calendar dates which are used as exceptions to the normal operation of time periods.

Calendars can be applied to time periods as calendar exceptions. Calendars are focused on dates rather than days of the week and provide alternative start and end times to the regular recurring times of time periods for the dates defined within the calendar. Calendar exceptions are used when the same time change for a day should be repeated on multiple days throughout the year. The calendar can be updated, which could affect multiple Time Periods in a consistent way.

A common use case example for calendar exceptions are extended trading hour days, where trading hours change consistently on many days throughout the year. The calendar, in that case, could apply to multiple time periods, like working hours, automatic door times and automatic area arm times, each reacting to the same calendar. A user can then update the calendar throughout the year and the system reacts correctly. Another example is public holidays, a calendar can include holidays that always fall on the same day each year, such as Christmas and New Year’s Eve and have them repeat, as well as being manually updated each year with holidays that change like Easter. The calendar exception could prevent the time period from being valid on a particular day and as such the normal operations would not apply to such things as user permissions or door free access times.

Calendars can be programmed once and applied to many time periods, which saves programming time.

Your ability to view or view and edit calendar dates from this page is based on your web page profile.
This page is used to test the inputs and outputs of each module individually.

This page will dynamically display the state of all the configured inputs and outputs allowing the installer to do a physical inspection or walk test. This page provides control of outputs without needing the output set as User Controllable or the having the output assigned to the user permissions.

When this page is first opened, all the current states for the inputs and outputs on the selected module are displayed and as they change state the page will update. The input or output state can easily be identified by the colour and the text description in the State column. The outputs can be controlled by clicking the On and Off buttons.

To test a module, select it from the module list, the page will update to display the current state of all the configured inputs and outputs for the module. If there are inputs or outputs missing from this list they have not been configured in the [Configuration > Hardware] page. The output test will allow you to control an output to confirm it has been wired correctly and is working as expected. The input test displays all the states that the inputs change to since the module was selected. This can be used to walk test all the inputs for a particular module.

Please note: This page is only intended to be used by an installer to test the installation of inputs and outputs. Controlling outputs directly could override behaviour, like a door that is considered locked could have it's lock output turned on. It is important to return any controlled outputs to their original state, or interact with the item that should control the outputs state, like unlocking the door.
This page is used to generate various types of reports which contain information about the system.

Each report can be customized to show specific information, for example, when running the Access History report, you may wish to only include certain Doors or Users, or set the included timeframe of the results to be between 1 month ago and now.

Multiple file formats are available for report generation: PDF outputs a single pdf which should be sufficient for most cases, however, extremely large reports (i.e. 1000+ pages) are not able to be generated in a single PDF file due to system limitations. If this is the case, you can either try to reduce the size of the report data by filtering it to only include specific users or items, or choose a different file format option. The HTML format option is ideal for viewing the report data directly in a web browser, and is not subject to the size limitations of the PDF option. The Multi-PDF (zipped) format option outputs a series of individual PDF documents packed together for download in a single ZIP file, and is also not subject to the size limitations of the PDF option.

After the Report Parameters have been specified for the desired Report Type, click the Create Report button to download a report file from the Inception System containing the desired information in the selected file format.
This page is used to create and edit scheduled tasks to perform regular system operations automatically.

Scheduled tasks are items that automatically execute system tasks on a specific schedule, and then send the results to the user(s) who require them. For example, you could use a scheduled task to schedule a database backup every 2 weeks on a Sunday night at 11:00pm, and have the backup file emailed to the system administrator.

Scheduled tasks consist of 3 main sections:

  • Schedule - When and how often the task should be executed.
  • Action to Perform - What task to carry out in order to produce a result.
  • Results - What to do with the task's results once it runs successfully.

    • Scheduled tasks can have their schedules set to Basic, Advanced, or None. A Basic schedule allows you to specify a frequency (daily, weekly, etc.), a day of the week, and a time of day at which to run the task. This method is best suited for when you just want to run a task at a set interval and don't need precise control over which weeks or months it runs during. An Advanced schedule allows you to specify a list of entries that consist of a Calendar and a time of day to run. With this method, the task will run on every day that one of the referenced calendars are active on, at the corresponding time of day. This method is suited for when more precise control over the task's execution time is required, for example: when you want to run a task at 9:00am every first Sunday of the month, and also at 10am on every public holiday. For more information on how calendar items work and how to configure them, see the [Configuration > Scheduling > Calendars] page. A schedule set to None means the task will not automatically execute itself at regular intervals, but doesn't necessarily mean that it is disabled entirely, as it can still be executed manually by users and triggered by automated actions. For example, a scheduled task could be configured to send a user location report as an email attachment, that is triggered by automated action when an emergency alarm occurs.

    Scheduled tasks with Basic and Advanced schedules will remember their next scheduled run time even if the Inception device loses power, so that when power is restored, tasks that missed their scheduled run time will be immediately executed as soon as possible.

    There are currently two types of Actions to Perform that Scheduled Tasks support, Database Backup and Create Report. Database Backup backs up the current contents of the Inception database and produces a backup file that can be used to restore the database to that point in time (same as the file produced by the Download Backup button on the [System > Backup / Restore] page). Create Report allows you to configure a report to be automatically generated by the system at regular intervals. Any type of report that can be requested on the [System > Reports] page can also be produced by a scheduled task.

    After the task has completed successfully, it needs a way of handling the results so that they can be viewed by users who are interested in them. The results can be sent via email attachment, or copied to a USB drive attached to the Inception controller.

    To send results via email attachment, select the Send as Email Attachment option from the How to Handle Results control. This option allows you to select a list of Users in the Inception System to email the results to. Sending results via email requires that the email server configuration is correctly set up on the [Configuration > General > System] page. Users who will be receiving emails must also have their email address configured on the [Configuration > Users > Manage Users] page, in the [Credentials] section.

    To copy results to an attached USB drive, select the Copy to Attached USB Drive option from the How to Handle Results control. With this method selected, the task will copy the resulting file to the root folder of the USB drive after it completes.

    To copy results to a shared network folder, select the Copy to Network Share option from the How to Handle Results control. This option allows you to specify a network share location, a domain name, and the domain user credentials to use when copying the output file to the network share location.

    Scheduled tasks can be immediately executed at any time by pressing the Control button available in the toolbox. Pressing the Run Task Immediately button on the dialog will manually run the task which can be helpful for testing the task. This requires the currently logged in user to have permission to control scheduled tasks via Web Page Permissions.

    Scheduled tasks can fail in certain cases, either due to not being able to complete the task, or not being able to successfully handle the results. If a task fails, it will generate a system message notifying you about what task failed, when it failed, and the reason for failure.
This page is used to configure the Inner Range Mobile Access connection settings, connecting to a corresponding Mobile Access system using a pairing code generated from the Mobile Access portal.

To link these two systems, first navigate to https://mobileaccess.innerrange.cloud/, create a new system, navigate to the Integration page, then generate a pairing code. On this page of the Inception system, click the Link System button in the toolbox and enter the pairing code that appears. Once the systems are linked, no further configuration is required, and mobile credentials can be enrolled for each user on the Edit Users page and selecting Allocate Mobile Credential.

The Inception system can be unlinked from the Mobile Access servers by clicking the Unlink button in the toolbox. While unlinked, new mobile credentials cannot be allocated, however existing mobile credentials will continue to function. Upon relinking of the system back to the same mobile access system, new mobile credentials can then be allocated.

The current number of Mobile Credential licenses used and total available can be viewed by clicking the Licensing button in the toolbox. For further licensing information, refer to the Licensing page ([Configuration > General > Licensing]).
This page is used to search for mobile credentials in the Inception system based on details like their card number, status, linked user, etc.

The Mobile Credential search table displays all the mobile credentials in the system in a format that enables you to sort and filter the data to see specific mobile credentials that you are interested in. The search boxes above the table header for each column allow you to search for matches in individual fields. You can also combine multiple search filters to get more specific results.

The Column visibility button allows you to selectively show and hide mobile credential fields. Not all fields are visible by default, so you may find it useful to enable visibility for other fields that you want to be included in search results, as hidden fields are not searched by the Search All Fields text box.

The Mobile Credential search table can be sorted by a specific column by clicking on that column's header to toggle sorting order between ascending and descending.

Clicking the Configure button on a mobile credential row will open a Manage Credential dialog for that specific mobile credential, allowing you to quickly perform actions such as resending the invitation code to the linked user, revoking the credential, or deleting the credential.

Column visibility options set through the Column visibility button are saved system-wide, and can be reset to the default visibility options with the Reset visibility button.

NOTE: Any changes made to mobile credentials after the page has loaded will not be reflected in the search results until the page is reloaded.
This page is used to monitor configured wireless devices. To control a device you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page lists wireless inputs and remote fobs. This page will dynamically display the state of wireless devices on the Inception Controller.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the devices and the page will dynamically update as the devices change state. The device state can easily be identified by the colour of the device and the text description in the Status column.

Clicking the Re-Pair button for an offline or unpaired device that can be paired will initiate a re-pairing process with the associated hardware module.
This page is used to configure and control IR Video connections to third party systems, utilising supported Inception integrations.

IR VIdeo Connections allow the Inception's functionality to be extended beyond the typical intruder and access control functionality already provided, providing rich integration into external systems. One such integration that Inception supports is Inner Range's own 'IR Video' range, allowing the Inception to connect to, monitor, and control IR Video NVRs and cameras.

Integrations are represented in Inception as two different components: IR VIdeo Connections and Cameras. The Inception's connection to a third-party device, be it an NVR, camera, or some other device, is stored as an IR VIdeo Connection. A Refresh Devices can then be performed against this connection to populate all devices linked to this connection. For example, if connecting to an IR Video NVR, an IR Video Connection is configured with the NVR's details, and then a Refresh Devices will pull in all cameras connected to the NVR and populate them in the Inception system as Cameras. The NVR itself would also be confingured as an Cameras, allowing for its state to also be monitored.
This page is used to configure cameras that have been fetched from an IR Video connection.

Cameras can be fetched from an IR Videos Connection by performing a Refresh Devices on the Integrated Connections page. From this page, Cameras can be managed and deleted as necessary, however they cannot be added. Each Cameras has a reference to its Parent Connection, as well as a button that directly links to that IR Video Connection.

If the Cameras supports PTZ, then two additional properties will be displayed in the editor: Supported PTZ Presets and Supported PTZ Tours. These values are provided by the Cameras and reflect what's been configured on the third-party system. If these presets are changed on the third-party system, a Refresh Devices will need to be performed on the parent IR Video Connection

Pulse States

Pulse state conditions are for moment-in-time events and are instantly set to 'true' then 'false' when the event occurs. Normal state conditions in contrast will transition to be 'true' upon meeting the condition and will stay 'true' until the condition stops being met.

For example, 'Detecting Input Activity' for an area stays true while any input is active, preventing further triggers if one input remains active. 'Detecting New Input Activity (pulsed)' will trigger briefly for each new input activation, allowing actions like timed lighting control based on user movement. Generally, pulse conditions are used to trigger a set of actions when the event occurs.

Pre-arm Isolation Failed
There are one or more system messages that must be acknowledged before the area can be armed. Acknowledge the messages before trying the area arm again.

If the issue preventing arming can not be resolved, the issue can be temporarily overridden with permission from the ARC or alarm company. If permission is granted, an Anti-Code is provided to allow temporary overriding of the issue.

Change the state of areas using the buttons below. This will only work for areas that you have permission to control.

Arm
Change the state of areas to Armed.

Arm With Exit Delay
Change the state of areas to Armed while utilising the designated Exit Delay to allow people time to vacate the area before it is armed.

Disarm
Change the state of areas to Disarmed.

Arm Mode - For use on areas with multi-mode arming enabled, ignored otherwise. Full Area arms the whole area. Perimeter Mode arms the perimeter, with entry delay when someone opens a door. Night Mode also arms the perimeter, with instant alarm if any perimeter input activates.

Change the state of counters using the buttons below.

Increment by 1
Increases the counter's value by 1.

Decrement by 1
Decreases the counter's value by 1.

Manually Set Value
Sets the counter's value to the given number.

Change the state of doors using the buttons below. This will only work for doors that you have permission to control, and that are able to perform the designated action.

Unlock
Change the state of doors to Unlocked. The doors will remain unlocked until the overrides are cleared.

Lock
Change the state of doors to Locked. The doors will remain locked until the overrides are cleared.

Lockout
Change the state of doors to Locked Out - this locks the doors and prevents cards and PINs from unlocking them.

Clear
Revert the state of doors to their default state by clearing any overrides.

Grant Access
Unlock doors for the designated Door Unlock Time.

Change the state of floors using the buttons below. This will only work for floors that you have permission to control.

Free Access
Change the state of floors to Free Access, allowing buttons in lift cars that go to the floor to be selected without needing a credential. The floors will remain in free access until the overrides are cleared.

Secure
Change the state of floors to Secure, where credentials are required to access the floors. The floors will remain secured until the overrides are cleared.

Lockout
Change the state of floors to Locked Out. This secures the floors and prevents cards and PINs from enabling buttons that go to the floor.

Clear
Revert the state of floors to their default state by clearing any overrides.
Free Access
Secure
Change the state of the selected input using the buttons below.

Isolate
Isolate the input. This will allow users to arm any areas that this input is assigned to regardless of whether the input is in the alarm state.

De-Isolate
De-Isolate the input. This will return the input to it's usual behaviour and prevent the arming of any areas that this input is assigned to if the input is in the alarm state.
Isolate
De-Isolate
Soak Test Duration
This dialog allows for manual commands to be sent to Integrated Devices, allowing direct control or intervention where required.

Send to PTZ Preset
If supported, the Integrated Device will move the specified preset.

Control PTZ Tour
If supported, the Integrated Device will start/stop the specified tour, based on whether Start is ticked or not.

Control Physical Output
Turns the specified on or off, depending on the value of the Set checkbox.

Control Night Mode
Sets the Integrated Devices Day/Night Mode setting.

Control White Light Mode
Turn On/Off the Integrated Devices White Light Mode setting for the time specified in White Light Duration filed.

Control Audio Alarm
Sets the Integrated Devices Audio Alarm setting. Audio file spcified in Audio File ID will be played. Number of times this Alarm will be played based on the Cycle field value.

Control Manual Record
Sets the Integrated Devices Manual Record Start/Stop setting.

Change the state of the selected module using the buttons below.

Isolate
Isolates the module. This causes the system to ignore any health issues that this module could report, which could possibly prevent area arming, cause sirens to sound and be reported to monitoring stations. Used primarily when issues have been identified with the module that will take some time to fix. If an issue is expected to last for an extended period of time, it may be better to edit the module on the [Configuration > Hardware] page and stop the system from monitoring that item.

De-Isolate
De-Isolates the module. This causes the system to resume monitoring the module's health, processing and reporting issues if any exist.
Change the state of the selected output using the buttons below. This will only work for outputs that you have permission to control.

On
Turn the selected output on.

Off
Turn the selected output off.

Control for Time
Select a time to apply to the on/off controls.
Control for Time
On
Off
Control Scheduled Tasks using the buttons below. This will only work for scheduled tasks that you have permission to control.

Run Task Immediately
Execute the scheduled task immediately.
Run Task Immediately
Change the state of storage units using the buttons below. This will only work for storage units that you have permission to control.

Unlock
Change the state of units to Unlocked, allowing them to be accessed.

Secure
Change the state of units to Secure, enabling input alarm monitoring.

Vacant
Change the state of units to Vacant. Vacancy allows free access to the unit and will remain until it is cleared.

Clear Vacancy
Removes the Vacancy state for the unit if it is active.

Note: Vacancy control requires permissions to be granted via Permission Groups.
Vacant
Clear Vacancy
Forgive User Anti-Passback violations or user login lockouts using the buttons below.

Users forgiven from an anti-passback violation will be able to move to another area if their permissions allow, even if it will violate Anti-Passback. Allows the user to correct themself if Anti-Passback rules are preventing them from moving.

Forgive Users with Violations
Forgives all users who have an unresolved Anti-Passback violation.

Forgive All Users
Grants all users an Anti-Passback exemption, regardless of whether they have an unresolved Anti-Passback violation.

Forgiving user lockouts will lift all current lockouts for all users, giving them another chance to login after having exceeded the threshold of failed login attempts.

End All User Login Lockouts
Lifts all lockouts (that occur on too many failed login attempts) for all users with a same or lower priority ranking.
Forgive Anti-Passback Violations
Forgive Users with Violations
Forgive All Users
Forgive User Lockout
End All User Login Lockouts
Inputs and Outputs
The table below is used to configure inputs for the Inception Controller.

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the Inception Controller. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the controller.

Behaviour - Each input hardware point on the controller can have its functionality configured. Although traditionally these have only been able to be used for the purpose labelled on the module, Inception gives you the flexibility to define the purpose per input. EOL resistors are required for all input behaviours except REX/REN and Generic Switch/Button.

  • Not Wired - This is the default behaviour for an input, and if not changed the input will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
  • Reed - Configures the input as a reed switch and once set you will need to link this input to a door. This input behaviour allows the system to know if a door is currently open or closed. The system can use this state to determine if a user went through a door after requesting access and, along with the state of the door lock output, determine if the door has been held open too long (DOTL) or has been forced open.
  • Tongue/Bond Sense - Configures the input as a tongue/bond sense and once set you will need to link this input to a door. The tongue/bond sense provides similar functionality as the reed above, allowing knowledge of whether the door is closed securely.
  • REX / REN - Configures the input as a request to exit or request to enter button and once set you will need to link this input to a door. This input would normally have a button wired that when shorted will cause the linked door to unlock temporarily. This input should not have EOL resistors.
  • Breakglass - Configures the input as a breakglass and once set you will need to link this input to a door. Emergency breakglass is often wired directly into the door's power circuit, allowing the door to be unlocked in case of emergency. This however bypasses the lock output state so even if the controller says the door is locked, the door can actually be opened. By wiring the breakglass detector into the system and linking it to a door, the door will be marked as unlocked and identify the cause as the breakglass. A warning is also created, prompting that the breakglass should be fixed and that the door is not secure.
  • Generic Detector - Configures the input as a standard security detector input such as PIR. Once this behaviour is selected you will not be able to link this input to a door. Instead, this is just a normal EOL input that can be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes.
  • Generic Switch/Button - Configures the input as a standard input for buttons or switches such as a duress point. Once this behaviour is selected you will not be able to link this input to a door. Instead, this is just a normal two state switch or button input that can be used for security or as a trigger for an automated action.
  • Reader Tamper - Configures the input as a reader tamper input and once set you will need to link this input to a door. This input would normally be connected to the readers tamper wire to indicate when the reader has been tampered with. By using this behaviour, the module will monitor the reader for tampers and if detected, the module will report the alarm if alarm reporting is configured. This input should not be wired with EOL resistors and assumes that the tamper type is normally open. If the tamper type is actually normally closed, the Swap Active and Secure States option can be set for this input on the [Configuration > Inputs > Hardware Inputs] page. If EOL resistors are desired, the Generic Detector option above can be selected and the input can be manually monitored.

Linked Door - If the behaviour of the input is set to an access control function such as door reed, tongue/bond sense, REX, REN or break glass, then the linked door option must be set. The linked door is used to determine which door should be affected by the information from the input. The linked door option is not available if the input behaviour is set to generic detectors or generic switch/buttons as these behaviours do not relate to access control.

Name - The input will automatically be named based on the name of the controller and the input's behaviour. If entering a name it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the input behaviour and the linked door.

The table below is used to configure the outputs that are not controlling door locks for the Inception Controller.

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the Inception Controller. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the controller.

Behaviour - Each output hardware point on the controller can have its function configured. Although these have been traditionally only been able to be used for the purpose labelled on the module, Inception gives you the flexibility to define the purpose of each output.

  • Not Wired - This is the default behaviour for an output, and if not changed the output will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
  • Held (DOTL) - Configures the output as a DOTL output, if the door is held open for longer than the Door Held Time Limit the output will turn on. This can be used to sound a buzzer or turn on a light to indicate that the door should be closed. Once set, you will need to link this output to a door. Note: For the door to generate a Held (DOTL) event the door must be linked to an input that is configured as a door reed or tongue sense.
  • Valid - The output will turn on for several seconds when the door unlocks or a valid card or PIN is presented at a reader linked to the door. The output will also turn on briefly if an area on either side of the door arms successfully. Once set you will need to link this output to a door. The valid output can be wired to the valid wire on a Wiegand reader or to an indicator of your choice.
  • Invalid - The output will turn on for several seconds when an invalid card or pin is presented at a reader linked to the door. Once set you will need to link this output to a door. The invalid output can be wired to the invalid wire on Wiegand readers or to an indicator of your choice.
  • Beeper - The beeper is generally connected to an output that provides an audible tone such as the beeper in a reader. The output will be pulsed on and off for one of the following reasons:
  • Door Unlock - two short beeps.
  • Additional Credentials Required - two long beeps.
  • Door Access Denied - on for three seconds.
  • Arm Success (One of the areas on a side of the door armed) - 3 quick beeps.
  • Arm Failure (One of the areas on a side of the door failed to arm) - On for 3 seconds.
  • Area State Notification (Area in Arm Warning, Entry or Exit) - Continuous beeping
  • Generic Output - Configures the output on the controller as a general purpose output that is not related to access control. This output behaviour allows the output to be used for intruder detection purposes such as a strobe or siren output, or for automation such as lighting control.
  • Follows Door State - The output will match the state of the door lock output. Can be wired into a light to indicate whether the door is locked or unlocked. The output will be on if the door is unlocked.
  • Inside Area State - The output will match the state of the area on the inside of the door. Can be wired into a light to indicate whether the area is locked or unlocked. The output will be on if the area is armed.
  • Outside Area State - The output will match the state of the area on the outside of the door. Can be wired into a light to indicate whether the area is locked or unlocked. The output will be on if the area is armed.

Linked Door - If the behaviour of the output is set to an access control function such as DOTL, valid, invalid or beeper, then the linked door option must be set. The linked door option is used to determine which door the outputs are associated with and controlled by. The linked door option is not available if the output behaviour is set to generic output as this does not relate to access control.

Name - The output will automatically be named based on the name of the controller and the output's behaviour. If entering a name it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the module, output behaviour and linked door.
The table below is used to configure the outputs that are not controlling door locks for the Standard LAN Access Module.

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the Standard LAN Access Module. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the SLAM.

Behaviour - Each output hardware point on the SLAM can have its function configured. Although these have been traditionally only been able to be used for the purpose labelled on the module, Inception gives you the flexibility to define the purpose of each output.

  • Not Wired - This is the default behaviour for an output, and if not changed the output will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
  • Held (DOTL) - Configures the output as a DOTL output, if the door is held open for longer than the Door Held Time Limit the output will turn on. This can be used to sound a buzzer or turn on a light to indicate that the door should be closed. Once set, you will need to link this output to a door. Note: For the door to generate a Held (DOTL) event the door must be linked to an input that is configured as a door reed or tongue sense.
  • Valid - The output will turn on for several seconds when the door unlocks or a valid card or PIN is presented at a reader linked to the door. The output will also turn on briefly if an area on either side of the door arms successfully. Once set you will need to link this output to a door. The valid output can be wired to the valid wire on a Wiegand reader or to an indicator of your choice.
  • Invalid - The output will turn on for several seconds when an invalid card or pin is presented at a reader linked to the door. Once set you will need to link this output to a door. The invalid output can be wired to the invalid wire on Wiegand readers or to an indicator of your choice.
  • Beeper - The beeper is generally connected to an output that provides an audible tone such as the beeper in a reader. The output will be pulsed on and off for one of the following reasons:
  • Door Unlock - two short beeps.
  • Additional Credentials Required - two long beeps.
  • Door Access Denied - on for three seconds.
  • Arm Success (One of the areas on a side of the door armed) - 3 quick beeps.
  • Arm Failure (One of the areas on a side of the door failed to arm) - On for 3 seconds.
  • Area State Notification (Area in Arm Warning, Entry or Exit) - Continuous beeping
  • Generic Output - Configures the output on the SLAM as a general purpose output that is not related to access control. This output behaviour allows the output to be used for intruder detection purposes such as a strobe or siren output, or for automation such as lighting control. Note: The SLAM outputs are limited in the amount of current they can switch, please refer to the SLAM installation manual for the contact ratings.
  • Follows Door State - The output will match the state of the door lock output. Can be wired into a light to indicate whether the door is locked or unlocked. The output will be on if the door is unlocked.
  • Inside Area State - The output will match the state of the area on the inside of the door. Can be wired into a light to indicate whether the area is locked or unlocked. The output will be on if the area is armed.
  • Outside Area State - The output will match the state of the area on the outside of the door. Can be wired into a light to indicate whether the area is locked or unlocked. The output will be on if the area is armed.

Linked Door - If the behaviour of the output is set to an access control function such as DOTL, valid, invalid or beeper, then the linked door option must be set. The linked door option is used to determine which door the outputs are associated with and controlled by. The linked door option is not available if the output behaviour is set to generic output as this does not relate to access control.

Name - The output will automatically be named based on the name of the Standard LAN Access Module and the output's behaviour. If entering a name it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the module, output behaviour and linked door.

The table below is used to configure inputs for the Inception Controller

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the Inception Controller. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the Inception Controller.

Behaviour - Each input hardware point on the Inception Controller can have its functionality set one of the three options below.

  • Not Wired - This is the default behaviour for an input and left unchanged the input will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
  • Generic Detector - If selected the input will function as a standard security detector input such as a PIR. This input will require the detection device to be wired with EOL resistors to allow the system to identify alarm, seal and tamper states. Once configured, this input needs to be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes or used as a trigger for automation.
  • Generic Switch/Button * If selected the input will function as a standard input for buttons or switches such as a duress point. This input will not require EOL resistors and it is just a normal two state switch or button input. Once configured, this input needs to be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes or used as a trigger for automation.

Name - The input will automatically be named based on the name of the Inception Controller and the input's behaviour. If entering a name, it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the hardware point and the device that is connected.
The table below is used to configure inputs for the 8 Input Expander

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the 8 Input Expander Module. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the 8 Input Expander.

Behaviour - Each hardware point on the 8 Input Expander can have its functionality set one of the three options below.

  • Not Wired - This is the default behaviour for an input and left unchanged the input will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
  • Generic Detector - If selected the input will function as a standard security detector input such as a PIR. This input will require the detection device to be wired with EOL resistors to allow the system to identify alarm, seal and tamper states. Once configured, this input needs to be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes or used as a trigger for automation.
  • Generic Switch/Button - If selected the input will function as a standard input for buttons or switches such as a duress point. This input will not require EOL resistors and it is just a normal two state switch or button input. Once configured, this input needs to be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes or used as a trigger for automation.

Name - The input will automatically be named based on the name of the hardware module and the input's behaviour. If entering a name, it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the hardware point and the device that is connected.

Associations
Associated Area

This setting associates a single area to this LCD terminal for the purpose of status indication and convenience.

When navigating to the Control Area menu, this area will be focused by default if multiple areas are configured in the system

When logged off, this terminal will beep with feedback messages to indicate that the area has an active alarm, that entry or exit delay is active or that the area is about to automatically arm. If a user logs on when one of these feedback messages are active, the terminal will automatically take them to the Control Area menu to allow quick control.

In addition, associating an area to the terminal allows that area to be armed and disarmed without needing to navigate to the Control Area menu. When logging on to the terminal, instead of pressing the [OK] button after entering the user PIN, the [ON] or [OFF] button can be pressed instead to arm or disarm the area directly. For example, to disarm the associated area a user could press [PIN Number] [OFF] as a shortcut, minimising the key presses required.
Filter Controllable Areas

This setting determines which areas will be displayed in the Control Area menu of the LCD terminal.

If there are areas assigned to the list, the Control Area menu will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of areas.

This feature can be useful in systems with many areas, for example, by filtering to only the nearby areas for a given LCD terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an area, they will only see the most relevant areas for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every area in the system.

Note: This setting also affects the Associated Area setting. If there are any areas included in this list, the Associated Area must also be included in the list.
Associated Door

This setting associates a door to the LCD Terminal. When a user logs into this LCD Terminal and presses the OK button (e.g [PIN Number], [OK], [OK]) they will be granted access to the associated door, if they have the appropriate user permissions.

Once associated with a door, the Side of Door option below must be set before this LCD terminal can be used for access control.

This LCD terminal will also beep if the door is Held Open (reed or tongue switch is held open when the door is locked for a duration that exceeds the door's Held (DOTL) Time ).
Side of Door

Determines the location of the LCD Terminal in relation to the Associated Door. This is required if you intend to use this terminal for PIN access, or the [OK] button as a logged off access button. This also allows a user's location to be updated when used for PIN access with an area on the other side of the door.

Note, to allow PIN access, ensure the door is configured to allow PIN access from the side of the door that the terminal is located on
Logged Off OK Behaviour

Allows the terminal's [OK] button to be used as a Request Access button for the Associated Door when the terminal is logged off. This option is useful in situations where terminals may already be in proximity to a door, and an exit button / enter button is required. This feature avoids the need to install a separate access button.
Access Only Keypad

If enabled, the LCD terminal will be restricted to access control functions only, such as PIN access for entry/exit or logged off [OK] button for REN/REX.

This option prevents all users from logging into the LCD Terminal to perform any other tasks, for example, make programming changes, arm areas or control outputs.

Note: This option should only be enabled if the LCD terminal is used solely for access control or there is potentially a security issue due to the LCD terminal being located in a public place.
Provide Alert Feedback

If enabled, the LCD Terminal will be able to alert users about the presence of System Messages and System Warnings. This allows Inception to attract the users attention when it has new information to display, or as a reminder that there are still issues to resolve.

Alert feedback will be provided via the LCD Terminal's beeper and LEDs. If Inception needs to alert users, the internal beeper of the LCD Terminal will sound and any LEDs that are not showing an items state (configured in the LED State Display section) will flash.

Alert feedback will only be allowed if the system is disarmed. If there is an area associated with the terminal (implying the terminals location), then alert feedback will be allowed if that area is disarmed. Otherwise, all areas in the system must be disarmed.

Two different types of alert feedback can occur. When a new Warning or Message is created, the terminal will beep continuously with the LEDs flashing fast. Once a user has viewed the Messages and Warnings (i.e by viewing the System Event menu of the LCD terminal, or by viewing the [System > System Overview] web page), the terminal will beep for 1 second every two minutes to remind the user that there are still warnings or messages to process. Note that a user can only view the System Event menu of the LCD terminal if their LCD Terminal Profile gives them permission.

In addition, if a Message or Warning will persist for some time, alert feedback for any currently present events can be muted by pressing OFF in the System Event menu of the LCD Terminal (and ON to unmute), or via the Mute/Unmute Alert Feedback option on the [System Overview] page. If a new Message or Warning is generated while others are muted, then standard alert feedback would still occur.

Not all Warnings or Messages trigger alert feedback. In general, Warnings and Messages relating to hardware health issues, reporting path issues and input fault issues trigger alert feedback. Also, temporarily isolated inputs and unauthorized level 3 user logins will trigger alert feedback.
LED State Display
LED
Item to Display
Invert State

These options allow the state of areas, doors or outputs to be mapped to the LCD terminal LEDs.

  • Areas - LED on for area armed, off for disarmed. LED flashing fast for area in alarm, flashing slowly for area entry, exit and arming warning states
  • Doors - LED on for door locked, off for unlocked. LED flashing fast for door forced, flashing slowly for door held (DOTL)
  • Outputs - LED on for output on, off for output off.

Note: The Invert State option is used to reverse the states that turn the LEDs on and off.
Terminal Inputs/Outputs
Enable Tamper Detection

If configured, the Cabinet Tamper input on the terminal will be monitored by the system and will be included in the overall system health monitoring. The system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured. In addition, all areas will go into alarm when a tamper is detected, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.
The table below is used to configure inputs for the Touchscreen Terminal

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the Touchscreen Module. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the Touchscreen.

Behaviour - Each hardware point on the Touchscreen Terminal can have its functionality set one of the three options below.

  • Not Wired - This is the default behaviour for an input and left unchanged the input will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
  • Generic Detector - If selected the input will function as a standard security detector input such as a PIR. This input will require the detection device to be wired with EOL resistors to allow the system to identify alarm, seal and tamper states. Once configured, this input needs to be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes or used as a trigger for automation.
  • Generic Switch/Button - If selected the input will function as a standard input for buttons or switches such as a duress point. This input will not require EOL resistors and it is just a normal two state switch or button input. Once configured, this input needs to be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes or used as a trigger for automation.

Name - The input will automatically be named based on the name of the hardware module and the input's behaviour. If entering a name, it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the hardware point and the device that is connected.
The table below is used to configure outputs for the Touchscreen Terminal

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the Touchscreen Module. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the Touchscreen.

Output Type - Each output hardware point on the Touchscreen can have its functionality set one of the options below.

  • Not Wired - This is the default behaviour for an output and left unchanged the output will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
  • Wired - If selected the output can be controlled by the system or by a user to activate devices such as strobes, sirens and lighting control. The output will appear on configuration or status pages, however for a user to be able to control the output it must be configured as User-Controllable and be assigned to the user's permissions

Name - The output will automatically be named based on the name of the hardware module and the hardware point. If entering a name, it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the hardware point and the device that is connected.
Onboard Outputs
Output
Enable Input 1

If selected, a normally closed detection device or switch / button can be wired to the LCD Terminal. The LCD terminal input does not support end of line resistors and therefore can not identify the tamper state of a detector, it is therefore not recommended to use this input for security purposes. This input is commonly used for automation such as a button that performs an automated action.

The input is wired to the GND and X3/Z01 connector on the rear of the LCD Terminal.
Input 1 Name
The name of this input that will be used throughout the system
Input 1 Type

Determines how this input should behave and how the state information should be interpreted.

  • Switch/Button - If selected, the input will function as a standard input for buttons or switches such as a duress point. This input will not require EOL resistors and it is just a normal two state switch or button input. Once configured, this input needs to be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes or used as a trigger for automation.
  • Detector (EOL Resistors) - If selected, the input will function as a standard security detector input such as a PIR. This input will require the detection device to be wired with EOL resistors to allow the system to identify alarm, seal and tamper states. Once configured, this input needs to be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes or used as a trigger for automation.
Enable Input 2

If selected, a normally closed detection device or switch / button can be wired to the LCD Terminal. The LCD terminal input does not support end of line resistors and therefore can not identify the tamper state of a detector, it is therefore not recommended to use this input for security purposes. This input is commonly used for automation such as a button that performs an automated action.

The input is wired to the GND and X3/Z02 connector on the rear of the LCD Terminal.
Input 2 Name
Input 2 Type
Enable Output 1

If selected, an open collector output can be wired to the LCD Terminal. This output is a general purpose output and can be used for automation and security functionality. The output can switch a maximum of 100mA, if you need to switch a larger current it is recommended to use this output to switch a relay.

The output is wired to the +VE and X1 connector on the rear of the LCD Terminal.
Output 1 Name
The name of this output that will be used throughout the system
Enable Output 2

If selected, an open collector output can be wired to the LCD Terminal. This output is a general purpose output and can be used for automation and security functionality. The output can switch a maximum of 100mA, if you need to switch a larger current it is recommended to use this output to switch a relay.

The output is wired to the +VE and AX2 connector on the rear of the LCD Terminal.

Lift Interfaces
The table below is used to configure the lift interface cards for the 8 Input Expander.

Each floor hardware point on the Lift Interface card has a relay contact to control a lift button, as well as a sense input to detect when the button has been pressed. See the installation manual for the Unibus 16-Floor Lift Interface Expander for more information about wiring these components.

The tables below allow configuring of the floor inputs and outputs for the Lift Interface cards that have been connected. Up to 6 Lift Interface cards can be connected via the unibus interface, allowing up to 96 floor points to be configured.

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the 16-Floor Lift Interface Expander. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the Lift Interface cards.

Floor Wired - Each hardware point can be configured as either Wired or Not Wired. If wired, an input and an output will be automatically created for that hardware point. The floors that aren't are not wired will not have anything generated.

Name - The input and output will automatically be named based on the name of the hardware module and the hardware point. If entering a name, it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the hardware point and the device that is connected. The input that is generated will have - Sense appended to the name and the output will have - Contact appended.

Additional Components
Monitor SIFER/OSDP Tamper

If enabled, SIFER or OSDP readers connected to the module will be monitored by the system for tampers. The SIFER reader, for example, detects a physical tamper using an optical beam to surface it is mounted on.

If a reader tamper occurs, the system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station is alarm reporting if enabled and correctly configured. When reporting the event, the system will include the door's name that the reader is connected to, to help in identifying where the event occurred. In addition, all areas will go into alarm, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.

If configured, the Cabinet Tamper on the module will be monitored by the system and will be included in the overall system health monitoring. The system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured. In addition, all areas will go into alarm when a tamper is detected, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.
Cabinet Tamper Switch Type

If configured, the Cabinet Tamper input on the module will be monitored by the system and will be included in the overall system health monitoring. This option determines the type of Cabinet Tamper switch that is connected to the hardware module.

  • None - No switch is connected.
  • Open Circuit Secure - The tamper switch is open circuit when sealed/closed.
  • Closed Circuit Secure - The tamper switch is closed circuit when sealed/closed.

Note: Inner Range enclosures are supplied with Open Circuit Secure tamper switches.

If configured, the system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured. In addition, all areas will go into alarm when a tamper is detected, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.
Monitor RF Interference

If enabled, the module will be monitored by the system for RF Interference events and this event will be included in the overall health monitoring of the system. The system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured.
Enable Card Cache

Enabling Card Cache will ensure stored user card credentials on the Access Control Module will be valid after the Module goes offline. Disabling the Card Cache will block valid user credentials while the Access Control Module stays offline. By default the Card Cache is enabled.
Internal Siren Wired

If enabled, the Internal Siren driver (S1INT) on the module will be monitored for tampers and will be included in the overall system health monitor. To detect siren tampers, the siren devices need to be wired with a normally closed tamper switch.

Note, if enabled, the system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured. In addition, all areas will go into alarm when a tamper is detected, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.
Internal Siren is DC Driven

If enabled, the Internal Siren driver will operate in DC driven mode, rather than driving the siren to make specific tones. This option allows a piezo screamer to be plugged directly into the siren driver point, rather than wired into an actual relay.
External Siren Wired

If enabled, the External Siren driver (S2EXT) on the module will be monitored for tampers and will be included in the overall system health monitor. To detect siren tampers, the siren devices need to be wired with a normally closed tamper switch.

Note, if enabled, the system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured. In addition, all areas will go into alarm when a tamper is detected, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.
External Siren is DC Driven

If enabled, the External Siren driver will operate in DC driven mode, rather than driving the siren to make specific tones. This option allows a piezo screamer to be plugged directly into the siren driver point, rather than wired into an actual relay.
Power Supply Connected to DC IN

When enabled, the supplied Inception 24V power supply will be monitored for AC Fail in the overall system health monitor. Only enable this option if the Inception controller is being powered via the DC IN supply.

The Inception Controller will report AC Fail events to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured if this option is enabled and the DC power supply fails.
Backup Battery Connected to BATT

When enabled, the connected battery will be monitored for Low Battery and Missing Battery in the overall system health monitor. This option also allows the configuration of battery testing options below. The Inception Controller will report these hardware events to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured, should the battery run low or a battery test is failed.

This option should only be used if there is an actual battery connected to the BATT connector of the controller. If the controller is being powered from an external 12-14V power supply into the BATT connector, this option should not be enabled.
Integriti SMART Power Supply Connected

If enabled, the external power supply's AC Fail, LAN Fuse Blown and Power Supply Failure states will be included in the overall system health monitor.

Note: Only enable this option if this module is connected to an Inner Range 3/8Amp Smart Power Supply via the 10pin PSU patch lead.

The Inception Controller will report these hardware events to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured, should any alarm conditions occur with this power supply.
Battery connected to Integriti SMART Power Supply

If enabled, the battery connected to the external power supply will be monitored for the Low Battery condition by the overall system health monitor. Regular battery tests can also be performed to verify the battery's condition.

Note: Only enable this option if this module is connected to an Inner Range 3/8Amp Smart Power Supply via the 10pin PSU patch lead and there is a battery attached to that power supply.

The Inception Controller will report these hardware events to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured, should the battery run low or a battery test is failed.
Enable Battery Testing

If enabled, along with the power supply and the battery options above, the module will be included in the systems scheduled battery testing.

Battery testing is required to ensure that in the event of AC failure, each module can be powered from its attached battery. The regular testing of the battery ensures that if a battery fails over time it is detected and can be replaced.

This option, along with Battery Test Duration below, only allow this module to be added to the list of modules to test. When the tests occur and how often testing is performed is configured on the [Configuration > General > System] page. When the scheduled time occurs, all modules configured for battery testing will be added to a queue. Modules are tested one at a time so that if there is an AC fail mid test, only part of the system is vulnerable, not all of it. Testing will only start if the module is online, AC power is present, the battery state is not low or missing and no short is detected.

A battery test passes if no issues are detected for the duration of the test. If a battery issue is detected during a battery test, the monitoring station will be notified about it and a warning will appear on the web interface. A module with a failed battery test state must pass a battery test to clear it. This can happen with the next scheduled test, or a test can be manually run on the [State / Control > Hardware Test] page.

Battery tests can abort for a number of reasons, a user can cancel the test, AC power was lost on the power supply, a wire was shorted, comms to the module was lost or the module needed to sound sirens. An aborted test is neither a pass or fail and nothing is reported to the monitoring station.
Battery Test Duration

Determines the duration of time that the module's battery will be tested.

The duration of the battery test should be determined by the average current consumption of the module and the capacity of the connected battery, as well as the minimum amount of time that you want the system to remain powered in the event of AC power failure.

The output will appear on configuration or status pages, however for a user to be able to control the output it must be configured as User-Controllable and be assigned to the user's permissions

  • Wired - If selected the output can be controlled by the system or by a user to activate devices such as strobes, sirens and lighting control. The output will appear on configuration or status pages, however for a user to be able to control the output it must be configured as User-Controllable and be assigned to the user's permissions.

  • Wired - If selected the output can be controlled by the system or by a user to activate devices such as strobes, sirens and lighting control. Unibus Expander 3 - 8
Readers
Reader Type

Sets the type of reader that is connected to the module. The other options on this page are based on the Reader Type chosen.

Note, for Wiegand readers, the reader configured in each slot (e.g Reader 1 and Reader 2 ) should match the readers on the Wiegand reader ports on the module. SIFER/OSDP readers, on the other hand, can be in any order.
Reader Serial

This is the unique identification number that is hard coded to the SIFER/IR Reader. In most cases the serial number will automatically appear once a SIFER/IR reader is detected and there is a free reader location. Otherwise, the serial can be found on the back of the reader itself, the SIFER packaging or on [Review Events] page when connecting the SIFER Reader.
OSDP Address

This is the OSDP Address that the Reader currently uses to communicate with the host module. It must be unique between the readers connected to the same module. In most cases the OSDP Address will automatically appear once an OSDP reader is detected and there is a free reader location. Otherwise, it can be manually entered if known, or on [Review Events] page when connecting the OSDP Reader.
3 Badge Action

Readers can be configured to arm one or more areas when a user presents their card at the reader three times in a row in short succession. A user can only arm areas that they have permission to arm and the arm may fail if the area contains unsealed inputs. If this occurs, the user should use a more advanced interface to try and arm, such as the LCD Terminal or the web interface.

  • None - No action will occur.
  • Arm Area on the Other Side of the Door - The area configured on the other side of the door from this reader will arm. Exit delay will not be triggered.
  • Arm All Areas - This will arm all areas currently configured in the system, that the user has permission to arm. Any areas that a user does not have permission to arm will not be armed. Exit delay will not be triggered.
  • Arm Area on the Same Side of the Door - The area configured on the same side of the door as this reader will arm. This will also begin Exit delay for the area.
Status LED to Follow

The SIFER/OSDP reader can provide visual and audible feedback for the state of an area or door. This option determines which item, if any, to provide feedback for. In addition, an LED on the reader can be used to show that item's state, instead of a static colour. For SIFER readers, this is the small LED. For OSDP readers, this is the only LED. If configured, the colour of the status LED is determined by the item it is following and can't be manually set, which overrides the status LED field below (Small LED Colour for SIFER readers or LED Colour for OSDP readers).

  • None - The status LED will default to the color selected in the field below (Small LED Colour for SIFER readers or LED Colour for OSDP readers). When a user badges a card, the status LED will turn green if access is granted and red if access is denied.
  • Door State - The status LED will turn red if the door that this reader is linked to is locked and turn green when unlocked.
  • Area State, Same Side - The SIFER will provide feedback on the state on the area located on the same side of the door as the reader. The reader will beep with different tones to indicate an alarm in the area, entry delay, exit delay and auto-arm warning. The small LED will turn red if the area is armed and will turn green when the area is disarmed.
  • Area State, Other Side - Same behaviour as the Area State, Same Side option above, but for the area located on the other side of the door to the reader.

Note, both LED brightness and feedback volume for SIFER readers can be adjusted on the [Configuration > Access Control > Readers] page
Big LED Colour

Sets the colour of the big LED on the SIFER reader.

Note, the LED brightness can be adjusted on the [Configuration > Access Control > Readers] page
Small (Status) LED Colour

Sets the colour of the small LED on the SIFER reader. This only applies if the above Status LED to Follow option is set to None.

Note, the LED brightness can be adjusted on the [Configuration > Access Control > Readers] page
LED Colour
Sets the colour of the LED on the reader. This only applies if the above Status LED to Follow option is set to None.
Keypad Area To Control
Keypad Type

Wiegand keypad readers allow the entering of a user's PIN number in order to request access through a door. Different keypads send through PIN numbers in different ways, this option specifies how to process the key presses. If the type of keypad connected is not listed, try each of the available options anyway, as it may match a known type.

A user can only request access through a door if that door's Door Access Mode is set to a mode that uses PIN.
RF Inputs
The table below is used to configure inputs for the RF Expander Module

The IR-Paradox RF Module supports up to 32 RF input detector devices.

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the 32 RF inputs that can be used on the IR-Paradox RF Module.

Device Type - Each RF Input hardware point on the IR-Paradox RF Module can have its functionality set to one of the four options below.

  • No Device - This is the default behaviour for an RF input and left unchanged the RF input will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
  • Motion Detector - If selected the input will function as a motion detector, this is used if the device you are connecting is a Wireless PIR.
  • Door / Glassbreak / Asset Protector - If selected the input will function as a door/window contact, glassbreak detector or asset protector. This is used if the device you are connecting is a wireless door reed, a wireless glassbreak detector or a wireless asset protector
  • Smoke Detector - If selected the input will function as a smoke detector, this is used if the device you are connecting is a wireless smoke detector.

Serial Number - This is the six-digit serial number of the RF input, often located on the back or inside of the detector. If there is no number on the detector, you can identify the serial number by opening up the detector, waiting for its transmit LED to stop flashing and then pressing and releasing the tamper detection switch. You can then navigate to the [State / Control > Review Events] page and find the most recent RF Device Learn Button Pressed event, this event will contain the serial number. See the detector's instructions for more information about learn mode.

Name - The input will automatically be named based on the name of the RF module and the hardware point. If entering a name, it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the hardware point and the device that is connected.

The Inovonics RF Module supports up to 32 RF input detector devices.

Hardware Point - The hardware point refers to the 32 RF inputs that can be used on the Inovonics RF Module.

Device Type - Each RF Input hardware point on the IR-Paradox RF Module can have its functionality set to one of the four options below.

  • No Device - This is the default behaviour for an RF input and left unchanged the RF input will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
  • Detector - If selected the input will function as a standard alarm detector. Device alarm and tampering will be monitored. This can be used with most transmitter devices like motion, smoke and glassbreak detectors. In addition, RF remotes could also be used if they are mounted as push-button inputs for wireless button inputs or user duress.
  • Detector Alarm 2 - Certain Inovonics detectors are capable of reporting multiple alarms, this option is used to access the secondary alarm of the detector. This would be used, for example, with dual-input detectors that allow a separate device to be wired into the transmitter.
  • Repeater - If selected, the input will represent the state of a repeater. The input will not go into the alarm state, however it will enter the tamper state if the repeater is tampered with, as well as monitoring for failed polls and low battery information.

Serial Number - This is the eight-digit serial number of the RF input, often located on the back or inside of the detector. If there is no number on the detector, you can identify the serial number by opening up the detector and pressing the learn button. You can then navigate to the [State / Control > Review Events] page and find the most recent RF Device Learn Button Pressed event, this event will contain the serial number. See the detector's instructions for more information about learn mode.

Name - The input will automatically be named based on the name of the RF module and the hardware point. If entering a name, it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the hardware point and the device that is connected.
