Emergency Buttons
The following options will enable calculated inputs that can be manually triggered from the touchscreen using an Emergency button.

Enabling any of the emergency buttons will cause the Emergency button to be shown on the Touchscreen's main screen, which will open a menu with the enabled options when tapped. It will also automatically create a calculated input for this touchscreen associated with that emergency button. The calculated input will be pulsed when the button on the touchscreen's Emergency menu is activated by a user.

To set up monitoring and reporting of these events, the calculated inputs should be added to an area using the Edit Areas or Area Input Map pages, using an appropriate input process group.
Enable Fire Button
If configured, a button will be enabled on the touchscreen interface to allow users to trigger a fire alert. When activated, it will trigger a calculated input in Inception.
Enable Ambulance Button
If configured, a button will be enabled on the touchscreen interface to allow users to trigger an ambulance alert. When activated, it will trigger a calculated input in Inception.
Enable Police Button
If configured, a button will be enabled on the touchscreen interface to allow users to trigger a police alert. When activated, it will trigger a calculated input in Inception.
Entity Filters

This setting determines which areas will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.

If there are areas assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of areas.

This feature can be useful in systems with many areas, for example, by filtering to only the nearby areas for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an area, they will only see the most relevant areas for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every area in the system.
Filter Controllable Doors

This setting determines which doors will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.

If there are doors assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of doors.

This feature can be useful in systems with many doors, for example, by filtering to only the nearby doors for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an doors, they will only see the most relevant doors for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every door in the system.
Filter Controllable Inputs

This setting determines which inputs will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.

If there are inputs assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of inputs.

This feature can be useful in systems with many inputs, for example, by filtering to only the nearby inputs for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an input, they will only see the most relevant inputs for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every input in the system.
Filter Controllable Outputs

This setting determines which outputs will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.

If there are outputs assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of outputs.

This feature can be useful in systems with many outputs, for example, by filtering to only the nearby outputs for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an output, they will only see the most relevant outputs for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every output in the system.
Filter Hardware Modules

This setting determines which Hardware Modules will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.

If there are modules assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of modules.

This feature can be useful in systems with many modules, for example, by filtering to only the nearby modules for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to view a module state, they will only see the most relevant modules for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every module in the system.

Entry/Exit Path Inputs
Select the Inputs that are located on the Entry/Exit Path.

The entry/exit path is required when an LCD Terminal that allows area control is located within an armed area. This lets the terminal be protected, however a user still needs to be able to access the terminal to arm and disarm the area. A primary entry point can be designated which if triggered first, allows a user to move through the armed area to the LCD Terminal, forming the entry/exit path. The path is created using the security inputs, where the inputs that protect the path through the area are programmed differently. This lets a user enter through a specific door and walk a specific path to the terminal to disarm, or arm the area from the terminal and walk along the path to exit the area. Any deviation from this path causes an alarm to be triggered.

Assign the inputs that should be triggered first at the primary entry point as Primary Inputs. These start the entry timer and put the area in entry mode, allowing the user to walk along the path without triggering an alarm. This is generally going to be a door reed or a PIR near an entry door.

All other inputs which cover the entry/exit path should be assigned as Handover Inputs. While the exit delay or entry delay timer is active, Primary and Handover Inputs are ignored, allowing a user to walk along the path. If a Handover input is triggered before a Primary Input on the entry path (that is, the user bypassed the primary entry point), then the area entry time will be ignored and the area will go into alarm instantly.

In entry mode, an alarm will be triggered if the area is not disarmed before the entry time expires or if the user leaves the path and triggers an instant alarm input. Similarly, in exit mode, an alarm will be triggered if the user leaves the path and triggers an instant alarm input.
Primary (Entry Delay Start)

Generally the first input that will be triggered when a user enters the building via the primary entry point. These inputs will start the entry delay, allowing the user to disarm the system from a keypad or reader without generating an area alarm. Without a primary input being triggered first, the handover inputs would be treated the same as intruder inputs causing the area to go into alarm.

To assign an input to this area as a primary input, click the Add Items button, this will open a window that contains all the available inputs. Once an input is added, it will automatically be assigned the Primary (Entry Delay Start) input type (process group).
Handover (Entry/Exit Path)

These are secondary inputs that are triggered as the user moves through the entry/exit path to disarm the area via a keypad or reader. If a primary input is triggered first and starts the entry delay, the activations on these inputs will be ignored for the duration of the entry delay. If the area entry delay is not triggered, the handover inputs will immediately generate an area alarm. These inputs should include most of the inputs that cover the entry/exit path, so if an intruder bypasses the primary entry point the alarm will sound as soon as possible.

To assign an input to this area as a handover input, click the Add Items button, this will open a window that contains all the available inputs. Once an input is added, it will automatically be assigned the Handover (Entry/Exit Path) input type (process group).
Intruder and Other Inputs
Select all the other inputs that are monitored by this area that aren't part of the Entry/Exit Path.

When the area is armed, the intruder inputs when triggered will cause the area to go into alarm instantly, the intruder inputs are generally located outside of the primary entry/exit path.

If the area monitors additional inputs that aren't used for intruder detection, such as a panic button or automation switch, you will need to add the input to the Other Inputs section. Once assigned, you will be able to select a process group to determine how the area will respond to the input state changes, this will determine the activation of sirens and the reporting of alarms. These different inputs may trigger alarms or sirens even if the area is disarmed.

  • Automation - No sirens or strobes are enabled, no alarms are reported. Used for automation purposes, an automated action can have an area trigger condition of Detecting Active Inputs which this along with all other input types would trigger.
  • Door Forced - When the input is triggered, alarm siren tones are played on internal sirens only, strobes are enabled and a door forced event is sent to the monitoring station. The alarm response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. Used on doors that should only be opened when unlocked, such as after an access request or REX button. Best used with the Forced calculated input that is dynamically created when a door has a reed or tongue input assigned to it. Note that doors with free egress (e.g an external door that can be opened from the inside without a REX button or unlocking the door) should not be monitored with this input type, as the door will be forced during this action.
  • Door Held Open - When the input is triggered, no sirens or strobes are enabled, but a door held open event is sent to the monitoring station. The event will be sent regardless of the area's arm state. Used as an escalation option when important doors are held open too long. A warning phase can occur with local buzzers to attract attention so that the door can be closed, but if no one is nearby and that phase passes then an event can be sent, allowing the monitoring station to contact someone to ensure the door is closed. Best used with the Held Open calculated input that is dynamically created when a door has a reed or tongue input assigned to it.
  • Duress - When the input is triggered, no sirens or strobes are enabled, but a duress event is sent to the monitoring station. The duress event will be sent regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
  • Emergency - When the input is triggered, evacuation siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and an emergency event is sent to the monitoring station. The emergency response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
  • Environmental - When the input is triggered, no sirens or strobes will be activated. However, a non-burglary event will be sent to the monitoring station. The non-burglary response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. Used to represent non-intruder alarms like temperature alarms or water sensors to notify the monitoring station or other users of the event. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
  • Hold Up - Reports with the same alarm event type as a Duress input type, however alarm sirens are played and the area goes into alarm. Hold up events are processed regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state
  • Medical - When the input is triggered, alarm siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and a medical event is sent to the monitoring station. The medical response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
  • Smoke - When the input is triggered, fire evacuation siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and a smoke event is sent to the monitoring station. The smoke response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
Intruder Inputs

These are the inputs that will immediately generate an area alarm. The intruder inputs should not include inputs that are located on the primary entry/exit path of the building. These inputs will generally be located on doors and in sections of the building that are accessed once the area is disarmed.

To assign an input to this area as an intruder input, click the Add Items button, this will open a window that contains all the available inputs. Once an input is added, it will automatically be assigned the Instant Alarm input type (process group).
Other Inputs

Other inputs and devices that are not related to intruder detection but nevertheless require processing or monitoring by an area. To assign an input to this area as an Other input, click the Add Items button, this will open a window that contains all the available inputs. Once an input is selected you will be able to manually select an input type, it is recommended that you do not select primary, handover or instant alarm process groups.

The following input types (process groups) can be selected:

  • Automation - No sirens or strobes are enabled, no alarms are reported. Used for automation purposes, an automated action can have an area trigger condition of Detecting Active Inputs which this along with all other input types would trigger.
  • Door Forced - When the input is triggered, alarm siren tones are played on internal sirens only, strobes are enabled and a door forced event is sent to the monitoring station. The alarm response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. Used on doors that should only be opened when unlocked, such as after an access request or REX button. Best used with the Forced calculated input that is dynamically created when a door has a reed or tongue input assigned to it. Note that doors with free egress (e.g an external door that can be opened from the inside without a REX button or unlocking the door) should not be monitored with this input type, as the door will be forced during this action.
  • Door Held Open - When the input is triggered, no sirens or strobes are enabled, but a door held open event is sent to the monitoring station. The event will be sent regardless of the area's arm state. Used as an escalation option when important doors are held open too long. A warning phase can occur with local buzzers to attract attention so that the door can be closed, but if no one is nearby and that phase passes then an event can be sent, allowing the monitoring station to contact someone to ensure the door is closed. Best used with the Held Open calculated input that is dynamically created when a door has a reed or tongue input assigned to it.
  • Duress - When the input is triggered, no sirens or strobes are enabled, but a duress event is sent to the monitoring station. The duress event will be sent regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
  • Emergency - When the input is triggered, evacuation siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and an emergency event is sent to the monitoring station. The emergency response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
  • Environmental - When the input is triggered, no sirens or strobes will be activated. However, a non-burglary event will be sent to the monitoring station. The non-burglary response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. Used to represent non-intruder alarms like temperature alarms or water sensors to notify the monitoring station or other users of the event. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
  • Handover (Entry/Exit Path) - When the input is triggered with the area armed and no entry or exit delay active, alarm sirens are played, strobes are enabled and an intruder event is sent to the monitoring station. If disarmed, the alarm responses will not occur. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state
  • Hold Up - Reports with the same alarm event type as a Duress input type, however alarm sirens are played and the area goes into alarm. Hold up events are processed regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state
  • Instant Alarm - When the input is triggered with the area armed, alarm sirens are played, strobes are enabled and an intruder event is sent to the monitoring station. If disarmed, the alarm responses will not occur. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state
  • Medical - When the input is triggered, alarm siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and a medical event is sent to the monitoring station. The medical response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
  • Primary (Entry Delay Start) - When the input is triggered, if the area is armed and no alarms are active, Entry Delay mode will begin. If alarms are active or the area is not disarmed before entry delay ends, then alarm siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and an intruder event is sent to the monitoring station. If disarmed, the alarm responses will not occur. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state.
  • Smoke - When the input is triggered, fire evacuation siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and a smoke event is sent to the monitoring station. The smoke response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.
  • Storage Unit - When the input is triggered, alarm sirens are played and the area goes into alarm, regardless of the area's arm state. However, intruder events are only reported if the area is armed. Used to represent a Storage Unit alarm, where sirens can sound during the day for unauthorised unit access while staff are present, while sirens and event reporting can occur after hours when the area is armed. If tampered with, area sirens occur and a tamper event will be sent to the monitoring station. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state
Strobes
Test that the strobe is correctly programmed for the selected area by clicking the Turn On and Turn Off buttons.

If the strobe does not work as expected, manually turn on the output that it is wired to the strobe on the [State / Control > Hardware Test] page. If the strobe works when the output is controlled check the area's programming on the [Configuration > Areas > Edit Areas] page.
Turn On
Turn Off
Internal Sirens
Test that the internal siren and any internal siren outputs are correctly programmed for the selected area by clicking the Turn On and Turn Off buttons.

If the internal siren does not work as expected, navigate to the [Configuration > Areas > Edit Areas] page and confirm that the area has been assigned an Internal Siren Device or Internal Siren Outputs. If siren outputs are being used, they can be manually controlled via the [State / Control > Hardware Test] page to ensure the wiring is correct.

This is useful to manually test the sirens without needing to trigger an alarm. This option also bypasses Service Mode.
External Sirens
Test that the external siren and any external siren outputs are correctly programmed for the selected area by clicking the Turn On and Turn Off buttons.

If the external siren does not work as expected, navigate to the [Configuration > Areas > Edit Areas] page and confirm that the area has been assigned an External Siren Device or External Siren Outputs. If siren outputs are being used, they can be manually controlled via the [State / Control > Hardware Test] page to ensure the wiring is correct.

This is useful to manually test the sirens without needing to trigger an alarm. This option also bypasses Service Mode.

A Walk test can be started and stopped for the selected area using the buttons below. Once started, the inputs will automatically move from the Inputs To Test list to the Passed list as they are tested.

While a walk test is active, input state processing is disabled, allowing emergency response inputs like user duress buttons, emergency buttons and smoke detectors to be tested without sounding alarms.

However, input processing is only disabled for the area(s) running the walk test, so if inputs are included in multiple areas then the alarm response could still occur. In this case, it may be worth testing multiple areas at once.

A test will automatically complete when all inputs in that area have been successfully tested. If there are some inputs that can't be tested, then the test can be cancelled early with the Cancel Walk Test button below. Progress of a running test can be seen from multiple devices simultaneously if required.
Start Walk Test
Cancel Walk Test
Activate each of the inputs in the list below to test them. Once tested, they will automatically move to the 'Passed' list.

Final Steps

This step is used to configure network settings and the method of connection to the Inception web interface. The default network settings are Ethernet using DHCP, if required you can set up the Ethernet connection with a static IP address.

If you have purchased the Inception Wi-Fi Adapter, you can connect the controller to the site's wireless network or connect directly using a Wireless Hotspot. It is recommended to leave this option enabled and set to Wireless Hotspot if you are not leaving a Wi-Fi adapter on site, but will use one in future to aid in servicing this Inception Controller.

You can enable Skytunnel if a remote connection from a device outside of the local network to the Inception Controller is required. Skytunnel provides a hassle free connection that does not need port forwarding. Skytunnel is a cloud-based service and requires internet access for the Inception Controller and the connecting device.

Configure Network Settings

Configure the Ethernet network settings for DHCP or static IP.

Configure the Wi-Fi settings if installing a Wi-Fi Adapter on site.

This step is for the setup of the date and time settings, once completed the controller time displayed should match the time at the panel’s location. It is important to set the date and time accurately to ensure that time periods and calendar events occur at the correct time. Time periods can be used to trigger a number of scheduled actions in the system, for example, Door Free Access, Area Auto-Arming and Automated Actions.

It is recommended to configure the controller to synchronize with an NTP server this will ensure that the date and time are always correct. The NTP server can be located on the local network or the internet.

You will need to set the date and time manually if the network the controller is connected to does not have access to an NTP server. Note: If you manually set the date and time you will also need to manually correct Daylight Savings time changes for your location.
Current Date and Time
NTP Server
Time Zone
Configure Date and Time Settings
Configure the NTP server and time zone settings, or set date and time manually if no NTP server is available.

This step is used to configure settings such as the user PIN length, End of Line and Duress User. You can select a minimum and a maximum length for user PINs. The default PIN length is two to four digits, however, the site may require longer PINs as they are more secure.

If there is a chance that users onsite will be put in a duress situation, you can enable the Duress User feature. This involves creating a PIN that all users can enter into a terminal or keypad while under duress to silently notify the monitoring station about the duress situation. The Duress User must also be assigned permissions, these should allow the user under duress to be able to access the items they usually access without raising suspicion, but not allow too much access as everyone should know this PIN.

If you are replacing an existing security system, you can avoid changing the End of Line resistors for every detection device by selecting a matching End of Line config. If you are installing a new system, it is recommended to use the default 2k2 + 2k2 or 2k2 + 6k8 as these match the resistors shipped with Inner Range products.

If in Europe, a Compliance Mode can be chosen in order to meet the local security standards. EN50131 and BS8243 are the two standards that can currently be chosen, which allows the system to show additional options or features specific to those standards.

Configure the user PIN length, Duress User and End of Line settings.

If in Europe, select the Compliance Mode to use if required (e.g EN50131 for European installations).
Configure System Settings and Compliance

This step is used to create and name all the areas that will monitor inputs in the system. This involves just creating the areas to allow them to be used in the next Hardware Enrolment step, the actual configuration of areas is done later on in the Configure Intruder Detection step of the [Commissioning Checklist].

You can program the system with a single area or multiple areas. The benefit of a single area is the entire system is easy to arm and disarm. However, creating multiple areas allows you to divide the site and limit user access to certain areas, not every user will require access to the area like the Server Room.

If you are using the Inception System for access control, it is recommended to have separate areas on the inside and outside of doors to take advantage of features like 3-Badge Arming or User Location.

You should create additional areas if you want to group inputs that have a common purpose such as all the smoke detectors within the building.
Programmed Areas
Create Areas
Create and name all the areas for the site.
Setup Site Areas

This step is used to configure the Inception Controller and LAN modules. This will include the programming of inputs, outputs, doors and readers based on the type of module, as well as configuring module behaviour and which module health components have been utilized (for example, cabinet tampers, siren tampers, smart powersupplies, external battery).

Inputs, outputs and readers are only created in the system once the module is configured. When configuring a module, you will be stepped through multiple sections of programming. These steps will depend on the type of module being configured. This step is complete when all the modules appear in the Configured Modules list.

If you want to configure a module that you have not yet purchased or attached to the system, you can create it by clicking the Create New button.
Unconfigured Hardware Modules
Configured Hardware Modules
Run Hardware Wizard
Configure the Inception Controller and connected LAN modules.
Test Hardware Components (Inputs, Outputs, Battery)
Test the inputs and outputs of the Inception Controller and all other LAN modules. Ensure all inputs and outputs are wired and named correctly
Perform Hardware Enrolment

If the system is used for intruder detection, this three-part step maps the inputs to the areas for monitoring, configures the areas, then tests the strobes and sirens to ensure their configuration is correct.

The first part is to map the inputs to the areas you created. The [Area Input Mapping] page lets you add inputs to one of four categories. Three of these categories relate to intruder detection and will automatically assign the correct Input Type (Process Group). The fourth category is Other Inputs, these are devices that are not related to intruder detection but still require processing or monitoring by the area.

The second part is the area configuration where you can set the area's Entry/Exit Delays, Siren, Strobe and Auto-Arm functionality. The options set for each area will vary based on the area's purpose.

The third part allows the testing of area strobes and sirens, which will confirm that the physical sirens and strobes are wired correctly and that the area options that determine which sirens sound are configured correctly.
Yes, configure intruder detection
No, intruder detection is not needed
Is Intruder Detection being used on this site?
Link Inputs to Areas
Assign inputs to the appropriate areas
Edit Area Settings
Configure the options for all areas.
Test Area Strobes and Sirens
Confirm that the sirens and strobes are activated correctly for each area.
You have said that no intrusion detection is necessary for this site. If this isn't the case, reset the step and answer Yes
Configure Intruder Detection

If the system is used for lift integration, this involves the creation of Lift Floors and Lift Cars, the two main components in the integration.

Essentially, low-level lift integration involves wiring of outputs in the Inception system to lift buttons in a lift car, allowing the individual buttons to be enabled or disabled by the Inception system. Then, a user is able to present their card at a reader located in the lift car and based on their permissions, certain floor selection buttons can be enabled. In addition, the lift buttons can also be wired into Inputs in the Inception system, allowing Inception to know which button a user pressed.

The first step is to create Lift Floors. Lift Floors represent a destination that a button in a lift car would take the user to, and multiple lift cars may have a button that can take the user to the same lift floor. In some cases there may be multiple lift floors created for the same physical floor in a building, for example if the lift car has two doors and there are different buttons for 7A and 7B. In this case, different floors can be created with different permissions for each side of the car. Alternatively, if a particular lift car needs its access to be restricted to only certain users, for example a service elevator, there could be normal floors created for the other cars, along with another set of service floors for the service Lift Car, with only maintenance workers having permission to those floors.

The second step involves the creation of the Lift Cars. These represent a physical lift car or button panel that the user interacts with. These contain one or more readers for the user to present their card or PIN at, as well as a number of lift buttons to be enabled or disabled by the Inception system. Each lift button requires both a Lift Floor for the button destination and an output to enable the button on the panel. Optionally, an input can also be specified that would indicate when the button has been pressed.
Yes, configure lift integration
No, lift integration is not needed
Is Lift Integration being used on this site?
Create Lift Floors
Create the Lift Floors for the site. Floors can be shared between multiple Lift Cars.
Create Lift Cars
Create the Lift Cars for the site.
You have said that no lift integration is necessary for this site. If this isn't the case, reset the step and answer Yes
Configure Lift Integration

This step is used to create and configure time periods that are used in later steps such as Door Free Access, Area Auto-Arming, triggering automation and to qualify User Permissions.

Time periods are based on weekly recurring times, where the time periods are generally valid at the same times each week. Exceptions to these rules can be added to allow temporary changes to be made to the schedule without significant programming changes.

The default time period is called Working Hours; this time period is commonly used to trigger events to occur during a typical working day, such as internal doors unlocking at the start of the work day or turning on an output that controls lighting. This is only an example and can be edited to match the hours of operation for the site or removed.

Consider what items in the system should be scheduled and how they might be grouped together. You may need to create multiple time periods to handle these cases, such as when door free access should be allowed, when areas should automatically arm in the evenings, any scheduled automation required and when user permissions should be allowed or limited.
Programmed Time Periods
Configure Time Periods
Create and configure all the time periods for the site.

This step is used to configure Door Free Access, Area Auto-Arm and Lift Floor Free Access schedules. Door Free Access uses time periods and area states to unlock the door and provides a user access to a door without presenting a credential. This can be used to unlock internal doors when the inside area of the door is disarmed during working hours. The door will relock when a user arms the inside area or at the end of the day when the time period goes invalid.

You can use the Area Auto-Arm feature with a time period to ensure that the area is armed by a certain time each night, preventing the area from accidentally being left disarmed overnight. The Area Auto-Arm will not disarm the area when the time period goes valid.

The Lift Floor Free Access option uses time periods and area states to automatically set the floor into free access, allowing a user to access the floor in a lift car without presenting a credential. This can be used to allow access to a floor during certain times of the day, but only if the area on that floor is disarmed. The floor will re-secure when a user arms the area on that floor or when the time period goes invalid.

This step is used to configure Door Free Access and Area Auto-Arm schedules. Door Free Access uses time periods and area states to unlock the door and provides a user access to a door without presenting a credential. This can be used to unlock internal doors when the inside area of the door is disarmed during working hours. The door will relock when a user arms the inside area or at the end of the day when the time period goes invalid.

You can use the Area Auto-Arm feature with a time period to ensure that the area is armed by a certain time each night, preventing the area from accidentally being left disarmed overnight. The Area Auto-Arm will not disarm the area when the time period goes valid.
Go to the Scheduling Page

Assign time periods to doors that require Door Free Access and mark the area to follow if required.

Assign time periods to areas that require Area Auto-Arm functionality.

Assign time periods to lift floors that require Lift Floor Free Access and mark whether the area state should be followed.

Assign time periods to doors that require Door Free Access and mark the area to follow if required.

Assign time periods to areas that require Area Auto-Arm functionality.
Set Door, Area and Lift Schedules
Set Door and Area Schedules

This step is used to select which outputs can be controlled by users from the web interface or an LCD Terminal.

Outputs that are configured as User Controllable allows them to be manually controlled by a user, however they still need to be given permission to control that output. Once assigned to the user's permission they will be able to control the output from the Inception web interface or the LCD Terminal, as well as see it's state. You can also configure an output to allow Logged Off Control, this allows any person to control the output from any LCD Terminal on-site without needing to enter a security PIN. Note: the LCD Terminal must be configured for Logged Off Output Control.

It is recommended that only outputs that should be controlled manually by users should be marked as User Controllable. These are generally automation related, for example lighting, water sprinklers or airconditioning control.
Number of Controllable Outputs
Go to the User Controllable Outputs Page
Select the outputs that users can control.
Choose Outputs that are User Controllable

This step is used to create the permission groups that will be assigned to one or more users to determine the doors, areas and outputs the users will be able to access or control.

Permission groups are useful as they can include a common set of permissions that can then be assigned to multiple users. Once assigned, the permissions for all of those users can be updated by changing the permission group directly, making permission maintenance easy. When creating permission groups, it is recommended to first identify what is common between a number of users. This will generally be role based such as IT staff, admin staff etc. However on some sites it may simply be different level of access such as basic access or management access. The permission groups would then contain all the permissions required for a worker to access the doors and control the areas and outputs for them to do their job.

It is recommended that you clearly name the permission groups based on the user’s role to allow someone else to assign them to users without needing to look at their contents.
Programmed Roles/Permission Groups
Configure Permission Groups
Create permission groups containing the doors, areas and outputs for different user roles.
Configure Permission Group and Roles

If the system is used for access control, this step is used to configure the types of credentials used on site.

Credential templates contain information such as how the credentials presented at readers should be interpreted (e.g. as cards, fingerprints, facial scan data) and the card format being used (if applicable). Credentials are then assigned to users by specifying a credential template and the credential number. Credential templates can be configured with a specific card format and include a site code to match cards, or can be configured to use raw card information (direct entry), which uses the full card data as the credential number.

If you intend to use site coded cards, you will need to specify the card format and the site code. To identify the card format and the site code of the cards, open the [State/Control > Review Events] page and badge a card at a reader.

If multiple credential types are used on-site, multiple templates can be created, or raw (direct entry) can be used.
Yes, credentials are being used on site
No, credentials are not being used on site
Is this system being used for access control with cards or other credentials?
Configure Access Card Template
Create a credential template that matches the format and site code of the credentials being used onsite.
You have said that no access control is used on this site. If this isn't the case, reset the step and answer Yes.
Configure Access Credential Template

This step is used to review and configure web page profiles. Web page profiles determine which pages a user is able to access via the Inception web interface, as well as what they can do on those pages. The web page profile includes permissions for Item Editing, Item Monitoring, Task Execution and Dashboard Content. The Dashboard Content sets which areas, doors, outputs and shortcut groups appear on the user's Dashboard.

Some profiles have been created by default to cover the common types of users. If the default profiles are not suitable for the site, you will need to create new ones or modify them.

It is recommended that when creating or modifying profiles, the profile should only be able to view and interact with the pages that are actually required, with the rest being hidden away. This results in an uncluttered and easy to use interface with unimportant items for that user hidden away.
Programmed Web Profiles
Review and Configure Web Profiles

Review the existing profiles and modify as needed or create new ones if required.

Assign any areas, doors or outputs that should appear on the dashboard pages for users

This step is used to create and configure users. A user is any person that interacts with the Inception System by either badging a card, entering a pin number or logging into the web interface.

Credentials are what identify a user and must be unique between all users. Credentials include the PIN number used to access keypad readers or log into an LCD terminal, cards for access control and RF remotes for remote control. You can also provide the user a username and password to grant them access to the Inception Web Interface; the user will also require a Web Page Profile, which determines which pages they can view and how they can interact with those pages.

You will need to assign the user individual permissions or the permission groups you created in a previous step, this will determine what areas, doors and outputs a user can access or control on the system and when they are allowed to control them.

Users can also be bulk imported via CSV if a number of users need to be added
Number of Users
Create and Configure Users
Create a new user for each person that will interact with the Inception System and assign them the appropriate credentials and permissions.

This step is used to configure the reporting of alarms and events to a monitoring station. The inception controller supports reporting via Skytunnel or with an external T4000 device.

  • Skytunnel Reporting - This uses the Inception controller's network connection with internet access to communicate with the SkyTunnel server. Once the SkyTunnel server receives the event, it is delivered to a monitoring station. This feature works out of the box and requires no other specialized hardware.
  • T4000 Reporting - The T4000 is an Inner Range device that connects to the Inception Controller via USB. It provides multiple redundant reporting paths using multiple 3G networks in addition to Ethernet internet reporting. With all paths polled frequently, this ensures reliable alarm monitoring.

Note: These options only enable the reporting paths. Either the Inception controller for SkyTunnel, or the T4000 must also be enrolled onto a monitoring station's Multipath IP system. Contact your monitoring station in order to set up this account.

Multiple paths can be used by marking them as either the primary path or backup path.
Yes, alarms will be monitored
No, this system will not be monitored
Is this system being monitored by a monitoring station?
Configure Alarm Monitoring

Successfully send a test report.

This step is used to configure the system to be able to send notification messages to users when events in the system occur. Notifiers can be configured, for example, to send notifications whenever an alarm occurs in a particular area or access to a particular door is granted or denied. Notifications can also be sent when repeated failed login attempts are made on the web interface or LCD Terminal, or if programming changes are made to particular entities. Maintenance events, such as power and battery issues of modules or RF devices can also be communicated.

Inception supports sending messages to users via email. To use the email notification path, Inception must be configured with an email server to use and the recipient users must have an email address assigned to them.

Multiple notifiers can be created to notify different users about different events at different times. Create as many notifiers as required by the system to send desired events to users.
Yes, user notifications are required
No, this system will not use user notifications
Is this system sending event notifications to users via email?
Configure Email Server Settings

Configure the email server settings to allow Inception to send emails.

Successfully send a test email (using the Send Test Email button in the toolbox) to ensure the path works.

This step is used to configure any automation logic that is beyond the basic intruder and access control system. Automated actions provide you with the ability to solve automation scenarios for the site. These would most often be simple automation cases such as turning on the lights when the area is disarmed, but have the flexibility to handle very complicated scenarios.

Identify any automated responses that you want the system to perform and when you would want them to happen. They could be things like turning on lighting or air conditioning when the area is disarmed or when there is activity detected. They could also tie in to the security of the site, such as arming some areas when another area arms. Triggers can involve the state of multiple things in the system including time periods, so automation can also be set up to occur only during certain times. Depending on the automation required, multiple actions may need to be configured for the one task.

See the page description on the [Automated Actions] page for more information about configuring the actions.
Configure Automated Actions

It is extremely important to change the Web Interface password and PIN if you have enabled the Enable Web Access over Skytunnel option as the panel will be accessible via the internet. In this case it is also recommended that the username is also changed.

This step disables service mode that is enabled by default when first configuring the system. Service mode allows you to install and setup the Inception controller without generating sirens and alarm reporting. Once service mode has been disabled the system's sirens and alarm reporting will be enabled.

You can enable service mode at a later date to perform maintenance without generating sirens and alarm reporting.

Note: When disabling service mode, any current alarms will be processed, potentially sounding sirens. Similarly, if any modules are reporting tamper health issues (e.g cabinet tamper or siren tampers), it may also cause the sirens to sound.

As database backups are encrypted, this is intended to be a summary of all programming in the system at the end of commissioning and can aid in future maintenance or used as reference material, or verifying any programming issues at a future time.

Filter Events
Review events can be filtered to make it easier to find particular events.

There are 3 ways to filter review events these are time, message or category based. These 3 filters can be used individually or combined for advanced searching.

  • Time-Based Filters - The From filter is used to specify a date and time in the past that will filter out any events that occurred before that time. This can be used to display the last 7 days of review. The To filter is used to specify a date and time that will filter out any events that occurred after that time. Specifying a To filter in the past will prevent live review from being added. These options are commonly used together to display only the review events that occurred at a specific time range.
  • Text-Based Filter - The search field will filter the currently displayed review events based on any text entered. This text will be searched across the Message, Who, What and Where columns. The text does not have to appear in a particular order, with each word separated by a space being searched for in a row. The results will display all the Review events that contain every entered word in the search field between the fields. This is commonly used to find specific events such as a user called Mary controlling the Foyer area. If the text Mary Foyer was entered into the search field then only the review events that contain both words would be displayed.
  • Category Filter - Each review event in the Inception system falls into a category which can be filtered upon to narrow down the types of message shown. These categories are Security, Access, Hardware, System and Audit. Multiple categories can also be included in the filter at once. To set whether a category is included in the filter, click or tap the category to toggle whether it is enabled or not. By default the Review Events page only displays the Security, Access and System message categories.

The above filters can be combined together to offer a flexible way to filter events. When one or more filters are used, any events that don't match the filter will be hidden and information about the number of shown events vs the total number of events will be updated, for example, Showing 241 Events (filtered from 744 total events).

Note: The Download PDF and Download CSV options are affected by the filtering of events.

Choose an OSDP reader connected to the currently selected module that will have its address changed. Only OSDP readers that are currently online and connected will be listed.

SIFER readers will not be included in this list, and do not require usage of this feature due to the automatic management of addresses when connected to any Inner Range system.

Enter a new numerical address for the OSDP Reader to be set to. After confirming, a command will be sent to the selected reader which will set the address to the one specified.

After the command has been sent, the reader will be disconnected and then promptly reconnected to the module under the new address as long as it is free. Configure SSL Certificate
This dialog allows you to upload a custom SSL certificate for HTTPS access, or generate a self-signed SSL certificate.

Inception's web interface can be configured to allow access via HTTPS for a greater level of security (compared to standard HTTP) when configuring the system. HTTPS access can be enabled either in combination with HTTP access, or with HTTP disabled for the highest level of security and encryption. When HTTP access is disabled, plain HTTP requests made to the Inception controller will be redirected to use the HTTPS protocol instead.

HTTPS access for Inception is enabled by default with an auto-generated SSL certificate which is self-signed by the Inception controller, you can choose to keep the default SSL certificate or upload your own custom SSL certificate and private key. NOTE: Choosing to use a self-signed certificate will provide encryption on web traffic, but may cause your browser to display a warning that \"the connection is not private\". The self-signed certificate can be manually imported into your browser's trusted certificate store, but for more permanent situations, it is recommended that a trusted custom SSL certificate is installed by your network administrator.

If the new configuration cannot be applied (e.g. due to an invalid certificate or password), the existing HTTPS configuration and certificate will be left unmodified.

WARNING: It is highly recommended that you perform a database backup before modifying the HTTP/S configuration in case it affects your access to the web interface.
Current SSL Certificate Info

Upload SSL Certificate
This dialog allows you to upload a custom SSL certificate for HTTPS access.

When uploading a custom SSL certificate, you should have a PEM-formatted certificate file (.crt or .pem) and the accompanying PEM-formatted key file (.key). If your certificate is in a different file format (e.g. .p12/.pfx), you will first need to convert it to PEM format using an external utility such as OpenSSL.
Upload Custom SSL Certificate File (.crt, .pem)

Doors can be scheduled to automatically unlock and lock based on a time period or whether an area on one side of the door is armed or not.

To configure when a door will automatically lock and unlock, select a time period from the Free Access Time Period drop-down or an area from the Area State to Follow drop-down.

Free Access Time Period - When the time period goes valid (the current time is inside of the specified times) the door will unlock and when the time period goes invalid (the current time is outside of the specified times) the door will lock.

This is commonly used when an internal door needs to be in free access during working hours.

Time periods are programmed and edited on the [Configuration > Scheduling > Time Periods] page.

Area State to Follow - When the area is armed the door will automatically lock, however, disarming the area will not unlock the door.

This is commonly used when you need to ensure a door is locked when the area on the inside of the door is armed.

Both options can be combined to result in a door that will only unlock when both the time period is valid and the area is disarmed. The door will relock when the time period goes invalid or the area re-arms. This allows a door to be normally unlocked during the day, but only after someone with authorization to disarm the area is there.
Door Free Access
To configure when an area will automatically arm, select a time period from the Auto-Arm Time Period drop-down.

The area will automatically arm when the selected time period becomes active (the current time is inside of the specified times). This feature is commonly used to automatically arm the area late at night to ensure that the area is armed over night in case a user forgets to arm it.

By default, the area will not automatically disarm when the time period goes invalid, however this can be changed with the Auto-Disarm when Schedule Inactive option on the [Configuration > Areas > Edit Area] page.

Time periods are programmed and edited on the [Configuration > Scheduling > Time Periods] page.
Area Auto-Arm
Lift Floors can be scheduled to be set to free access or secured based on a time period or whether the area on that floor is armed or not.

To configure when a lift floor will automatically be set to free access, select a time period from the Free Access Time Period drop-down and set whether the floor should also follow the area on that floors state.

Free Access Time Period - When the time period goes valid (the current time is inside of the specified times) the floor will be set to free access and when the time period goes invalid (the current time is outside of the specified times) the floor will secure. A floor in free access allows any user to select that floor in the lift car, while a secured floor requires the user to present a credential to allow them to select that floor.

This is commonly used when a common floor needs unrestricted access at certain times of the day.

Time periods are programmed and edited on the [Configuration > Scheduling > Time Periods] page.

Follow Area State - When the area is armed the floor will automatically secure, however, disarming the area will not set the floor to free access.

This is commonly used when you need to ensure a floor is secured when the area on that floor is armed, restricting access to only those who can disarm the area.

Both options can be combined to result in a floor that will only be in free access when both the time period is valid and the area is disarmed. The floor will re-secure when the time period goes invalid or the area re-arms. This allows a floor to be normally free to all users during the day, but only after someone with authorization to disarm the area is there.
Lift Floor Free Access

Clicking Manage Languages will open the Manage Languages menu, where you can upload, delete and apply Language Packs. Clicking the Download Translation Template button will download a translation template file to use with the Inception Translation Tool.

The Inception web interface is capable of being displayed in different languages through the usage of Inception Language Pack (.irlanguage) files. These files can either be supplied by Inner Range or the installer, or manually created and edited with the Inception Translation Tool, a separate stand-alone application. To create a language pack, the first step is to download a copy of the Inception System's language pack template file to import into the Inception Translation Tool. This includes all of the translatable text in the Inception system for the current firmware version.

You can download a language pack template file by clicking the Download Translation Template button from the Manage Languages dialog. You can import this template file into the Inception Translation Tool to generate a new translation project. Or, it can be merged with an older translation project to translate only the text that has been added in the new firmware since a translation was last done.

After you've finished the translation process using the Inception Translation Tool, you can export a Language Pack file (.irlanguage) and then upload it to the Inception controller from a PC. To upload a language pack, click the Manage Languages button, then click the Upload button and choose the file on disk that you want to upload. Upon being uploaded successfully, the language pack should appear in the list of available languages in the Manage Languages dialog box. From here, you can select the desired language from the list and click Apply in order to change the current language of the Inception controller (this action requires rebooting the device).

If uploading the language pack fails, it could mean that the language pack file data was exported incorrectly, or that the file has been corrupted at some point during transfer. Try re-exporting the language pack from the Inception Translation Tool if possible and re-uploading it.

The LAN Encryption Key Changeover mode is intended to help migrate Ethernet LAN modules (e.g. Ethernet Bridge) from an old encryption key to the current key configured in Inception. All Ethernet LAN modules in your system must be configured with the same encryption key as the controller in order for them to be able to communicate.

The LAN Key Changeover mode puts the Inception controller in a state where it will temporarily accept Ethernet LAN messages encrypted with an alternate specified key (or the default key), for a period of 2 minutes. When Inception receives a connection request from a module using the alternate key, it will automatically migrate that module to the current key.

This mode can be useful in the event where you have changed the Inception's LAN encryption key but a module was offline and did not receive a key change message, or if you have connected a new, unconfigured Ethernet LAN module and need to change its key so it can be enrolled.
Key Type
Old Encryption Key

If the fault cannot be fixed right away, the \"Mute\" button on this dialog can stop the periodic beeping until the warning/message can be attended to. The calendar preview below is used to check that the time period is programmed correctly.

The calendar preview can be used to view any date in the future and will display when the time period is valid on that day, taking into account any calendar exceptions or date & time exceptions. This is useful to confirm whether exceptions planned for the future have been applied correctly.

When changes are made to the time period or the exceptions, it is recommended to update the calendar preview first by clicking the Refresh Preview. The calendar can be displayed in days, weeks or months by pressing the appropriate button. Depending on the selected display mode you can advance the calendar forward and backwards by pressing the arrow keys.

The green colour indicates times that the time period will be active and white indicates the invalid times. Certain times may be red, which indicates times that are invalid due to exceptions.

Export CSV
Opens the export CSV file menu to export user information into a .csv file. The menu will have user fields that can be selected to filter down the exported user data.
Import CSV
Opens the import CSV file menu to import a .csv file that will update existing users and create new ones based on the names of the users specified in the .csv file.
Import User Photos

Opens a menu that allows for the import of user photos in bulk through uploading a ZIP file with JPEG image files. The image files should be named to the corresponding user the photo will be assigned to.
Importing Users

Import user photos in bulk by uploading a ZIP file containing multiple JPEG image files inside. Image filenames are matched up with users based on the user's Name, so it is recommended that user names are unique.

The uploaded ZIP file should contain only JPEG (.jpg/.jpeg) image files whose filenames match the names of users in the Inception system. Files which are not JPEG files or files whose names do not match a user in the system will be ignored. All images should be placed in the main directory of the ZIP folder, not in subfolders. The uploaded zip file cannot exceed 100MB, larger batches of images may have to be split into multiple zip files.

The maximum supported image size is 480 x 640px (3:4 aspect ratio), images will be scaled down if they exceed these dimensions, so it is recommended that user photos are uploaded with a similar resolution and aspect ratio for optimal quality.
Upload Zip File
Import Photos from Zip

Import users in bulk by uploading a CSV file. Users can either be added or updated by matching on the user Name in the CSV. As updating of users is keyed on Name, it is recommended that user names are unique.

Download the CSV import spreadsheet here. Add the user information to the spreadsheet, save it as a CSV file and upload it in this menu.

The CSV file must contain a header line that specifies the User data columns that are to be imported, and the column titles must match the User properties as they appear on the Manage User page. The \"Name\" property is a mandatory field and must be included as one of the header line columns, all other fields are optional.

An example of a valid header column format:

  • Name*
  • Permissions
  • Security PIN
  • Credentials
  • Notes
  • Email Address
  • User Expiry Time
  • User Start Time

* mandatory field

Each data row must have at least the Name column filled in.

Entries for the \"Permissions\" field are only allowed to be the name of an existing Permission Group in the system (case-insensitive); user permissions for individual items cannot be assigned through CSV import.

Entries for the \"Credentials\" and \"Remote Fobs\" fields should contain only the Credential/Fob number; the Credential Template and Fob Template are specified below in the import options.

Note that Mobile Credentials are unable to be imported via CSV.

Entries for checkbox fields (e.g. \"Use Extended Unlock Times\") will only accept the words \"true\" or \"false\" (case-insensitive).

Fields consisting of lists of items (e.g. Credentials, Remote Fob, Permissions) can only accept a single item at a time due to the limitations of the CSV format. For newly imported Users, the single item will become the only item in the list field. For existing users, the single item will be appended to the existing list of items (if it is not already present). In the case where you wish to import a User(s) with 2 credentials assigned to them, for example: you can CSV import the User(s) initially with a single credential number specified in the Credentials field, and then perform another CSV import with the same Users' names but with the 2nd credential number in the Credentials field, and the existing User will gain the 2nd credential number in their Credentials list.

Fields that refer to other items in the system (e.g. LCD Terminal Profile, Permission Groups, Web Page Profile) will be linked up based on the item's Name field (case-insensitive). If no item is found with the specified item name, the import will fail.

Fields with drop-down lists of options (e.g. When to Cancel User) will only accept values that match the text options in the drop-down list (e.g. \"First Card Or PIN\"). The values are case-insensitive.

The recommended format for date/time field data (e.g. User Expiry Time) is \"YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS\". This avoids any ambiguity with the date format. Alternate date formats may be accepted, but through testing should be done first to confirm and will depend on the selected system language.

The \"Encrypted Security PIN\" and \"Encrypted Password\" fields will only accept encrypted data strings that have been exported via the Export User CSV dialog or from the Inception REST API. This option is provided to allow cloning User data from one Inception system to another without having to restore an entire database backup, as plain-text PINs and passwords cannot be exported from Inception for security reasons. To input plain-text PINs or passwords in the CSV import, use the \"Security PIN\" or \"Password\" fields instead. Avoid using the encrypted and unencrypted variants of the same input field when entering user data.

When using CSV Import to update users, if no value is specified for a field then no change is made. This means that, for example; to update an email address for a user, no permission group, PIN, credential, or notes need to be specified.

Existing user data fields can also be erased by inputting \

\" for the specific field. This is different to not specifying a value for a field, which will leave the field unchanged for the user. This only works for fields that are allowed to be empty (e.g. Notes, Credentials); fields that must reference another item (Web Page Profile, LCD Terminal Profile) cannot be set to blank and will cause the import to fail.

If the import fails the process will abort and no users will be created. However if the import was being used to update users, any updates that occurred before the failure will be persisted in the system.

Some causes of import failures are:

  • One or more of the users don't have a name.
  • The specified item name (Permission Group, Web Page Profile, etc.) name could not be found in the system.
  • A PIN provided already matches an existing User or there are duplicate PINs in the import file.
  • A credential provided already matches an existing User or there are duplicate credentials in the import file.
  • A supplied user data field was not in a valid format (Email Address, User Expiry Time, etc.)
Upload CSV File

Export Users to CSV
Choose the fields that you want to be included in the User CSV Export data.

Due to security reasons, the \"Security PIN\" and \"Password\" fields will not export any sensitive data to the CSV file if they are included (the fields will be left blank), however they can still be included in the CSV output in order to use the exported CSV as a template to be filled in for importing users.

If you wish to export user PINs or passwords from a system and import them into another Inception system, select the \"Encrypted Security PIN\" and/or the \"Encrypted Password\" fields instead. These fields will contain an encrypted version of the data which is only readable by Inception systems, and can be imported into other systems with the sensitive data intact.

User 2FA Configuration
Inception supports Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) with a smartphone or tablet using the Google Authenticator app or another compatible Time-based One-time Password (TOTP) authenticator app.
Using your authenticator app, scan the QR code using your smartphone's camera, or manually enter the authentication key to add a new 2FA account.
Once you have created an account, enter the 6-digit code from the authenticator app and click the Enrol button to confirm your 2FA enrolment.
Enter Code
Get Backup Code
Search/Filter User Data
Search user data by entering text in the Search All Fields text box.

The Search All Fields text box will search all visible user data fields and filter the table results to display only the users whose details contain a partial or exact match for the search text. Note that hidden fields are not included in the search results, you will need to make them visible by using the Column visibility button if you want to search the contents of those fields. The Search All Fields text box can also be be used in combination with the individual column search filters in order to filter the results to a finer degree.
